The H-bridge contains **four switches (S1, S2, S3, S4)** arranged in an "H" shape with the motor on the crossbar. - **S1 + S4 ON** → Current flows left-to-right → Motor spins **CW** - **S2 + S3 ON** → Current flows right-to-left → Motor spins **CCW** - **S1 + S2 ON (or S3 + S4 ON)** → Creates a short circuit → **DANGEROUS — avoided in L293D internal logic** ## 2.3 Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) — Speed Control Principle A DC motor's speed is **approximately proportional to the average applied voltage**. Since Arduino cannot output a variable analog voltage (only 0 V or 5 V), it fakes an analog voltage by rapidly switching the pin HIGH and LOW. **The Effective (Average) Voltage Formula:** $$V_{avg} = V_{CC} \times D$$ Where: - $V_{CC}$ = Supply voltage to the motor (e.g., 5 V or 12 V) - $D$ = Duty Cycle (0 to 1) **Duty Cycle in Terms of Arduino PWM Value:** $$D = \frac{\text{analogWrite value}}{255}$$ **Effective Voltage to Motor:** $$V_{motor} = V_{CC} \times \frac{\text{analogWrite value}}{255}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** PWM-based motor speed control is the foundation of **drone propeller control (ESC modules)**, **CNC spindle speed regulation**, **electric vehicle traction motors**, and **robotic vacuum cleaners (e.g., Roomba wheel motors)**. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Engineering Use | |---|---------------------|------------|-------|-----------------| | 1 | Duty Cycle (D) | $D = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{total}}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | PWM speed control | | 2 | Duty Cycle from Arduino | $D = \dfrac{\text{analogWrite}}{255}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | Firmware calculation | | 3 | Average Motor Voltage | $V_{avg} = V_{CC} \cdot D$ | Volts (V) | Speed estimation | | 4 | Motor Speed Relation | $N \approx k \cdot V_{avg}$ | RPM | Open-loop speed model | | 5 | PWM Frequency (Pin 5,6) | $f_{PWM} \approx 980 \text{ Hz}$ | Hertz (Hz) | Default Timer0 | | 6 | PWM Frequency (Pin 9,10) | $f_{PWM} \approx 490 \text{ Hz}$ | Hertz (Hz) | Default Timer1 | | 7 | PWM Resolution | $2^{8} = 256 \text{ steps}$ | Bits | 8-bit DAC equivalent | | 8 | L293D Output Current Limit | $I_{out} \leq 600 \text{ mA (continuous)}$ | Amperes (A) | Current budget | | 9 | L293D Motor Voltage | $V_{M} \leq 36 \text{ V}$ | Volts (V) | Driver rating | | 10 | Back-EMF Constant | $V_{back} = k_e \cdot \omega$ | Volts (V) | Motor dynamics | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Questions frequently ask *"Why is PWM used instead of a digital potentiometer or DAC for speed control?"* The answer is **energy efficiency** — PWM transistors operate in cut-off (0 W) or saturation (low V·I = low loss), yielding >90 % efficiency, whereas a linear series resistor dissipates $I^2R$ as heat. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Pin Mapping & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 L293D Pin-out — Complete Reference (16-Pin DIP IC) | Pin # | Pin Name | Function | Arduino Connection | |:-----:|----------|----------|--------------------| | 1 | **1,2EN (Enable)** | Enables Motor 1 (PWM input for speed) | Arduino PWM pin (e.g., D9) | | 2 | **1A (Input 1)** | Direction bit A | Arduino digital pin (e.g., D8) | | 3 | **1Y (Output 1)** | Motor 1 terminal A | **Motor terminal T1** | | 4 | **GND (Logic)** | Logic ground | Arduino GND | | 5 | **GND (Motor)** | Motor power ground | Battery/Supply GND | | 6 | **2Y (Output 2)** | Motor 1 terminal B | **Motor terminal T2** | | 7 | **2A (Input 2)** | Direction bit B | Arduino digital pin (e.g., D7) | | 8 | **VCC2 (Motor Supply)** | Motor power input (4.5–36 V) | External battery +ve | | 9 | **3,4EN (Enable 2)** | Enables Motor 2 | Optional (unused for 1 motor) | | 10 | **3A** | Input for Motor 2 | Optional | | 11 | **3Y** | Output for Motor 2 | Optional | | 12 | **GND** | Ground | Common ground | | 13 | **4Y** | Output for Motor 2 | Optional | | 14 | **4A** | Input for Motor 2 | Optional | | 15 | **GND** | Ground | Common ground | | 16 | **VCC1 (Logic Supply)** | 5 V logic power | **Arduino 5 V pin** | > [!NOTE] > **Crucial Wiring Note:** Pins **4, 5, 12, 15** are *all* grounds and *must* be tied together to form a **single common ground** shared with the Arduino. Failure to common-ground will result in **erratic L293D switching** due to floating logic references. ## 3.2 Sample Arduino Sketch — Step-by-Step Logic Walkthrough ```cpp // ===== DC Motor Speed and Direction Control with L293D ===== // Pin Definitions const int pwmPin = 9; // L293D Enable 1,2 (PWM speed control) const int dirPin1 = 8; // L293D Input 1A (direction bit A) const int dirPin2 = 7; // L293D Input 2A (direction bit B) void setup() { pinMode(pwmPin, OUTPUT); pinMode(dirPin1, OUTPUT); pinMode(dirPin2, OUTPUT); // Initial state: motor stopped digitalWrite(dirPin1, LOW); digitalWrite(dirPin2, LOW); analogWrite(pwmPin, 0); } void loop() { // --- Phase 1: Forward Acceleration --- digitalWrite(dirPin1, HIGH); // Set direction = Forward (CW) digitalWrite(dirPin2, LOW); for (int speed = 0; speed <= 255; speed += 5) { analogWrite(pwmPin, speed); // Ramp up speed 0% → 100% delay(50); } // --- Phase 2: Forward Full Speed Hold --- analogWrite(pwmPin, 255); delay(2000); // --- Phase 3: Forward Deceleration --- for (int speed = 255; speed >= 0; speed -= 5) { analogWrite(pwmPin, speed); delay(50); } // --- Phase 4: Stop (Coast) --- digitalWrite(dirPin1, LOW); digitalWrite(dirPin2, LOW); analogWrite(pwmPin, 0); delay(1000); // --- Phase 5: Reverse Acceleration --- digitalWrite(dirPin1, LOW); // Swap direction bits digitalWrite(dirPin2, HIGH); // Direction = Reverse (CCW) for (int speed = 0; speed <= 200; speed += 5) { analogWrite(pwmPin, speed); delay(50); } analogWrite(pwmPin, 200); delay(2000); // --- Phase 6: Stop (Brake) --- digitalWrite(dirPin1, HIGH); // Both inputs HIGH = brake digitalWrite(dirPin2, HIGH); analogWrite(pwmPin, 0); delay(2000); } ``` ### Line-by-Line Conceptual Breakdown | Code Line | Engineering Interpretation | |-----------|----------------------------| | `pinMode(pwmPin, OUTPUT)` | Configures Arduino pin 9 as output to drive L293D Enable pin | | `digitalWrite(dirPin1, HIGH)` | Sets logic bit A = 1 → activates upper-left H-bridge switch | | `digitalWrite(dirPin2, LOW)` | Sets logic bit B = 0 → activates lower-right H-bridge switch | | `analogWrite(pwmPin, 128)` | Outputs ~50 % duty cycle → effective $V_{motor} = V_{CC} \times 0.5$ | | `for (int speed = 0; speed <= 255; speed += 5)` | Soft-start ramp; reduces inrush current spike by ~80 % | | `delay(50)` | 50 ms step delay → full ramp takes 2.55 s | ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — PWM to Voltage Conversion **Problem:** A 12 V DC motor is connected to an L293D driven by Arduino. The sketch calls `analogWrite(pwmPin, 180)`. Calculate: 1. The duty cycle (D) 2. The average voltage applied to the motor 3. The approximate motor speed if $k = 80 \text{ RPM/V}$ ### Step 1 — Compute Duty Cycle $$ D = \frac{\text{analogWrite value}}{255} = \frac{180}{255} $$ $$ D = 0.7059 \approx 70.59\% $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Average Motor Voltage $$ V_{avg} = V_{CC} \cdot D = 12 \text{ V} \times 0.7059 $$ $$ V_{avg} = 8.4706 \text{ V} \approx 8.47 \text{ V} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute Motor Speed $$ N = k \cdot V_{avg} = 80 \text{ RPM/V} \times 8.4706 \text{ V} $$ $$ N = 677.65 \text{ RPM} $$ ### Final Answer Summary | Quantity | Value | Units | |----------|-------|-------| | Duty Cycle (D) | 70.59 | % | | Average Voltage | 8.47 | V | | Motor Speed (N) | 677.65 | RPM | ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Coast vs. Brake | Stop Method | dirPin1 | dirPin2 | Behavior | Use Case | |-------------|:-------:|:-------:|----------|----------| | **Coast (Free Run)** | LOW | LOW | Motor terminals open; rotor freewheels down to 0 RPM | Conveyor belt, gentle stop | | **Brake (Active)** | HIGH | HIGH | Both motor terminals connected to VCC; rotor stops quickly | Robotic arm joint, safety stop | | **PWM Zero** | X | X | Enable pin LOW; L293D disables output entirely | Hard power cutoff | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — System-Level Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Laptop USB
Programming] --> B[Arduino Uno
ATmega328P MCU] B -- digitalWrite 5V/0V --> C[L293D H-Bridge
Logic Side] C -- PWM Enable Signal --> D[Enable 1,2 Pin1] C -- Direction Bits A,B --> E[Input 1A Pin2
Input 2A Pin7] F[External Battery
6V-12V] --> G[L293D Motor Side
VCC2 Pin8] D --> H[Power H-Bridge
4 Darlington Switches] E --> H G --> H H -- Output Pin3 and Pin6 --> I[DC Motor
M Terminals] I --> J[Mechanical Load
Wheel or Gearbox] B -.-> K[Common GND Bus] F -.-> K C -.-> K ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Firmware Logic Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD S([Start]) --> P1[Setup: Configure pwmPin, dirPin1, dirPin2 as OUTPUT] P1 --> P2[Initialize: All pins LOW, motor OFF] P2 --> Q{User Command} Q -- FORWARD --> R1[Set dirPin1 HIGH, dirPin2 LOW] R1 --> R2[analogWrite pwmPin, 0 to 255 gradually] R2 --> R3[Motor spins CW at variable speed] Q -- REVERSE --> T1[Set dirPin1 LOW, dirPin2 HIGH] T1 --> T2[analogWrite pwmPin, 0 to 255 gradually] T2 --> T3[Motor spins CCW at variable speed] Q -- STOP_COAST --> U1[Set dirPin1 LOW, dirPin2 LOW] U1 --> U2[analogWrite pwmPin, 0] U2 --> U3[Motor freewheels to stop] Q -- STOP_BRAKE --> V1[Set dirPin1 HIGH, dirPin2 HIGH] V1 --> V2[analogWrite pwmPin, 0] V2 --> V3[Motor brakes actively to stop] R3 --> Q T3 --> Q U3 --> Q V3 --> Q ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — H-Bridge Switching States ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> MotorOff MotorOff --> ForwardCW: dirPin1 HIGH, dirPin2 LOW, PWM ON MotorOff --> ReverseCCW: dirPin1 LOW, dirPin2 HIGH, PWM ON ForwardCW --> CoastStop: dirPin1 LOW, dirPin2 LOW ForwardCW --> BrakeStop: dirPin1 HIGH, dirPin2 HIGH ReverseCCW --> CoastStop: dirPin1 LOW, dirPin2 LOW ReverseCCW --> BrakeStop: dirPin1 HIGH, dirPin2 HIGH CoastStop --> MotorOff: Enable LOW BrakeStop --> MotorOff: Enable LOW ForwardCW --> ReverseCCW: Swap direction bits ReverseCCW --> ForwardCW: Swap direction bits ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Explain the need for a motor driver IC like L293D when interfacing a DC motor with Arduino. Why cannot a DC motor be connected directly to an Arduino GPIO pin?* **Model Answer:** A standard Arduino Uno GPIO pin can source or sink a **maximum of 40 mA** at **5 V logic level** (absolute maximum per pin as per ATmega328P datasheet, with 200 mA total for the entire MCU package). In contrast, a typical small DC motor (e.g., a 6 V BO motor) requires **150–500 mA** and operates at **3–12 V**. If connected directly: 1. The GPIO pin will be overloaded → **excessive current draw** → **thermal shutdown** or **permanent silicon damage** to the ATmega328P. 2. The Arduino's **5 V regulator** (fed by USB or barrel jack) cannot supply the required current and may **brown-out reset** the board. 3. **Back-EMF** generated when the motor is turned off will produce a **voltage spike** of 60–100 V, destroying the microcontroller. The **L293D** solves all three problems by: - Acting as a **current buffer** (logic side draws < 5 mA; motor side delivers up to 600 mA per channel). - Accepting a **separate motor power supply** (4.5–36 V on VCC2) independent of the 5 V logic rail. - Providing **internal flyback diodes** that safely dissipate back-EMF. *(3 marks — definition 1, problem statement 1, L293D solution 1)* ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *What is Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)? How is it used in Arduino to control the speed of a DC motor?* **Model Answer:** **PWM Definition:** Pulse Width Modulation is a technique for generating a pseudo-analog output using a digital signal by rapidly toggling the output between HIGH and LOW. The **duty cycle (D)** — defined as the fraction of time the signal remains HIGH within one period — determines the **average voltage** delivered to the load. **Arduino Implementation:** - Arduino Uno's ATmega328P has **6 hardware PWM-capable pins**: **3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11** (marked with ~ on the board). - The function `analogWrite(pin, value)` outputs a PWM wave where `value` ranges from **0 (0 % duty cycle)** to **255 (100 % duty cycle)**. - The PWM frequency is approximately **490 Hz on pins 9, 10** and **980 Hz on pins 5, 6**. **Application to DC Motor Speed Control:** - A DC motor's rotational speed is approximately **proportional to the average applied voltage**. - By setting `analogWrite(9, 128)`, we output a 50 % duty cycle, applying an effective voltage of $V_{CC}/2$ to the motor via the L293D Enable pin. - Varying `value` from 0 to 255 produces a **smooth, continuous speed range** — equivalent to a digital-to-analog converter. *(3 marks — PWM definition 1, Arduino function 1, motor speed link 1)* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **DO NOT** confuse the **Enable pin** (PWM, controls speed) with the **Input pins** (direction). This is the **#1 mistake** in board exams and costs 2–3 marks. > - **Always** mention the **common ground** connection in circuit diagrams. Skipping it loses **1 mark** as it is a mandatory exam requirement. > - When writing Arduino code, **declarations and pinModes** must appear in `setup()`. Writing `pinMode()` inside `loop()` wastes marks. > - **L293D ≠ L298N.** L293D is a dual H-bridge IC for low-power (≤ 600 mA); L298N is a higher-current (≤ 2 A) variant. Wrong IC choice in design questions = −2 marks. ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the interfacing of a DC motor with Arduino Uno using the L293D motor driver IC. Clearly label all pin connections and explain the role of each pin. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a complete Arduino program to control the speed of a DC motor in both forward and reverse directions. The motor should accelerate from 0 to maximum speed in 3 seconds, run at full speed for 2 seconds, and then decelerate to stop. Use PWM for speed control. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks) **Circuit Description:** The L293D has **16 pins** in a DIP package. The key connections are: | L293D Pin | Connection | Purpose | |-----------|------------|---------| | Pin 1 (1,2EN) | Arduino D9 (PWM ~) | Enable + Speed control via PWM | | Pin 2 (1A) | Arduino D8 | Direction Input A | | Pin 3 (1Y) | Motor Terminal 1 | Motor output A | | Pin 6 (2Y) | Motor Terminal 2 | Motor output B | | Pin 7 (2A) | Arduino D7 | Direction Input B | | Pin 8 (VCC2) | External +9 V battery | Motor power supply | | Pin 16 (VCC1) | Arduino 5 V | Logic supply | | Pins 4, 5, 12, 15 | Common GND | Common ground rail | **Operating Logic:** - When **1A = HIGH, 2A = LOW**: current flows from Pin 3 → motor → Pin 6 → ground → **Motor rotates CW (Forward)**. - When **1A = LOW, 2A = HIGH**: current flows from Pin 6 → motor → Pin 3 → **Motor rotates CCW (Reverse)**. - The **PWM signal on Pin 1 (Enable)** modulates the average voltage, controlling speed. *Marking distribution: Pin table — 3 marks, Operating logic — 2 marks, Circuit diagram (or description) — 2 marks.* #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks) **Arduino Sketch:** ```cpp const int enablePin = 9; // PWM pin → L293D Pin 1 const int input1 = 8; // Direction A → L293D Pin 2 const int input2 = 7; // Direction B → L293D Pin 7 void setup() { pinMode(enablePin, OUTPUT); pinMode(input1, OUTPUT); pinMode(input2, OUTPUT); // Stop motor initially digitalWrite(input1, LOW); digitalWrite(input2, LOW); analogWrite(enablePin, 0); } void loop() { // ===== FORWARD Direction ===== digitalWrite(input1, HIGH); // CW digitalWrite(input2, LOW); // Accelerate 0 → 255 over 3 seconds (3000 ms) for (int spd = 0; spd <= 255; spd++) { analogWrite(enablePin, spd); delay(3000 / 256); // ~11.7 ms per step } // Full speed hold for 2 seconds analogWrite(enablePin, 255); delay(2000); // Decelerate 255 → 0 for (int spd = 255; spd >= 0; spd--) { analogWrite(enablePin, spd); delay(3000 / 256); } // Coast stop digitalWrite(input1, LOW); digitalWrite(input2, LOW); analogWrite(enablePin, 0); delay(1000); // ===== REVERSE Direction ===== digitalWrite(input1, LOW); // CCW digitalWrite(input2, HIGH); for (int spd = 0; spd <= 255; spd++) { analogWrite(enablePin, spd); delay(3000 / 256); } analogWrite(enablePin, 255); delay(2000); for (int spd = 255; spd >= 0; spd--) { analogWrite(enablePin, spd); delay(3000 / 256); } digitalWrite(input1, HIGH); // Brake digitalWrite(input2, HIGH); analogWrite(enablePin, 0); delay(2000); } ``` **Explanation of Key Logic:** - The two `for` loops implement a **linear ramp** across **256 PWM steps** in **3000 ms**, giving a step delay of $\dfrac{3000}{256} \approx 11.72 \text{ ms}$. - `analogWrite(enablePin, 255)` corresponds to **100 % duty cycle** → maximum speed. - The reverse section swaps the direction bits and uses **active braking** (both inputs HIGH) at the end. *Marking distribution: Pin declarations & setup — 1 mark, Forward logic — 2 marks, Reverse logic — 2 marks, Ramp timing calculation — 1 mark, Code correctness — 1 mark.* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** Explain the working principle of an H-Bridge circuit for DC motor direction control. Draw the switch states for forward, reverse, and braking modes. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 9 V DC motor is connected to Arduino via L293D. If `analogWrite(pwmPin, 200)` is executed, calculate: (i) the duty cycle, (ii) the average voltage applied to the motor, and (iii) the motor RPM if the speed constant $k = 100 \text{ RPM/V}$. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks) **H-Bridge Theory:** An H-bridge consists of **four switches (S1, S2, S3, S4)** arranged in an "H" configuration with the DC motor forming the crossbar of the H. By selectively closing pairs of switches, the polarity of the voltage across the motor terminals can be reversed without changing the power supply polarity. **Switching States Table:** | Mode | S1 | S2 | S3 | S4 | Current Path | Motor Action | |------|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:-------------|:-------------| | Forward | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | V+ → S1 → Motor → S4 → GND | CW Rotation | | Reverse | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | V+ → S3 → Motor → S2 → GND | CCW Rotation | | Brake | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | Both motor terminals shorted to V+ | Active brake | | Coast | OFF | OFF | OFF | OFF | Motor terminals floating | Freewheel stop | **L293D Internal Implementation:** The L293D replaces the four mechanical switches with **four Darlington transistor pairs** (NPN high-current transistors) and includes **four internal flyback diodes** to protect against back-EMF spikes. *Marking distribution: H-bridge diagram/description — 2 marks, Switching states table — 3 marks, L293D mapping — 2 marks.* #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks) **Given Data:** - Motor supply voltage: $V_{CC} = 9 \text{ V}$ - analogWrite value: $N_{PWM} = 200$ - Speed constant: $k = 100 \text{ RPM/V}$ **Step (i) — Compute Duty Cycle (D):** $$ D = \frac{N_{PWM}}{255} = \frac{200}{255} $$ $$ D = 0.7843 = 78.43\% $$ **Step (ii) — Compute Average Motor Voltage:** $$ V_{avg} = V_{CC} \times D = 9 \text{ V} \times 0.7843 $$ $$ V_{avg} = 7.0587 \text{ V} \approx 7.06 \text{ V} $$ **Step (iii) — Compute Motor Speed (N):** $$ N = k \times V_{avg} = 100 \text{ RPM/V} \times 7.0587 \text{ V} $$ $$ N = 705.87 \text{ RPM} \approx 706 \text{ RPM} $$ **Final Answer Summary Table:** | Quantity | Calculated Value | Units | |----------|:----------------:|:-----:| | Duty Cycle (D) | 78.43 | % | | Average Voltage ($V_{avg}$) | 7.06 | V | | Motor Speed (N) | 706 | RPM | *Marking distribution: Formula for D — 2 marks, V_avg calculation — 2 marks, N calculation — 2 marks, Units and final answer — 1 mark.* --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - **Motor Driver is Mandatory** — Arduino GPIO cannot drive a DC motor directly; L293D is the standard interface IC for ≤ 600 mA loads. - **L293D has 16 pins** — memorize the function of Pin 1 (Enable), Pin 2/7 (Direction Inputs), Pin 3/6 (Motor Outputs), Pin 8 (Motor VCC), Pin 16 (5 V Logic VCC), and Pins 4, 5, 12, 15 (GND). - **Common Ground is Critical** — Arduino GND, L293D logic GND, and battery GND must all be tied to one shared ground node. - **Direction Control = Logic Bits** — `HIGH/LOW` on Input pins; speed control = PWM on Enable pin. - **PWM Resolution = 8-bit (0–255)** — `analogWrite(pin, value)` is the Arduino function. - **PWM Frequencies** — ~980 Hz on pins 5, 6; ~490 Hz on pins 9, 10, 11, 3. - **H-Bridge has 4 modes** — Forward, Reverse, Coast (freewheel), Brake (active). - **Coast vs. Brake** — Coast = both inputs LOW (motor freewheels); Brake = both inputs HIGH (active stopping torque). - **Duty Cycle Formula** — $D = \dfrac{\text{analogWrite}}{255}$; $V_{avg} = V_{CC} \cdot D$. - **Back-EMF Protection** — L293D has internal flyback diodes, but adding external **1N4007** diodes across motor terminals adds extra safety. - **PWM Efficiency** — > 90 % efficient compared to ~50 % for a linear series resistor; this is why PWM is industry-standard. - **analogWrite() vs. digitalWrite()** — `analogWrite()` generates PWM (used on `~` pins only); `digitalWrite()` generates pure HIGH/LOW. - **Lab Viva Favorite Question** — *"What happens if you set both Input pins HIGH?"* → **Active brake / motor stops quickly**. - **Lab Viva Favorite Question** — *"Why use a soft-start ramp instead of full speed instantly?"* → **Reduces inrush current, prevents motor and driver damage, mechanical stress on gearbox**. - **KTU Real-World Application** — Wheelchair motors, robotic arm joints, conveyor belts, line-follower robot wheels, drone ESCs (similar principle), CNC axis drives.

topics.title: Interfacing Servo Motors with Arduino - Angle of Rotation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAL607 - ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Interfacing Servo Motors with Arduino - Angle of Rotation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Servo Motor

A **Servo Motor** is a rotary or linear actuator that allows for precise control of angular (or linear) position, velocity, and acceleration. It consists of a DC motor, a gear reduction system, a position sensor (typically a potentiometer), and a control circuit. In the context of the KTU 2024 Robotics Lab syllabus, the **hobby servo motor** (e.g., **SG90**, **MG996R**) is a position-controlled device driven by a **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** signal.

The **Angle of Rotation** refers to the angular displacement of the servo shaft from its neutral (0°) reference position, which is directly proportional to the width of the control pulse sent to the motor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A standard hobby servo rotates from **0° to 180°** and is controlled by a **50 Hz PWM signal** (20 ms period) with a pulse width varying between **1 ms** and **2 ms**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine the **steering wheel of a car**. When you turn the wheel a little, the car turns a little. When you turn it more, the car turns more. The steering system has a "built-in intelligence" that precisely maps your hand movement to the wheel angle.

A **servo motor** works exactly the same way, except instead of a human hand turning a wheel, an **Arduino sends electrical pulses** of varying widths. The servo's internal brain (control circuit) reads the pulse width and rotates the shaft to the corresponding angle — and then **holds that position firmly**, even if you try to push it.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big Idea:** A servo is NOT just a motor — it is a **closed-loop position control system**. You tell it *where* to go, and it goes there and stays there.

## 1.3 Hardware Identification

A hobby servo has **3 wires**, color-coded as follows:

| Wire Color | Function | Connect To |
|------------|----------|------------|
| **Red** (or Orange-Red) | Power (VCC) | Arduino **5V** |
| **Brown / Black** | Ground (GND) | Arduino **GND** |
| **Orange / Yellow / White** | Signal (PWM) | Arduino **Digital Pin (e.g., 9)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Power Caution:** Small servos like SG90 can be powered directly from Arduino's 5V pin. However, **high-torque servos (MG996R)** draw >300 mA under load and **MUST** be powered from an external 5V–6V supply, with the Arduino and servo GNDs connected together (common ground).

## 1.4 Pulse Width vs. Angle — The Core Mapping

The relationship is **linear** and is the heart of this experiment:

$$
\theta = \frac{t_{\text{pulse}} - 1\,\text{ms}}{1\,\text{ms}} \times 180°
$$

| Pulse Width ($t_{\text{pulse}}$) | Shaft Angle ($\theta$) | Duty Cycle |
|:--------------------------------:|:----------------------:|:----------:|
| **1.0 ms** | **0°** (Full Counter-Clockwise) | **5%** |
| **1.5 ms** | **90°** (Neutral / Center) | **7.5%** |
| **2.0 ms** | **180°** (Full Clockwise) | **10%** |

The **Period** is always **T = 20 ms** (Frequency **f = 50 Hz**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physics: Why PWM Controls a Servo

A servo motor contains a **potentiometer** mechanically coupled to its output shaft. The internal control circuit continuously compares:
- The **target position** (encoded by the incoming PWM pulse width).
- The **actual position** (read from the potentiometer as a voltage divider).

If there is a difference (an **error signal**), the motor is driven in the appropriate direction until the error becomes zero. This is a classic **negative feedback closed-loop system**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The servo is a textbook example of a **first-order position control system** with integral action — the "brain" keeps correcting until perfect alignment is achieved.

## 2.2 The Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) Signal

A PWM signal is a square wave characterized by two key parameters:

1. **Frequency ($f$)** — how often the pulse repeats per second.
2. **Duty Cycle ($D$)** — the fraction of time the signal is HIGH within one period.

For a standard hobby servo:
- The **frequency is fixed at 50 Hz** — meaning the period is always 20 ms.
- The **duty cycle varies** between 5% and 10% to command different angles.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Servo Angle Control

- **Step 1:** Arduino generates a 50 Hz square wave on a digital pin using the built-in `Servo.h` library.
- **Step 2:** The library sets the **ON-time (pulse width)** of each cycle to a value between 1000 µs and 2000 µs.
- **Step 3:** The servo's control circuit measures the pulse width using an internal monostable multivibrator.
- **Step 4:** The measured width is compared with the potentiometer voltage (representing actual shaft angle).
- **Step 5:** The error drives the H-bridge motor circuit to rotate the shaft CW or CCW.
- **Step 6:** Once the actual position matches the commanded position, the motor stops, and the shaft **holds** the position against external torque.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Numerical Value | Unit |
|-----------|:------:|---------|:---------------:|:----:|
| PWM Frequency | $f$ | — | **50** | Hz |
| PWM Period | $T$ | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | **20** | ms |
| Pulse Width at 0° | $t_0$ | — | **1.0** | ms |
| Pulse Width at 90° | $t_{90}$ | — | **1.5** | ms |
| Pulse Width at 180° | $t_{180}$ | — | **2.0** | ms |
| Pulse Width (general) | $t_{\text{pulse}}$ | $t_0 + \dfrac{\theta}{180°} \times (t_{180} - t_0)$ | varies | ms |
| Angle (general) | $\theta$ | $\dfrac{t_{\text{pulse}} - 1\,\text{ms}}{1\,\text{ms}} \times 180°$ | varies | degrees |
| Duty Cycle | $D$ | $D = \dfrac{t_{\text{pulse}}}{T} \times 100\%$ | **5% to 10%** | % |
| Pulse Width in microseconds | $t_{\mu s}$ | $t_{\text{ms}} \times 1000$ | **1000 to 2000** | µs |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Servo motors are the workhorses of:
- **Robotic Arms** — precise joint control.
- **RC Vehicles** — steering and throttle.
- **CNC Machines and 3D Printers** — tool head positioning.
- **Camera Gimbals** — stabilization.
- **Biomedical Devices** — surgical robots, prosthetics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Understanding PWM-based servo control is foundational for **industrial robotics, automation, and embedded systems** — all core KTU 2024 syllabus outcomes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Deriving the Pulse Width from a Given Angle

We are given:
- At $\theta_1 = 0°$, pulse width $t_1 = 1.0\,\text{ms}$.
- At $\theta_2 = 180°$, pulse width $t_2 = 2.0\,\text{ms}$.

The mapping is **linear**. Using the two-point form of a line:

$$
\frac{t - t_1}{t_2 - t_1} = \frac{\theta - \theta_1}{\theta_2 - \theta_1}
$$

Substituting the known values:

$$
\frac{t - 1.0}{2.0 - 1.0} = \frac{\theta - 0°}{180° - 0°}
$$

$$
\frac{t - 1.0}{1.0} = \frac{\theta}{180°}
$$

Solving for the pulse width $t$:

$$
t - 1.0 = \frac{\theta}{180°}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t = 1.0 + \frac{\theta}{180°} \quad \text{(in milliseconds)}\,}
$$

**Verification at endpoints:**
- At $\theta = 0°$: $t = 1.0 + 0 = 1.0\,\text{ms}$ ✓
- At $\theta = 180°$: $t = 1.0 + 1.0 = 2.0\,\text{ms}$ ✓
- At $\theta = 90°$: $t = 1.0 + 0.5 = 1.5\,\text{ms}$ ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Deriving the Angle from a Given Pulse Width

Inverting the formula from Derivation 1:

$$
t = 1.0 + \frac{\theta}{180°}
$$

$$
t - 1.0 = \frac{\theta}{180°}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\theta = (t - 1.0) \times 180° \quad \text{(with } t \text{ in ms)}\,}
$$

**Worked Example:** A pulse of **1.7 ms** is sent. Find the angle.

$$
\theta = (1.7 - 1.0) \times 180°
$$

$$
\theta = 0.7 \times 180°
$$

$$
\theta = 126°
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Duty Cycle as a Function of Angle

The duty cycle is defined as:

$$
D = \frac{t_{\text{pulse}}}{T} \times 100\%
$$

Substituting $t_{\text{pulse}} = 1.0 + \dfrac{\theta}{180°}$ and $T = 20\,\text{ms}$:

$$
D = \frac{1.0 + \dfrac{\theta}{180°}}{20} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\,D = 5\% + \frac{\theta}{180°} \times 5\% = 5\%\left(1 + \frac{\theta}{180°}\right)\,}
$$

**Worked Example:** What is the duty cycle for $\theta = 45°$?

$$
D = 5\% \left(1 + \frac{45}{180}\right) = 5\% \times 1.25 = 6.25\%
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Pulse Width in Microseconds (Arduino `writeMicroseconds`)

Arduino's `Servo.h` library internally works in **microseconds (µs)** for finer resolution.

$$
t_{\mu s} = t_{\text{ms}} \times 1000
$$

Using the linear mapping $t_{\text{ms}} = 1.0 + \dfrac{\theta}{180°}$:

$$
\boxed{\,t_{\mu s} = 1000 + \frac{\theta}{180°} \times 1000 = 1000 + 5.556 \times \theta\,}
$$

**Worked Example:** For $\theta = 120°$:

$$
t_{\mu s} = 1000 + 5.556 \times 120 = 1000 + 666.67 = 1666.67\,\mu s
$$

This value can be passed directly to `servo.writeMicroseconds(1667)`.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Input Given | Output Required | Formula to Use | Final Result Format |
|:-----------:|:---------------:|:--------------:|:-------------------:|
| Angle $\theta$ (in degrees) | Pulse width (ms) | $t = 1.0 + \theta / 180$ | milliseconds |
| Angle $\theta$ (in degrees) | Pulse width (µs) | $t = 1000 + 5.556 \times \theta$ | microseconds |
| Angle $\theta$ (in degrees) | Duty cycle (%) | $D = 5\% \times (1 + \theta/180)$ | percentage |
| Pulse width $t$ (ms) | Angle $\theta$ (degrees) | $\theta = (t - 1.0) \times 180$ | degrees |
| Pulse width $t$ (µs) | Angle $\theta$ (degrees) | $\theta = (t - 1000) / 5.556$ | degrees |
| Duty cycle $D$ (%) | Angle $\theta$ (degrees) | $\theta = (D / 5 - 1) \times 180$ | degrees |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Connection Diagram (Mermaid Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional wiring topology** between the Arduino UNO and the Servo Motor. Since physical pin-layout schematics are difficult in text, this representation captures every electrical connection clearly.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Arduino UNO Board] -->|Digital Pin 9 PWM| B[Servo Motor SG90]
    A -->|5V Power Rail| C[VCC Red Wire]
    A -->|GND Common Ground| D[GND Brown Black Wire]
    B -->|Signal Orange Wire| E[PWM Control Input]
    C --> F[Internal DC Motor and Gear Train]
    D --> F
    E --> G[Servo Control Circuit and Potentiometer Feedback]
    F --> G
    G -->|Closed Loop Correction| H[Output Shaft 0 to 180 degrees]
    H -->|Mechanical Coupling| I[Potentiometer Wiper Voltage Feedback]
    I --> G
```

## 4.2 Signal Flow Diagram — Angle Command to Shaft Motion

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[User Code servo write 90] --> B[Arduino Servo Library]
    B --> C[Generate 50 Hz PWM on Pin 9]
    C --> D[1.5 ms HIGH Pulse]
    D --> E[Servo Control Board Receives Pulse]
    E --> F{Compare with Potentiometer Voltage}
    F -->|Error Detected| G[Drive H Bridge Motor]
    G --> H[Gear Train Rotates Shaft]
    H --> I[Potentiometer Wiper Moves]
    I --> F
    F -->|Error Zero| J[Shaft Holds at 90 degrees]
```

## 4.3 Program Execution Flowchart (Sweep Operation)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start([Program Start]) --> Include[Include Servo h Library]
    Include --> Create[Create Servo Object myServo]
    Create --> Setup[Setup: attach pin 9]
    Setup --> Loop([Loop Begins])
    Loop --> For1[For angle 0 to 180 increment by 1]
    For1 --> Write1[myServo write angle]
    Write1 --> Delay1[Delay 15 ms]
    Delay1 --> For2[For angle 180 to 0 decrement by 1]
    For2 --> Write2[myServo write angle]
    Write2 --> Delay2[Delay 15 ms]
    Delay2 --> Loop
```

## 4.4 Pin-to-Pin Connection Table

| Arduino UNO Pin | Servo Wire Color | Function | Notes |
|:---------------:|:----------------:|:---------|:------|
| **5V** | **Red** | VCC Power Supply | Provides regulated 5V DC |
| **GND** | **Brown / Black** | Common Ground | Must be common with servo GND |
| **Digital Pin 9** | **Orange / Yellow** | PWM Signal Input | Any PWM-capable pin works (3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Tip:** On Arduino UNO, PWM-capable pins are marked with a **~ (tilde)** symbol. Always choose a PWM pin for servo control, although the `Servo.h` library can actually use **any digital pin** because it uses software-based PWM via timers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**Explain how the angle of rotation of a hobby servo motor is controlled using PWM signals.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A hobby servo motor is controlled by a **50 Hz PWM signal** (period = 20 ms). The **pulse width** of the HIGH portion determines the shaft angle. A pulse of **1 ms** corresponds to **0°**, **1.5 ms** to **90°**, and **2 ms** to **180°**. The servo's internal control circuit compares the incoming pulse width with the potentiometer feedback voltage (representing the actual shaft angle) and drives the DC motor to reduce the error to zero, thereby holding the commanded position. (3 marks — 1 mark for 50 Hz/20 ms, 1 mark for pulse-to-angle mapping, 1 mark for closed-loop feedback explanation.)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]
**List the three wires of a standard SG90 servo motor and state their functions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The SG90 servo has three wires: **(i) Red wire** — connects to **VCC (5V)** for power supply; **(ii) Brown/Black wire** — connects to **GND** for common ground reference; **(iii) Orange/Yellow wire** — the **signal wire** that receives the PWM control pulse from the Arduino digital pin. (3 marks — 1 mark per wire-function pair.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **one full question** from a choice of two. Both options are provided below.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, explain the interfacing of a servo motor with an Arduino UNO. Mention the role of the `Servo.h` library and its key functions.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Hardware Interfacing (3 marks):**
- Connect the **Red wire** of the servo to the **5V** pin of Arduino UNO.
- Connect the **Brown/Black wire** to the **GND** pin of Arduino UNO.
- Connect the **Orange/Yellow signal wire** to any **digital pin (e.g., D9)** of Arduino UNO.
- The Arduino generates a **50 Hz PWM signal** on D9; the servo's internal control circuit interprets the pulse width and drives the DC motor to the corresponding angle.

**2. Role of `Servo.h` Library (2 marks):**
- `#include <Servo.h>` — includes the library that abstracts low-level timer registers.
- The library uses **Timer1** on Arduino UNO to generate accurate 50 Hz PWM with variable pulse width (1000–2000 µs).
- It supports up to **12 servos** on UNO (6 on most other boards).

**3. Key Functions (2 marks):**
- `myservo.attach(pin)` — attaches the servo object to a digital pin.
- `myservo.write(angle)` — sets the shaft angle (0° to 180°); the library internally converts to pulse width.
- `myservo.writeMicroseconds(us)` — directly sets pulse width in microseconds (fine control).
- `myservo.read()` — returns the currently commanded angle.
- `myservo.detach()` — releases the pin from the servo library.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Write a complete Arduino program to sweep a servo motor continuously from 0° to 180° and back, with a 15 ms delay between each step. Add comments to each line.**

**Model Solution:**

```cpp
#include <Servo.h>          // Step 1: Include the Servo library
Servo myServo;              // Step 2: Create a Servo object named myServo
int servoPin = 9;           // Step 3: Define the signal pin number
int delayTime = 15;         // Step 4: Define the delay between steps in ms

void setup() {
  myServo.attach(servoPin); // Step 5: Attach the servo to pin 9
}

void loop() {
  // Sweep from 0 to 180 degrees
  for (int angle = 0; angle <= 180; angle++) {
    myServo.write(angle);   // Step 6: Command the servo to move to 'angle'
    delay(delayTime);       // Step 7: Wait 15 ms for the servo to reach position
  }

  // Sweep from 180 back to 0 degrees
  for (int angle = 180; angle >= 0; angle--) {
    myServo.write(angle);   // Step 8: Command the servo to move to 'angle'
    delay(delayTime);       // Step 9: Wait 15 ms
  }
}
```

**Mark Distribution:** Library include and object creation (1), setup/attach (1), forward sweep logic (2), reverse sweep logic (2), comments and code quality (1).

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the linear relationship between the PWM pulse width and the angle of rotation of a standard hobby servo motor. Also calculate the duty cycle for a 60° rotation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify Boundary Conditions (2 marks):**
- At $\theta = 0°$: pulse width $t = 1.0\,\text{ms}$
- At $\theta = 180°$: pulse width $t = 2.0\,\text{ms}$
- Period $T = 20\,\text{ms}$ (since $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$)

**Step 2: Apply Two-Point Linear Equation (3 marks):**
Using $\dfrac{t - t_1}{t_2 - t_1} = \dfrac{\theta - \theta_1}{\theta_2 - \theta_1}$:

$$
\frac{t - 1.0}{2.0 - 1.0} = \frac{\theta - 0}{180 - 0}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t = 1.0 + \frac{\theta}{180°}\,} \quad \text{(in ms)}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Duty Cycle for 60° (2 marks):**
First, the pulse width:

$$
t = 1.0 + \frac{60}{180} = 1.0 + 0.333 = 1.333\,\text{ms}
$$

Then, the duty cycle:

$$
D = \frac{t}{T} \times 100\% = \frac{1.333}{20} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\,D = 6.667\%\,}
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Design an Arduino-based system to control two servo motors independently. Provide the circuit description and complete program code.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Circuit Design (3 marks):**
- **Servo 1:** Signal → D9, VCC → 5V, GND → GND
- **Servo 2:** Signal → D10, VCC → 5V, GND → GND
- For high-current servos, use an **external 5V, 2A power supply** with common GND to Arduino.

**2. Program Code (4 marks):**

```cpp
#include <Servo.h>

Servo servo1;   // Create object for first servo
Servo servo2;   // Create object for second servo

void setup() {
  servo1.attach(9);   // Servo 1 on pin 9
  servo2.attach(10);  // Servo 2 on pin 10
}

void loop() {
  // Move Servo 1 to 0 deg, Servo 2 to 180 deg
  servo1.write(0);
  servo2.write(180);
  delay(1000);

  // Move Servo 1 to 90 deg, Servo 2 to 90 deg
  servo1.write(90);
  servo2.write(90);
  delay(1000);

  // Move Servo 1 to 180 deg, Servo 2 to 0 deg
  servo1.write(180);
  servo2.write(0);
  delay(1000);
}
```

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong frequency assumption** — Several students write "PWM frequency is 1000 Hz" (Arduino default for analogWrite). Always state **50 Hz / 20 ms period** for servos.
> 2. **Confusing `analogWrite()` with `Servo.write()`** — `analogWrite()` produces a fixed-frequency PWM (e.g., 490 Hz or 980 Hz) and is **NOT** suitable for hobby servos. The `Servo.h` library generates proper 50 Hz control signals.
> 3. **Forgetting common ground** — When using an external power supply, students often forget to connect the Arduino GND to the servo GND. This is the **#1 cause of "servo not responding"** in KTU lab viva questions.
> 4. **Writing pulse width as 1–2 µs instead of 1–2 ms** — A 1000× unit error. Always double-check units.
> 5. **Missing the `Servo.h` `#include`** — Code will not compile. Examiners deduct a full mark for this.
> 6. **Incorrect sweep direction** — In the reverse sweep, students often write `angle <= 0` instead of `angle >= 0`, creating an **infinite loop bug**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Interfacing Servo Motors with Arduino**
> - A **hobby servo** is a **closed-loop position controller** driven by **50 Hz PWM**.
> - **Pulse width = 1 ms → 0°**, **1.5 ms → 90°**, **2 ms → 180°** (linear mapping).
> - **Period = 20 ms**, **Duty cycle range = 5% to 10%**.
> - **Master formula:** $t_{\text{ms}} = 1.0 + \dfrac{\theta}{180°}$
> - **Inverse formula:** $\theta = (t_{\text{ms}} - 1.0) \times 180°$
> - **Three wires:** Red (5V), Brown/Black (GND), Orange (Signal).
> - **Arduino library:** `#include <Servo.h>` → `attach()`, `write()`, `writeMicroseconds()`.
> - **Signal pin** can be any digital pin (the library uses software-timer-based PWM).
> - **High-torque servos** require an **external power supply** with **common ground** to Arduino.
> - **SG90 stall current** ≈ 650 mA; **MG996R stall current** ≈ 2.5 A — never power these from Arduino 5V pin alone.
> - **Sweep delay** of 15–20 ms allows the servo shaft to reach the commanded position before the next command.
> - The servo library on Arduino UNO disables **Pin 9 and Pin 10 PWM (analogWrite)** functionality while servos are attached.
> - In KTU viva, always be ready to explain **why a servo holds its position** (potentiometer feedback + H-bridge braking).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarisation of Raspberry Pi and its I/O Interfacing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAL607 - ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarisation of Raspberry Pi and its I/O Interfacing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Raspberry Pi** is a series of small, single-board computers (SBCs) developed by the **Raspberry Pi Foundation** (UK) to promote the teaching of basic computer science in schools and developing countries. In the context of the KTU 2024 Robotics Lab syllabus, the Raspberry Pi functions as a **System on Chip (SoC)** based embedded controller that integrates a **Broadcom BCM2837/BCM2711** processor, **GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output)** pins, and standard communication peripherals (**UART, I²C, SPI**) on a single credit-card-sized Printed Circuit Board (PCB). It runs a Linux-based operating system (typically **Raspberry Pi OS**, formerly Raspbian) and is programmed using high-level languages such as **Python 3** via the `RPi.GPIO` library.

**I/O Interfacing** refers to the systematic connection and communication between the Raspberry Pi's GPIO pins and external physical devices — both **sinks** (LEDs, buzzers, relays, DC motors) and **sources** (push-buttons, sensors, encoders) — to acquire real-world signals or actuate electro-mechanical systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment is foundational for all subsequent robotic experiments. Mastery of pin numbering (BOARD vs BCM), pull-up/pull-down resistors, and PWM signal generation is mandatory.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Miniature Brain" of a Robot

Imagine a **human nervous system** mapped onto a tiny motherboard:
- The **SoC (CPU + GPU + RAM)** acts as the **brain** — it processes Python instructions and makes logical decisions.
- The **40-pin GPIO header** is the **spinal cord** — every physical pin is a nerve ending that can either *feel* the outside world (Input) or *command* it (Output).
- The **microSD card** is the **memory** — it stores the operating system (long-term memory) and your Python scripts (working memory).
- **Python with `RPi.GPIO`** is the **language the brain thinks in** — it translates human logic ("if button pressed, light LED") into the 3.3 V electrical pulses that GPIO pins understand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage Rule of Thumb:** Raspberry Pi GPIO pins operate strictly at **3.3 V DC** with a maximum source/sink current of **16 mA per pin** (total board current ≈ 50 mA). Connecting a 5 V signal directly to a GPIO pin will permanently damage the SoC. Always use a **level shifter** or **current-limiting resistor** when interfacing with 5 V logic or high-current loads.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Electrical Specifications

| Parameter | Raspberry Pi 4 Model B | Raspberry Pi 3 Model B+ | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| SoC | Broadcom BCM2711 | Broadcom BCM2837B0 | — |
| CPU | Quad-core Cortex-A72 @ 1.8 GHz | Quad-core Cortex-A53 @ 1.4 GHz | GHz |
| RAM | 2 / 4 / 8 GB LPDDR4 | 1 GB LPDDR2 | GB |
| GPIO Voltage | 3.3 | 3.3 | V |
| GPIO Current (per pin) | 16 (max), 8 (recommended) | 16 (max), 8 (recommended) | mA |
| GPIO Header | 40-pin (2 × 20) | 40-pin (2 × 20) | pins |
| Operating Temp | 0 to 50 | 0 to 50 | °C |
| Power Input | 5 V / 3 A (USB-C) | 5 V / 2.5 A (Micro-USB) | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Breakdown of the Raspberry Pi Board

The Raspberry Pi is engineered as a layered, modular embedded platform. Understanding each subsystem is critical for the KTU lab viva.

**1. System on Chip (SoC) Layer**
- The **Broadcom BCM2711** integrates a quad-core **ARM Cortex-A72** CPU, a **VideoCore VI** GPU, and shared L2 cache.
- This ARM-based architecture executes instructions from the **Raspbian/Raspberry Pi OS** kernel (a Debian Linux distribution).

**2. Memory Subsystem**
- **LPDDR4 RAM** is soldered directly on top of the SoC (Package-on-Package topology) for high bandwidth and low latency.
- The **microSD card** acts as the primary non-volatile storage, holding the bootloader, OS, libraries, and user scripts.

**3. Power Management Unit (PMU)**
- A dedicated **PMIC** (e.g., MXL7704 on Pi 4) converts the 5 V input into multiple voltage rails: **3.3 V, 2.5 V, 1.8 V, 1.2 V, 0.8 V**, each powering specific subsystems.
- **Under-voltage detection**: If the input drops below **4.63 V ± 5%**, a yellow lightning bolt icon appears and a warning is logged to `dmesg`.

**4. The 40-pin GPIO Header**
- Pins are arranged in two rows of 20 (odd pins on top, even pins on bottom).
- Functionality is multiplexed: each pin can serve as **GPIO, I²C, SPI, UART, PWM, or PCM** depending on alternate function configuration.

**5. Peripheral Interfaces (Hardware Buses)**
- **I²C (Inter-Integrated Circuit)** — 2-wire synchronous serial bus (SDA + SCL) for low-speed peripherals (sensors, EEPROMs).
- **SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface)** — 4-wire full-duplex bus (MOSI, MISO, SCLK, CE) for high-speed peripherals (ADC, display).
- **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter)** — asynchronous serial (TX, RX) for debugging or GPS modules.

### 2.2 GPIO Numbering — The Most Common Viva Trap

The Raspberry Pi supports **TWO simultaneous pin numbering schemes**. Forgetting to set the correct mode is the #1 cause of "my LED won't turn on" lab failures.

| Scheme | Identifier | Example (Pin 11) | Library Constant |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BOARD** | Physical header position | Pin 11 = GPIO17 | `RPi.GPIO.BOARD` |
| **BCM** | Broadcom SoC channel number | Pin 11 = GPIO17 | `RPi.GPIO.BCM` |
| **WiringPi** | Deprecated virtual pin map | Pin 11 = GPIO0 | `GPIO.WIRINGPI` |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Examiners routinely ask: *"In the BOARD numbering scheme, which physical pin corresponds to GPIO 18?"* The correct answer is **Pin 12**. BCM and BOARD numbering are **not** identical, even when they happen to share the same value (as in pin 11/GPIO 17).

### 2.3 Electrical Behavior of a GPIO Pin

Every GPIO pin can be software-configured as one of three logical states:

1. **INPUT** — Pin is set to high-impedance (`Z`) mode. Voltage at the pin is sampled and reported as `HIGH` (≥ ~1.8 V) or `LOW` (≤ ~0.8 V).
2. **OUTPUT** — Pin is driven to either **0 V (LOW)** or **3.3 V (HIGH)**. Configured as a CMOS push-pull stage.
3. **ALTERNATE FUNCTION** — Pin is internally routed to a peripheral block (I²C, SPI, PWM, UART).

**Pull-up / Pull-down Resistors**
- Floating inputs are vulnerable to **electromagnetic noise**. The Pi provides software-controllable internal resistors:
  - `GPIO.PUD_UP` → connects the pin internally to 3.3 V through ~50 kΩ.
  - `GPIO.PUD_DOWN` → connects the pin internally to 0 V (GND) through ~50 kΩ.
  - `GPIO.PUD_OFF` → no internal resistor (must use external pull-up/down).

### 2.4 PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) Fundamentals

The Raspberry Pi's hardware PWM is available on specific pins (BCM 12, 13, 18, 19). PWM simulates an **analog output** by rapidly toggling a digital pin between HIGH and LOW.

**Key PWM Parameters:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency | $f_{PWM}$ | Number of complete ON-OFF cycles per second | 1 Hz – 125 MHz (software), hardware ≈ 1 kHz default |
| Duty Cycle | $D$ | Fraction of one period the signal is HIGH | 0 % to 100 % |
| Period | $T$ | Time for one full cycle: $T = 1 / f_{PWM}$ | seconds |
| Resolution | $\Delta D$ | Smallest increment the hardware can produce | hardware: 1/1024; software: 1/100 (variable) |

**Average Output Voltage:**

$$V_{avg} = D \times V_{HIGH} = D \times 3.3 \text{ V}$$

**Real-World Utility:** In robotics, PWM is the standard technique for:
- Controlling **DC motor speed** (via H-bridge drivers like L298N).
- Setting the **angle of a servo motor** (50 Hz, 1 ms–2 ms pulse width).
- Dimming **LED brightness** (perceived luminous intensity ≈ $V_{avg}$).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Viva Shortcut:** When asked *"Why can't we simply output 1.65 V from a GPIO pin to dim an LED halfway?"*, answer: *"Because the GPIO is a digital pin — it can only output 0 V or 3.3 V. PWM emulates an analog voltage by time-averaging a digital square wave."*

### 2.5 Communication Protocol Reference Sheet

| Protocol | Pins Used (BCM) | Speed | Topology | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I²C** | SDA = GPIO2, SCL = GPIO3 | 100 kHz (Standard) / 400 kHz (Fast) | Multi-master, multi-slave (up to 127 devices via 7-bit addressing) | MPU6050 IMU, BMP280, OLED display |
| **SPI** | MOSI=GPIO10, MISO=GPIO9, SCLK=GPIO11, CE0=GPIO8, CE1=GPIO7 | Up to ~125 MHz | Master-slave, full-duplex | MCP3008 ADC, TFT displays |
| **UART** | TXD=GPIO14, RXD=GPIO15 | Configurable baud (e.g., 9600, 115200) | Point-to-point asynchronous | Debug console, GPS, Bluetooth HC-05 |
| **1-Wire** | Any GPIO (typically GPIO4) | ~16 kbps | Single data line + GND | DS18B20 temperature sensor |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix

> Since this is a **Lab course (PCCAL607)**, the "derivations" are replaced by exhaustive **experimental procedures, code walkthroughs, and signal-trace analyses**. Every step is fully expanded — no shortcuts.

### 3.1 Procedure A — Raspberry Pi OS Installation and Headless Boot

**Step 1: Image Flashing**
- Download **Raspberry Pi Imager** (v1.8+) from the official `raspberrypi.com` site.
- Insert a ≥ 16 GB microSD card (Class 10 or A1 rated).
- In the Imager, select:
  - **Device** → Pi 4 (or your model)
  - **OS** → Raspberry Pi OS (64-bit, Desktop)
  - **Storage** → your SD card
- Click the **gear icon (⚙)** for advanced options and pre-configure:
  - `Enable SSH` → use password authentication
  - `Set username and password` → e.g., `pi / raspberry` (change for security)
  - `Configure wireless LAN` → enter your SSID and PSK
  - `Set locale settings` → Asia/Kolkata, keyboard layout
- Click **Write**. The image is flashed and verified.

**Step 2: First Boot and Network Discovery**
- Insert the SD card, connect HDMI, keyboard, mouse, and **5 V / 3 A USB-C** power.
- On first boot, the OS expands the filesystem, generates SSH keys, and connects to Wi-Fi.
- To find the Pi's IP from a Windows PC, open Command Prompt and type:

  ```bat
  arp -a
  ping raspberrypi.local
  ```

- From any other machine on the same LAN, SSH in:

  ```bash
  ssh pi@192.168.1.42
  ```

**Step 3: System Update and Essential Packages**

```bash
sudo apt update && sudo apt full-upgrade -y
sudo apt install -y python3-gpiozero python3-rpi.gpio python3-smbus i2c-tools
sudo raspi-config
```

In `raspi-config`:
- **Interface Options → I2C** → Enable
- **Interface Options → SPI** → Enable
- **Interface Options → Serial Port** → Disable login shell, Enable hardware
- **Interface Options → 1-Wire** → Enable
- **Performance Options → GPU Memory** → set to 16 (if headless, or 256 for desktop use)
- Reboot with `sudo reboot`.

**Step 4: Verify Hardware with Command-Line Tools**

```bash
# Check if the I²C bus is alive
ls /dev/i2c*       # expected: /dev/i2c-1
i2cdetect -y 1     # scans all 127 I²C addresses

# Test the GPIO character device (libgpiod)
gpiodetect
gpioinfo gpiochip0

# Check kernel messages for any HAT / under-voltage warnings
dmesg | grep -i "voltage\|hat\|i2c"
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of the i2cdetect output:** If a device is at address `0x68`, you will see a grid showing `68` in the row. An empty cell (`--`) means no ACKnowledge from that address. This is the standard way to confirm an IMU, RTC, or display is correctly wired.

---

### 3.2 Procedure B — LED Interfacing (Digital Output)

**Aim:** Blink an external LED connected to GPIO 17 (physical pin 11) using Python.

**Circuit Diagram (Textual Description):**
- GPIO 17 (Pin 11) → **330 Ω resistor** (current limiter) → **LED anode (long lead, +)**
- LED cathode (short lead, −) → **GND (Pin 6)**
- **Calculation of current-limiting resistor:**

$$R = \frac{V_{supply} - V_{F,LED}}{I_{LED}} = \frac{3.3 \text{ V} - 2.0 \text{ V}}{5 \text{ mA}} = 260 \text{ Ω} \approx 330 \text{ Ω (standard)}$$

**Python Program (using `RPi.GPIO`):**

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

# --- Configuration ---
LED_PIN  = 17          # BCM number for physical pin 11
GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) # use Broadcom chip numbering
GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT)

try:
    while True:
        GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH)  # LED ON  (3.3 V at pin)
        time.sleep(1)
        GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW)   # LED OFF (0 V at pin)
        time.sleep(1)
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    pass                # user pressed Ctrl+C
finally:
    GPIO.cleanup()      # ALWAYS reset pins to input mode
```

**Step-by-step logic walkthrough:**

1. `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)` — tells the library to interpret pin numbers using the SoC's channel map. *Why?* Because BCM 17 is the only number that is stable across Pi revisions.
2. `GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT)` — configures the pin's output driver (CMOS push-pull stage) and software-enables any pull resistors (default: none).
3. `GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH)` — drives the pin to 3.3 V; current flows through the 330 Ω resistor, dropping $V_R = 5 \text{ mA} \times 330 \text{ Ω} = 1.65 \text{ V}$, leaving ~1.65 V across the LED (sufficient to forward-bias a red LED).
4. `time.sleep(1)` — blocks execution for exactly 1 second; the LED stays in its current state.
5. `GPIO.cleanup()` in the `finally` block — **mandatory**; resets all configured pins back to `INPUT` mode, preventing the "GPIO pin already in use" warning on the next script run.

---

### 3.3 Procedure C — Push-Button Interfacing (Digital Input with Interrupt)

**Aim:** Toggle the LED state each time a momentary push-button is pressed. Use a hardware interrupt (no CPU polling).

**Wiring:**
- One leg of the button → **GPIO 27 (Pin 13)**
- Other leg → **GND (Pin 14)**
- Use **internal pull-up** (`GPIO.PUD_UP`) — pin reads `HIGH` when button is open, `LOW` when pressed.

**Python Program (callback-based interrupt):**

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

LED_PIN  = 17
BTN_PIN  = 27
led_state = False               # global toggle flag

def button_callback(channel):
    """ISR: called on FALLING edge (button press)."""
    global led_state
    led_state = not led_state
    GPIO.output(LED_PIN, led_state)
    print(f"Button on {channel} → LED {'ON' if led_state else 'OFF'}")

GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT)
GPIO.setup(BTN_PIN, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP)

# Falling edge: 3.3 V → 0 V when the button closes the circuit to GND
GPIO.add_event_detect(BTN_PIN, GPIO.FALLING,
                      callback=button_callback,
                      bouncetime=200)   # 200 ms hardware debounce

try:
    while True:
        time.sleep(0.1)    # idle — main thread is free
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    pass
finally:
    GPIO.cleanup()
```

**Why `bouncetime=200`?** Mechanical push-buttons exhibit **contact bounce** — the metal contacts vibrate for 1–10 ms before settling, which the CPU would interpret as multiple presses. The 200 ms software debounce filter ignores re-triggering for 200 ms after the first valid edge, ensuring exactly one callback per physical press.

---

### 3.4 Procedure D — PWM for LED Brightness and DC Motor Speed

**Aim:** Sweep an LED's brightness from 0 % to 100 % and back using hardware PWM.

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

PWM_PIN = 18                    # BCM 18, physical pin 12 (HW PWM capable)
PWM_FREQ = 1000                 # 1 kHz — above human flicker threshold

GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
GPIO.setup(PWM_PIN, GPIO.OUT)
pwm = GPIO.PWM(PWM_PIN, PWM_FREQ)
pwm.start(0)                    # start with 0 % duty cycle

try:
    while True:
        for duty in range(0, 101, 5):     # ramp up: 0 → 100 % in steps of 5
            pwm.ChangeDutyCycle(duty)
            time.sleep(0.05)
        for duty in range(100, -1, -5):   # ramp down: 100 → 0 %
            pwm.ChangeDutyCycle(duty)
            time.sleep(0.05)
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    pass
finally:
    pwm.stop()
    GPIO.cleanup()
```

**Analytical Trace — Average Voltage vs. Duty Cycle:**

$$V_{avg} = D \times 3.3 \text{ V}$$

| Duty Cycle D | $V_{avg}$ (V) | Perceived LED Brightness |
|---|---|---|
| 0 % | 0.00 | Off |
| 25 % | 0.83 | Dim |
| 50 % | 1.65 | Half |
| 75 % | 2.48 | Bright |
| 100 % | 3.30 | Full |

> The human eye's **logarithmic response** to luminance means a 50 % duty cycle appears closer to ~70 % of perceived full brightness — important for HMI design in robotics.

**For Servo Motor Control (50 Hz, 1 ms–2 ms pulse):**

$$D_{servo} = \frac{t_{pulse}}{T} = \frac{t_{pulse}}{20 \text{ ms}}$$

| Servo Angle | $t_{pulse}$ | Duty Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| 0° (full left) | 1.0 ms | 5.0 % |
| 90° (center) | 1.5 ms | 7.5 % |
| 180° (full right) | 2.0 ms | 10.0 % |

---

### 3.5 Procedure E — I²C Sensor Interfacing (HC-SR04 Ultrasonic via I²C, or MPU6050 IMU)

**Wiring (MPU6050 example):**
- VCC → 3.3 V (Pin 1)  ⚠ **Never 5 V** — the MPU6050's SDA/SCL are NOT 5 V tolerant
- GND → GND (Pin 6)
- SDA → GPIO 2 (Pin 3)
- SCL → GPIO 3 (Pin 5)

**Scan for the device:**

```bash
i2cdetect -y 1
# Expected output includes: 0x68 (default AD0 = GND)
```

**Python Read (raw accelerometer data):**

```python
import smbus2, time
bus = smbus2.SMBus(1)
MPU6050_ADDR = 0x68
PWR_MGMT_1   = 0x6B
ACCEL_XOUT_H = 0x3B

# Wake the sensor (clear sleep bit)
bus.write_byte_data(MPU6050_ADDR, PWR_MGMT_1, 0)

def read_word(reg):
    high = bus.read_byte_data(MPU6050_ADDR, reg)
    low  = bus.read_byte_data(MPU6050_ADDR, reg + 1)
    val  = (high << 8) + low
    return val - 65536 if val >= 32768 else val

while True:
    ax = read_word(ACCEL_XOUT_H)   / 16384.0   # g
    ay = read_word(ACCEL_XOUT_H + 2)/ 16384.0  # g
    az = read_word(ACCEL_XOUT_H + 4)/ 16384.0  # g
    print(f"ax={ax:+.2f}g  ay={ay:+.2f}g  az={az:+.2f}g")
    time.sleep(0.1)
```

**Why divide by 16384?** The MPU6050's accelerometer has a default full-scale range of **±2 g** and a 16-bit signed output (–32768 to +32767), so each LSB represents $\frac{4g}{65536} = \frac{1}{16384}$ g per LSB.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which I/O Method Should I Use?"

| Requirement | Recommended Method | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Just on/off an LED | Digital GPIO OUTPUT | Simplest, no CPU overhead |
| Read a push-button | Digital GPIO INPUT + interrupt | No polling; CPU is free |
| Dim an LED / control DC motor speed | Hardware PWM (BCM 12/13/18/19) | Timer hardware; jitter-free |
| Read a potentiometer (analog) | External ADC (MCP3008) via **SPI** | Pi has NO native ADC |
| Read a temperature sensor | I²C (BMP280) or 1-Wire (DS18B20) | Built-in, no extra hardware |
| Serial debug output | UART (TX = GPIO14) | Text-based, easy to read |
| Drive a 12 V / 2 A DC motor | GPIO → Relay or H-bridge (L298N) | Pi cannot supply motor current |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*List the key differences between the BOARD and BCM GPIO numbering schemes. Which one is recommended for production code and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **BOARD numbering** refers to the physical position of the pin on the 40-pin header (1 to 40). It is **stable across Raspberry Pi revisions** but does not match the SoC's internal channel map.
- **BCM numbering** refers to the Broadcom SoC's internal GPIO channel numbers (e.g., GPIO17). It is **not stable** — older Pi revisions used different channel-to-pin mappings.
- **Recommendation:** Use **BCM numbering** when writing production code because it makes the pin's *function* explicit and aligns with hardware datasheets (e.g., for I²C, SPI, PWM).
- *(+1 mark for mentioning the WiringPi deprecated virtual scheme.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the purpose of a pull-up resistor on a GPIO input pin? What happens if it is omitted when reading a mechanical push-button?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- A **pull-up resistor** ties the input pin to a defined logic level (3.3 V) when no active driver is connected. This prevents the pin from "floating" in an undefined voltage range (typically 1.0–2.0 V).
- For a push-button connected between the GPIO and GND: when the button is open, the pull-up forces the pin HIGH; when pressed, the pin is shorted to GND and reads LOW.
- If the pull-up is omitted, the floating input is highly susceptible to **electromagnetic noise**, causing **false triggering** — the Pi will detect "phantom" button presses due to capacitive coupling from nearby wires or hand movement.
- The Pi's internal pull-ups (≈ 50 kΩ) are weak; for high-noise environments, an external **4.7 kΩ – 10 kΩ** pull-up is preferred.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**
*`[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]`*

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the major functional blocks of a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B and the role of the 40-pin GPIO header. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a complete Python program using the `RPi.GPIO` library to interface an LED (on GPIO 18) and a push-button (on GPIO 23). The LED should remain OFF until the button is pressed for the first time, after which it should toggle (ON/OFF) on every subsequent press. Use a software debounce of 250 ms. *(7 marks)*

---

**(a) Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to **Section 4.1** in this document — the 5 V USB-C input feeds the **PMIC**, which generates 3.3 V, 1.8 V, 1.2 V, and 0.8 V rails for the **SoC (BCM2711)**, **LPDDR4 RAM**, and peripherals.)

**Major functional blocks (1 mark each):**

1. **Power Input & PMIC** — Accepts 5 V / 3 A via USB-C; PMIC generates all internal voltage rails and provides under-voltage protection.
2. **SoC (BCM2711)** — Quad-core ARM Cortex-A72 CPU @ 1.8 GHz + VideoCore VI GPU + shared L2 cache. This is the "brain" executing the OS and Python code.
3. **LPDDR4 RAM** — 4 GB of low-power DDR4 memory soldered in a Package-on-Package (PoP) configuration.
4. **microSD Card** — Primary non-volatile storage containing the bootloader, OS, libraries, and user scripts.
5. **Peripherals** — 2× micro-HDMI, 2× USB 3.0, 2× USB 2.0, Gigabit Ethernet, Wi-Fi 5, Bluetooth 5.0, CSI camera, DSI display.
6. **40-pin GPIO Header** — Provides 26 user-accessible GPIO pins, 3.3 V, 5 V, and multiple GND pins. Multiplexed to support **I²C, SPI, UART, PWM, PCM, 1-Wire**. *(1 mark reserved for the GPIO header explanation — describing multiplexing, pull-up/down, 3.3 V logic, 16 mA max current.)*

**GPIO Header Role (1 mark):** Acts as the physical interface between the SoC's internal logic and external hardware. Each pin can be software-configured as input, output, or alternate function, enabling the Pi to sense the physical world (sensors, buttons) and act upon it (LEDs, motors, relays).

---

**(b) Model Solution (7 marks):**

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

# --- Pin configuration (BCM numbering) ---
LED_PIN = 18     # Physical pin 12 — hardware PWM capable (not used here)
BTN_PIN = 23     # Physical pin 16

# --- Global state ---
first_press  = True
led_state    = False

def button_callback(channel):
    """ISR: triggered on every button press (FALLING edge)."""
    global first_press, led_state
    if first_press:
        led_state    = True            # first press → turn LED ON
        first_press  = False
    else:
        led_state = not led_state      # subsequent presses → toggle
    GPIO.output(LED_PIN, led_state)
    print(f"Button pressed → LED {'ON' if led_state else 'OFF'}")

# --- Setup ---
GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
GPIO.setwarnings(False)
GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT, initial=GPIO.LOW)
GPIO.setup(BTN_PIN, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP)

# --- Interrupt registration with 250 ms software debounce ---
GPIO.add_event_detect(BTN_PIN, GPIO.FALLING,
                      callback=button_callback,
                      bouncetime=250)

try:
    print("Waiting for first button press...")
    while True:
        time.sleep(0.1)
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    pass
finally:
    GPIO.cleanup()
    print("Cleanup complete. Exiting.")
```

**Key Evaluation Points (mark distribution):**
- Correct import & mode setup — **1 mark**
- Pin configuration (LED as OUTPUT, BUTTON as INPUT with PUD_UP) — **1 mark**
- Global state variables initialized correctly — **1 mark**
- ISR with `first_press` logic — **2 marks**
- Debounce of 250 ms and proper `GPIO.cleanup()` in `finally` — **2 marks**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
*`[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]`*

**(a)** Explain the principle of **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)**. Derive the relationship between the duty cycle $D$ and the average output voltage $V_{avg}$ for a Raspberry Pi GPIO pin driving an LED through a 220 Ω resistor. If the LED has a forward voltage $V_F = 2.0 \text{ V}$ and operates at $D = 60\%$, calculate the average current through the LED. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Interface a **DC motor** via an **L298N H-bridge** to the Raspberry Pi. Show the wiring diagram (textual), identify the GPIO pins for IN1, IN2, and ENA (enable/PWM), and write a Python program to vary the motor speed from 0 to 100 % and reverse its direction. *(7 marks)*

---

**(a) Model Solution (7 marks):**

**PWM Definition (2 marks):**
PWM is a modulation technique that varies the **average power** delivered to a load by switching a digital signal between HIGH and LOW at a fixed frequency. The ratio of ON-time to total period is called the **duty cycle**.

**Derivation of $V_{avg}$ (3 marks):**

The PWM signal has:
- ON duration: $t_{on}$
- OFF duration: $t_{off}$
- Period: $T = t_{on} + t_{off}$
- Frequency: $f = 1/T$
- Duty cycle: $D = t_{on} / T$

The instantaneous output voltage is:

$$v(t) = \begin{cases} V_{HIGH} = 3.3 \text{ V} & \text{if } 0 \leq t \leq t_{on} \\ 0 \text{ V} & \text{if } t_{on} < t \leq T \end{cases}$$

The average over one period is:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v(t)\,dt = \frac{1}{T} \left[ V_{HIGH} \cdot t_{on} + 0 \cdot t_{off} \right]$$

$$\boxed{V_{avg} = V_{HIGH} \cdot D = 3.3 \times D \text{ volts}}$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

For $D = 60\% = 0.6$:

$$V_{avg} = 3.3 \times 0.6 = 1.98 \text{ V}$$

Voltage across the current-limiting resistor:

$$V_R = V_{avg} - V_F = 1.98 - 2.0 = -0.02 \text{ V}$$

⚠ This **negative value** indicates the LED will not conduct at 60 % duty cycle when the supply is only 3.3 V (because $V_F = 2.0$ V exceeds the average voltage). This is a classic viva trap — the LED effectively only conducts during the brief ON pulse above 2.0 V.

**Corrected analytical approach (used by the examiner):**

The **instantaneous** current when the LED is ON:

$$I_{peak} = \frac{V_{HIGH} - V_F}{R} = \frac{3.3 - 2.0}{220} = 5.91 \text{ mA}$$

The **average** current (assuming the LED only conducts when $V > V_F$):

$$I_{avg} = I_{peak} \times D = 5.91 \text{ mA} \times 0.6 = \boxed{3.55 \text{ mA}}$$

---

**(b) Model Solution (7 marks):**

**L298N Wiring Table (2 marks):**

| L298N Pin | Connect To | Function |
|---|---|---|
| IN1 | GPIO 17 (Pin 11) | Direction bit 1 |
| IN2 | GPIO 27 (Pin 13) | Direction bit 2 |
| ENA | GPIO 18 (Pin 12) | PWM speed control (HW PWM) |
| OUT1, OUT2 | DC motor terminals | Motor output |
| 12V | External 7-12 V battery | Motor power (NOT from Pi) |
| GND | Pi GND + battery GND | Common ground (CRITICAL) |
| 5V (logic) | Optional 5V output | Can power Pi if battery > 7V |

**Direction Truth Table (1 mark):**

| IN1 | IN2 | Motor State |
|---|---|---|
| LOW | LOW | Stop (coast) |
| HIGH | LOW | Forward |
| LOW | HIGH | Reverse |
| HIGH | HIGH | Brake (fast stop) |

**Python Program (4 marks):**

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

IN1, IN2, ENA = 17, 27, 18
PWM_FREQ       = 1000     # 1 kHz

GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
GPIO.setwarnings(False)
for pin in (IN1, IN2):
    GPIO.setup(pin, GPIO.OUT, initial=GPIO.LOW)
GPIO.setup(ENA, GPIO.OUT)
pwm = GPIO.PWM(ENA, PWM_FREQ)
pwm.start(0)

def set_motor(direction, speed_percent):
    """direction: 'FWD', 'REV', 'STOP', 'BRAKE'"""
    if direction == 'FWD':
        GPIO.output(IN1, GPIO.HIGH); GPIO.output(IN2, GPIO.LOW)
    elif direction == 'REV':
        GPIO.output(IN1, GPIO.LOW);  GPIO.output(IN2, GPIO.HIGH)
    elif direction == 'BRAKE':
        GPIO.output(IN1, GPIO.HIGH); GPIO.output(IN2, GPIO.HIGH)
    else:                           # STOP (coast)
        GPIO.output(IN1, GPIO.LOW);  GPIO.output(IN2, GPIO.LOW)
    pwm.ChangeDutyCycle(speed_percent)

try:
    print("Ramping forward 0 -> 100%")
    for s in range(0, 101, 10):
        set_motor('FWD', s)
        time.sleep(0.3)
    time.sleep(1)
    print("Braking")
    set_motor('BRAKE', 0)
    time.sleep(1)
    print("Ramping reverse 0 -> 100%")
    for s in range(0, 101, 10):
        set_motor('REV', s)
        time.sleep(0.3)
    set_motor('STOP', 0)
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    pass
finally:
    pwm.stop()
    GPIO.cleanup()
```

**Mark allocation note:** 2 marks for wiring, 1 for direction table, 4 for code (1: pin setup, 1: `set_motor()` logic, 1: ramp loop, 1: cleanup).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting `GPIO.cleanup()` in `finally`** — leaves pins locked in OUTPUT state; subsequent runs throw a "channel already in use" warning (-1 mark).
> 2. **Confusing BOARD vs BCM pin numbers** — Pin 11 (BOARD) is **GPIO 17 (BCM)**, **not** "pin 17". Read the question twice! (-2 marks typical).
> 3. **Not tying all grounds together** — the L298N's GND must be common with the Pi's GND, otherwise the logic signals are referenced to a floating ground and the motor will not respond. (-1 mark for circuit design questions).
> 4. **Missing `time.sleep()` inside event callback** — long ISRs can cause "callback queue overflow" warnings; the debounce + main-thread idle pattern is the correct KTU-expected solution.
> 5. **Confusing software PWM with hardware PWM** — only **BCM 12, 13, 18, 19** support hardware PWM. Using software PWM for servos causes jitter. (–1 mark on viva).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **Raspberry Pi = ARM SoC SBC**, not a microcontroller — it runs a full Linux OS.
- 🔑 **GPIO logic level is 3.3 V**, **max 16 mA per pin**, **max 50 mA total** from all pins.
- 🔑 **Two numbering schemes exist** — BOARD (physical) and BCM (chip). Default your script with `GPIO.setmode()`.
- 🔑 **Always use `GPIO.cleanup()`** in a `finally` block to release pins.
- 🔑 **Floating inputs are dangerous** — use internal `PUD_UP`/`PUD_DOWN` for buttons and switches.
- 🔑 **Debounce mechanical inputs** with `bouncetime=200` (or higher) to prevent multiple triggers.
- 🔑 **PWM is the only way to simulate analog output** on the Pi. $V_{avg} = D \times 3.3$ V.
- 🔑 **Hardware PWM** is on **BCM 12, 13, 18, 19** only. For other pins, software PWM is used (with jitter).
- 🔑 **Servo control** uses **50 Hz** PWM with pulse widths between **1.0 ms (0°)** and **2.0 ms (180°)**.
- 🔑 **I²C** uses SDA = GPIO 2, SCL = GPIO 3. **SPI** uses GPIO 9, 10, 11, 8 (or 7). **UART** uses GPIO 14 (TX), 15 (RX).
- 🔑 **The Pi has no native ADC** — use an external **MCP3008 (10-bit SPI)** for analog sensors.
- 🔑 **Never power motors directly from GPIO** — always use a driver (L298N, DRV8833, or relay) with a separate power supply and a common ground.
- 🔑 **Verify the OS enable flags** for I²C, SPI, 1-Wire, and UART in `sudo raspi-config` → Interface Options.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mobile Robot Assembly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAL607 - ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Mobile Robot Assembly

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Mobile Robot Assembly** in the context of the KTU 2024 Robotics Laboratory (PCCAL607) refers to the systematic, step-by-step integration of mechanical, electrical, electronic, and sensor subsystems into a functional autonomous wheeled platform capable of executing locomotion, perception, and basic decision-making tasks.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCCAL607 – Robotics Lab**, Experiment 2 of Module 2 focuses on the **hands-on fabrication, mechanical coupling, electrical interfacing, and structural integration** of a differential-drive (or Ackermann-steered) mobile robot. The experiment tests Course Outcomes **CO2** (*Apply kinematic and dynamic principles to robotic systems*) and **CO5** (*Integrate sensors, actuators, and controllers to build functional robotic prototypes*).

**Key Subsystems Assembled:**
1. **Mechanical Chassis** – Acrylic, aluminum, or 3D-printed base plate.
2. **Locomotion Module** – DC geared motors with encoders, wheels, casters.
3. **Power Module** – Li-ion / Ni-MH battery pack with voltage regulation.
4. **Control Module** – Arduino Uno / Mega, Raspberry Pi, or STM32 board.
5. **Sensor Suite** – Ultrasonic (HC-SR04), IR proximity, IMU (MPU6050), line sensors.
6. **Driver Module** – L298N or BTS7960 motor driver.
7. **Communication Module** – Bluetooth HC-05 / Wi-Fi ESP8266.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Robot as a Human Body

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Think of a mobile robot like a **tiny wheeled creature with a brain, muscles, senses, and a skeleton**.

| Robotic Subsystem | Human Body Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Microcontroller (Arduino) | Brain | Decision-making and logic execution |
| DC Motors + Wheels | Legs and muscles | Physical movement and force generation |
| Battery Pack | Heart and lungs | Energy supply to all organs |
| Ultrasonic / IR Sensors | Eyes and ears | Perception of surroundings |
| Chassis + Frame | Skeleton | Structural support and rigidity |
| Motor Driver | Spinal cord | Signal amplification and routing |
| Encoder | Proprioception | Feedback on joint/wheel rotation |

When we "assemble" a mobile robot, we are essentially **wiring these organs together** so that the brain (microcontroller) can command the muscles (motors) using information from the senses (sensors), all powered by the heart (battery) and supported by the skeleton (chassis).

### 1.3 Standard Specifications (KTU Lab Reference Values)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Lab Mobile Robot Specifications** (typical values used in KTU-affiliated robotics labs):
> - **Operating Voltage:** 7.4 V to 12 V DC
> - **Motor Type:** 12 V DC Geared Motor, **100–300 RPM**, **torque 1.5–5 kg·cm**
> - **Wheel Diameter:** 65 mm to 100 mm
> - **Chassis Dimensions:** 200 mm × 150 mm × 60 mm
> - **Payload Capacity:** 1 kg to 3 kg
> - **Controller Clock Speed:** 16 MHz (Arduino Uno)
> - **Sensor Range (Ultrasonic):** 2 cm to 400 cm
> - **Battery Capacity:** 2200 mAh to 5200 mAh Li-ion

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of Mobile Robot Assembly

A mobile robot assembly is not a random collection of parts — it follows a **dependency hierarchy** where each subsystem relies on the previous one. The KTU lab manual mandates the following assembly sequence:

**Step 1 — Chassis Preparation**
- Mount the base plate (acrylic/aluminum) on a flat, ESD-safe surface.
- Identify mounting holes for motors, castor wheel, and electronics.
- Drill or use pre-cut slots for cable routing.

**Step 2 — Locomotion Module Mounting**
- Couple DC geared motors to the chassis using **L-brackets** or **motor clamps**.
- Attach wheels to motor shafts ensuring proper **keyway alignment**.
- Mount a free-rolling **castor wheel** at the front (or rear) for three-point stability.

**Step 3 — Power Subsystem Installation**
- Connect battery pack to a **common power rail** (GND bus + V+ bus).
- Use a **buck converter (LM2596)** to step down battery voltage to 5 V/3.3 V for logic circuits.
- Install a **SPST power switch** and **fuse (2 A–5 A)** for safety.

**Step 4 — Motor Driver Integration**
- Connect motor terminals to L298N outputs (OUT1, OUT2, OUT3, OUT4).
- Power the driver's VCC (12 V) from the battery, and 5 V logic from the buck converter.
- Connect Arduino PWM pins to ENA, ENB (enable) and IN1–IN4 (direction).

**Step 5 — Sensor Mounting**
