- Ultrasonic sensor (HC-SR04) → front bumper, 15°–30° downward tilt.
- IR sensors → left, right, and rear for obstacle avoidance.
- IMU (MPU6050) → center of chassis on a flat, vibration-isolated surface.

**Step 6 — Controller Mounting**
- Secure Arduino/Raspberry Pi on **standoffs** to avoid short circuits.
- Use **cable ties and spiral wrap** for harness management.

**Step 7 — Communication & Telemetry**
- HC-05 Bluetooth module for wireless serial commands.
- Optional: ESP8266 for IoT-based teleoperation.

**Step 8 — Firmware Upload & Calibration**
- Upload basic obstacle-avoidance firmware.
- Calibrate encoder counts per wheel revolution (PPR × gear ratio).
- Verify wheel alignment and zero-turn behavior.

### 2.2 Why This Order Matters (The "Why" Behind Each Step)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Chassis first** because all other components need a rigid reference frame.
> - **Motors before electronics** because wiring routes depend on motor placement.
> - **Power rail before drivers** because drivers cannot operate without stable supply.
> - **Sensors last** because sensor placement depends on chassis geometry and cable lengths.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **most-tested derivations** in KTU 2024 ESE and CE for the mobile robotics module. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = \omega \cdot r$ | Linear wheel velocity from angular velocity and wheel radius | m/s |
| 2 | $\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity from motor RPM (N) | rad/s |
| 3 | $v_{robot} = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$ | Forward velocity of differential-drive robot | m/s |
| 4 | $\omega_{robot} = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}$ | Angular velocity of robot about ICC | rad/s |
| 5 | $R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$ | Radius of curvature (Instantaneous Center of Curvature) | m |
| 6 | $T_{load} = F_{total} \cdot r_{wheel}$ | Load torque required at wheel | N·m |
| 7 | $F_{total} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g$ | Total traction force (rolling friction approximation) | N |
| 8 | $T_{motor} = \frac{T_{load}}{G \cdot \eta}$ | Required motor torque after gear reduction | N·m |
| 9 | $P_{motor} = T_{motor} \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power required per motor | W |
| 10 | $I_{total} = N \cdot I_{motor} + I_{logic}$ | Total current draw from battery | A |
| 11 | $t_{battery} = \frac{C_{batt}}{I_{total}}$ | Theoretical battery life (hours) | h |
| 12 | $d_{pulse} = \frac{\pi \cdot d_{wheel}}{PPR \cdot G}$ | Distance per encoder pulse | m/pulse |
| 13 | $\theta_{turn} = \frac{L \cdot \phi}{r_{wheel}}$ | Wheel rotation angle for robot turn (rad) | rad |

**Where:**
- $L$ = wheelbase (distance between two drive wheels)
- $G$ = gear ratio of geared motor
- $\eta$ = gear efficiency (typically 0.6 to 0.85)
- $\mu$ = coefficient of rolling friction (~0.02 for rubber on tile)
- $PPR$ = encoder pulses per revolution
- $C_{batt}$ = battery capacity in mAh
- $\phi$ = desired robot heading change (radians)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Mobile robot assembly skills are foundational to:
> - **Autonomous Ground Vehicles (AGVs)** in warehouses (Amazon Kiva robots).
> - **Last-mile delivery robots** (Starship Technologies, Nuro).
> - **Disaster response robots** (DARPA Subterranean Challenge finalists).
> - **Agricultural scouting bots** (John Deere autonomous tractors).
> - **Service robots** in hospitals and hotels.
>
> The KTU lab exposes students to the **exact subsystem integration** used in these industrial platforms.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Motor Torque for a Given Payload

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):**
A differential-drive mobile robot must carry a total mass $m = 3$ kg, including chassis, batteries, and payload. The wheel radius is $r = 0.0325$ m (65 mm diameter), the wheelbase $L = 0.18$ m, and the gear ratio $G = 30$ with efficiency $\eta = 0.75$. The rolling friction coefficient $\mu = 0.02$. Determine the **minimum motor torque** required to drive the robot on a flat surface.

**Step 1 — Compute total traction force:**

$$F_{total} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g$$

$$F_{total} = 0.02 \cdot 3 \cdot 9.81$$

$$F_{total} = 0.5886 \text{ N}$$

*Logic:* The traction force equals the rolling friction force the wheels must overcome. With $\mu = 0.02$, the effective drag is only 2% of the weight — this is a typical value for smooth rubber tires on indoor tile.

**Step 2 — Compute load torque at the wheel:**

$$T_{load} = F_{total} \cdot r_{wheel}$$

$$T_{load} = 0.5886 \cdot 0.0325$$

$$T_{load} = 0.01913 \text{ N·m}$$

*Logic:* Torque = Force × lever arm. The wheel radius is the lever through which traction force is transferred to the ground.

**Step 3 — Distribute load across two drive wheels:**

$$T_{load,per wheel} = \frac{T_{load}}{2} = \frac{0.01913}{2} = 0.00957 \text{ N·m}$$

*Logic:* The differential drive shares the load equally between two motors.

**Step 4 — Apply gear reduction to find motor torque:**

$$T_{motor} = \frac{T_{load,per wheel}}{G \cdot \eta}$$

$$T_{motor} = \frac{0.00957}{30 \cdot 0.75}$$

$$T_{motor} = \frac{0.00957}{22.5}$$

$$T_{motor} = 4.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N·m} = 0.425 \text{ mN·m}$$

*Logic:* The gearbox multiplies the small motor torque by factor $G \cdot \eta$. So the motor itself only needs to produce a tiny fraction of the wheel torque.

**Step 5 — Safety factor and practical selection:**

$$T_{motor,design} = T_{motor} \cdot SF, \quad SF = 3 \text{ to } 5$$

$$T_{motor,design} = 0.425 \cdot 4 = 1.7 \text{ mN·m}$$

A standard 12 V DC geared motor with rated torque of **1.5 kg·cm (≈147 mN·m)** is more than sufficient — explaining why small hobby motors comfortably drive 3 kg robots.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Robot Linear Velocity from Motor RPM

**Problem Statement:**
A DC motor rated at **150 RPM** is coupled to a wheel of diameter **65 mm** through a **30:1 gearbox**. Find the linear velocity of the mobile robot.

**Step 1 — Find motor shaft angular velocity:**

$$N_{motor} = 150 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\omega_{motor} = \frac{2 \pi N_{motor}}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \cdot 150}{60} = 5\pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 15.708 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply gear ratio to get wheel angular velocity:**

$$\omega_{wheel} = \frac{\omega_{motor}}{G} = \frac{15.708}{30} = 0.5236 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute linear velocity:**

$$r_{wheel} = \frac{0.065}{2} = 0.0325 \text{ m}$$

$$v = \omega_{wheel} \cdot r_{wheel}$$

$$v = 0.5236 \cdot 0.0325 = 0.01702 \text{ m/s} \approx 1.7 \text{ cm/s}$$

*Logic:* The gearbox trades speed for torque. A 150 RPM motor becomes a 5 RPM wheel — slow but powerful, ideal for indoor mobile robots.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Battery Life Estimation

**Problem Statement:**
A mobile robot uses **2 DC motors** drawing **200 mA each at full load**, an **Arduino Uno** drawing **50 mA**, **3 ultrasonic sensors** at **15 mA each**, and a **Bluetooth module** at **40 mA**. The battery is a **3-cell 18650 Li-ion pack** rated at **2200 mAh, 11.1 V**. Estimate the **operating time**.

**Step 1 — Compute total current draw:**

$$I_{total} = N \cdot I_{motor} + I_{logic}$$

$$I_{total} = 2 \cdot 200 + (50 + 3 \cdot 15 + 40)$$

$$I_{total} = 400 + 135 = 535 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2 — Apply battery life formula:**

$$t_{battery} = \frac{C_{batt}}{I_{total}} = \frac{2200}{535} = 4.11 \text{ h}$$

*Logic:* In practice, Li-ion batteries should not be discharged below 20%, so **safe operating time ≈ 3.3 hours**.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Encoder Resolution and Odometry

**Problem Statement:**
An encoder on a DC geared motor produces **20 PPR (pulses per revolution)** at the motor shaft, with a **30:1 gearbox** and a wheel of diameter **65 mm**. Calculate the **distance traveled per encoder pulse**.

**Step 1 — Find pulses per wheel revolution:**

$$PPR_{wheel} = PPR_{motor} \cdot G = 20 \cdot 30 = 600 \text{ pulses/rev}$$

**Step 2 — Compute wheel circumference:**

$$C_{wheel} = \pi \cdot d = \pi \cdot 0.065 = 0.2042 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute distance per pulse:**

$$d_{pulse} = \frac{C_{wheel}}{PPR_{wheel}} = \frac{0.2042}{600} = 3.40 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/pulse} = 0.34 \text{ mm/pulse}$$

*Logic:* The robot's position can be tracked by counting pulses. For 1 meter of travel, the robot must count approximately **2941 pulses**.

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Typical Lab Value | Engineering Insight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wheelbase | $L$ | Measured | 0.15 – 0.25 m | Smaller $L$ → tighter turns |
| Wheel radius | $r$ | Measured | 0.0325 – 0.05 m | Larger $r$ → higher speed, lower torque |
| Gear ratio | $G$ | Specified | 20:1 – 100:1 | Trade-off between speed and torque |
| Motor RPM | $N$ | Datasheet | 100 – 300 RPM | After gearing, wheel is 3–15 RPM |
| Robot velocity | $v$ | $\omega r$ | 0.01 – 0.5 m/s | Indoor bots are slow for safety |
| Battery life | $t$ | $C / I$ | 1 – 5 hours | Depends on duty cycle |
| Encoder resolution | $d_{pulse}$ | $\pi d / (PPR \cdot G)$ | 0.1 – 1 mm/pulse | Determines odometry accuracy |
| Turning radius | $R$ | $L/2 \cdot (v_R + v_L)/(v_R - v_L)$ | 0 (zero turn) to $\infty$ | $v_R = -v_L$ → pure rotation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mobile Robot Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Battery Pack 11.1V Li-ion] --> B[Buck Converter LM2596]
    A --> C[L298N Motor Driver]
    B --> D[Arduino Uno MCU 16MHz]
    C --> D
    D --> E[HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Sensor]
    D --> F[IR Proximity Sensor Array]
    D --> G[MPU6050 IMU Module]
    D --> H[HC-05 Bluetooth Module]
    D --> I[PWM Signal Output]
    I --> C
    C --> J[Left DC Geared Motor]
    C --> K[Right DC Geared Motor]
    J --> L[Left Wheel Encoder]
    K --> M[Right Wheel Encoder]
    L --> D
    M --> D
    H --> N[Remote Control / Smartphone]
```

### 4.2 Mechanical Assembly Layer (Top-Down View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Front Bumper / Ultrasonic Mount] --> F2[Front Cast or Caster Wheel]
    F2 --> F3[Acrylic Chassis Plate 200x150mm]
    F3 --> F4[Left DC Motor with L-Bracket]
    F3 --> F5[Right DC Motor with L-Bracket]
    F4 --> F6[Left Wheel 65mm]
    F5 --> F7[Right Wheel 65mm]
    F3 --> F8[Central Electronics Bay]
    F8 --> F9[Arduino Uno on Standoffs]
    F8 --> F10[L298N Motor Driver]
    F8 --> F11[Li-ion Battery Pack]
    F3 --> F12[Rear IR Sensor Mount]
```

### 4.3 Power Distribution and Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Battery 11.1V] --> P2{Switch and Fuse}
    P2 --> P3[Raw 11.1V Bus]
    P2 --> P4[Buck Converter to 5V]
    P3 --> P5[L298N Motor Driver VCC]
    P4 --> P6[Arduino 5V Rail]
    P4 --> P7[Sensor Power 5V]
    P5 --> P8[Left Motor]
    P5 --> P9[Right Motor]
    P6 --> P10[Arduino Logic]
    P7 --> P11[Ultrasonic Trigger and Echo]
    P7 --> P12[IR Sensors]
    P7 --> P13[IMU VCC]
    P10 --> P14[PWM to L298N IN Pins]
    P11 --> P10
    P12 --> P10
    P13 --> P10
```

### 4.4 Assembly Workflow Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Step 1 Unpack and Identify Components] --> S2[Step 2 Mount Motors on Chassis]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Attach Wheels and Castors]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Install Battery and Switch]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Mount Arduino and Motor Driver]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Wire Motor Driver to Motors and Arduino]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Mount and Wire Sensors]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Install Communication Module]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Upload Test Firmware Blink]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10 Calibrate Motors and Encoders]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11 Run Obstacle Avoidance Test]
    S11 --> S12{Test Passed?}
    S12 -->|Yes| S13[Robot Ready for Module 3 Experiments]
    S12 -->|No| S14[Debug Wiring and Code]
    S14 --> S9
```

### 4.5 Differential Drive Kinematic Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[Left Wheel velocity vL] --> K2[Sum and Difference Block]
    K3[Right Wheel velocity vR] --> K2
    K2 --> K4[v_robot = vR+vL over 2]
    K2 --> K5[omega_robot = vR-vL over L]
    K4 --> K6[Forward Motion Output]
    K5 --> K7[Rotational Motion Output]
    K6 --> K8[Pose Update x,y,theta]
    K7 --> K8
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**List the major subsystems of a differential-drive mobile robot and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A differential-drive mobile robot consists of the following subsystems:

1. **Mechanical Chassis** — Provides structural support and rigidity to mount all components. Typically acrylic, aluminum, or 3D-printed PLA.
2. **Locomotion Module** — Two independently driven DC geared motors coupled to wheels, providing differential steering.
3. **Power Module** — Battery pack (commonly 11.1 V Li-ion) with buck converter for logic-level voltages.
4. **Control Module** — Microcontroller (Arduino/Raspberry Pi/STM32) executing the motion and perception algorithms.
5. **Sensor Suite** — Ultrasonic, IR, IMU, and encoders enabling environmental awareness and odometry.
6. **Driver Module** — L298N H-bridge motor driver that amplifies low-current logic signals to high-current motor power.
7. **Communication Module** — Bluetooth or Wi-Fi for remote teleoperation and data logging.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is the purpose of a motor driver (L298N) in a mobile robot? Why can't the Arduino drive motors directly?**

**Model Answer:**

A motor driver such as the **L298N** acts as a **current amplifier and signal isolator** between the low-power microcontroller and the high-power DC motor.

The Arduino Uno GPIO pins can source/sink only about **40 mA** per pin and a maximum of **200 mA** in total, while a typical DC geared motor draws **200 mA to 2 A** under load. Connecting the motor directly would:
- Burn out the Arduino's ATmega328P pin driver transistors.
- Cause severe voltage drops and reset the controller.
- Provide no **back-EMF protection**.

The L298N accepts 5 V logic signals from the Arduino and switches up to **46 V, 2 A continuous** motor current per channel, and also provides **flyback diodes** to protect against inductive kickback. It thus acts as a safe, high-power interface between logic and actuation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Always **draw the block diagram** before explaining — marks are reserved for figures.
> - In numerical problems, **state given data explicitly** and **box the final answer**.
> - Mention **units** for every numerical result. A numerical answer without units loses 1 mark.
> - Do not confuse **wheelbase $L$** with **wheel radius $r$** — this is a very common error.
> - Always apply a **safety factor (SF = 3 to 5)** when selecting a motor in design problems.

---

#### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, describe the step-by-step assembly procedure of a differential-drive mobile robot. List the components used at each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot with wheel diameter **100 mm**, wheelbase **200 mm**, and gear ratio **20:1** uses two DC motors each rated at **200 RPM**. Compute the **linear velocity**, **angular velocity**, and **radius of curvature** when the left wheel rotates at 5 rad/s and the right wheel at 8 rad/s. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Assembly Procedure:**

The assembly follows a **seven-stage workflow** (refer to the Mermaid workflow diagram in Section 4.4).

1. **Mechanical Layer** — Mount the two DC geared motors on L-brackets fixed to the chassis. Install the wheels and a front castor wheel.
2. **Power Layer** — Mount the Li-ion battery pack, SPST switch, and fuse. Connect the buck converter to derive 5 V.
3. **Driver Layer** — Mount the L298N driver. Connect motor terminals to OUT1–OUT4. Power VCC from battery and 5 V logic from buck converter.
4. **Controller Layer** — Mount Arduino Uno on standoffs. Connect IN1–IN4 and ENA, ENB to Arduino digital/PWM pins.
5. **Sensor Layer** — Mount HC-SR04 at the front, IR sensors at left/right, IMU at the center.
6. **Communication Layer** — Mount HC-05 Bluetooth module for serial teleoperation.
7. **Testing Layer** — Upload test sketch, calibrate encoder PPR, and verify forward/reverse/turn behavior.

Each stage is followed by a **continuity and power test** to localize faults.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**

$$d_{wheel} = 100 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow r_{wheel} = 0.05 \text{ m}$$

$$L = 0.20 \text{ m}, \quad G = 20, \quad N_{motor} = 200 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\omega_L = 5 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \omega_R = 8 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 1 — Compute linear wheel velocities:**

$$v_L = \omega_L \cdot r_{wheel} = 5 \cdot 0.05 = 0.25 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_R = \omega_R \cdot r_{wheel} = 8 \cdot 0.05 = 0.40 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute robot linear velocity:**

$$v_{robot} = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{0.40 + 0.25}{2} = 0.325 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute robot angular velocity:**

$$\omega_{robot} = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{0.40 - 0.25}{0.20} = 0.75 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute radius of curvature:**

$$R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L} = \frac{0.20}{2} \cdot \frac{0.65}{0.15} = 0.10 \cdot 4.333 = 0.4333 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check (verify gear ratio info not used directly):**

The motor RPM and gear ratio are **red herrings** here, used to test whether the student can identify relevant data. The actual wheel speeds are given directly as $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$.

**Final Answer (boxed):**

$$v_{robot} = 0.325 \text{ m/s}, \quad \omega_{robot} = 0.75 \text{ rad/s}, \quad R = 0.433 \text{ m}$$

---

#### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **differential drive kinematics** of a mobile robot. Derive the equations for forward velocity, angular velocity, and Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 3 kg mobile robot uses two 12 V DC motors each drawing 1.5 A at full load, an Arduino (50 mA), three ultrasonic sensors (15 mA each), and a Bluetooth module (40 mA). The battery is an 11.1 V, 2200 mAh Li-ion pack. Calculate the **theoretical operating time** and the **safe operating time** (using 80% depth of discharge). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Differential Drive Kinematics:**

Consider a two-wheeled differential-drive robot with wheelbase $L$, wheel radius $r$, and wheel angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$.

**Step 1 — Linear wheel velocities:**

$$v_L = \omega_L \cdot r, \quad v_R = \omega_R \cdot r$$

**Step 2 — Forward (linear) velocity of robot:**

Since the robot is rigid and both wheels are in contact with the ground, the robot's center moves at the average of the two wheel velocities:

$$v_{robot} = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$$

**Step 3 — Angular velocity of robot:**

The difference in wheel velocities causes rotation about the ICC. The rate of heading change is:

$$\omega_{robot} = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

**Step 4 — Radius of Curvature (ICC distance):**

By definition, the ICC lies perpendicular to the wheelbase at distance $R$ from the robot center:

$$R = \frac{v_{robot}}{\omega_{robot}} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$$

**Special Cases:**

- If $v_R = v_L$, then $\omega_{robot} = 0$ and $R \to \infty$ → **straight-line motion**.
- If $v_R = -v_L$, then $v_{robot} = 0$ and $R = 0$ → **pure rotation (zero-radius turn)**.
- If one wheel is stationary, the robot pivots about that wheel.

**Part (b) — Battery Life Calculation:**

**Given Data:**

$$I_{motor} = 1.5 \text{ A}, \quad N_{motors} = 2, \quad I_{arduino} = 0.050 \text{ A}$$

$$I_{ultrasonic} = 0.015 \text{ A}, \quad N_{us} = 3, \quad I_{bt} = 0.040 \text{ A}$$

$$C_{batt} = 2200 \text{ mAh} = 2.2 \text{ Ah}$$

**Step 1 — Total current draw:**

$$I_{total} = 2 \cdot 1.5 + 0.050 + 3 \cdot 0.015 + 0.040$$

$$I_{total} = 3.0 + 0.050 + 0.045 + 0.040 = 3.135 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical operating time:**

$$t_{theoretical} = \frac{C_{batt}}{I_{total}} = \frac{2.2}{3.135} = 0.7018 \text{ h} \approx 42.1 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3 — Safe operating time (80% DoD):**

$$t_{safe} = t_{theoretical} \cdot 0.80 = 0.7018 \cdot 0.80 = 0.5615 \text{ h} \approx 33.7 \text{ minutes}$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**

$$t_{theoretical} \approx 42.1 \text{ minutes}, \quad t_{safe} \approx 33.7 \text{ minutes}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Mobile Robot Assembly**

- [ ] A mobile robot has **7 core subsystems**: Chassis, Locomotion, Power, Control, Driver, Sensors, Communication.
- [ ] The **L298N** is the most commonly used dual H-bridge motor driver in KTU labs.
- [ ] The **Arduino cannot drive motors directly** — GPIO current limit is ~40 mA per pin.
- [ ] **Differential drive** uses two independently driven wheels; the third point is a passive castor.
- [ ] The three kinematic equations are:
  - $v_{robot} = (v_R + v_L) / 2$
  - $\omega_{robot} = (v_R - v_L) / L$
  - $R = (L/2) \cdot (v_R + v_L)/(v_R - v_L)$
- [ ] **Zero-radius turn** occurs when $v_R = -v_L$ (one wheel forward, one reverse).
- [ ] **Straight-line motion** occurs when $v_R = v_L$.
- [ ] **Battery life** is computed as $t = C_{batt} / I_{total}$; apply 80% DoD derating for Li-ion.
- [ ] **Motor torque** is amplified by gear ratio: $T_{load} = T_{motor} \cdot G \cdot \eta$.
- [ ] **Encoder odometry** uses $d_{pulse} = \pi d / (PPR \cdot G)$.
- [ ] Always include a **fuse and SPST switch** on the main power line.
- [ ] **Buck converter** (LM2596) is used to derive 5 V/3.3 V from 11.1 V battery.
- [ ] **Ultrasonic sensor tilt** should be 15°–30° downward to avoid ground echoes.
- [ ] **IMU placement** must be at the **center of mass** on a vibration-isolated plane.
- [ ] Apply a **safety factor of 3–5** when sizing motors in design problems.
- [ ] Always **draw the block diagram first** in KTU exam answers — figures carry weightage.
- [ ] **Wheelbase $L$ ≠ Wheel radius $r$** — this is the most common dimensional confusion.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Networking with Arduino Using BLE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAL607 - ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Networking with Arduino Using BLE

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** is a power-efficient, short-range wireless personal area network (PAN) communication protocol defined under the **IEEE 802.15.1** standard (and subsequently managed by the Bluetooth Special Interest Group – **SIG**). It is designed for applications that require the **continuous transmission of small amounts of data** at low data rates while consuming a fraction of the power consumed by classical Bluetooth.

In the context of the **KTU Robotics Lab (PCCAL607)**, **Networking with Arduino using BLE** refers to the experimental procedure of establishing a **wireless master–slave (or peripheral–central) communication link** between two or more Arduino-compatible microcontroller boards (e.g., Arduino Nano 33 BLE, Arduino Nano 33 IoT, ESP32, or HM-10 module interfaced with Arduino Uno) by leveraging the **Generic Access Profile (GAP)** and **Generic Attribute Profile (GATT)** of the BLE stack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Module Experiment 2 in PCCAL607 tests the student's ability to (i) configure an Arduino board as a BLE **peripheral** (server) and another as a BLE **central** (client), (ii) define a custom BLE service and characteristic, (iii) read/write data wirelessly, and (iv) demonstrate a networked robotic control application such as wireless LED control, sensor telemetry, or motor actuation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are inside a **large, noisy classroom** (the **2.4 GHz ISM band**, shared by Wi-Fi, microwaves, and other devices). You want a student in the far corner to pass you a small **chit of paper** (a few bytes of data: "ON", "23°C", "MOVE"). Yelling across the room wastes energy and disturbs others. Instead, you hire a **tiny, super-efficient courier** named **BLE** who:

- **Sleeps most of the time** (ultra-low power, deep sleep currents in the **µA range**).
- **Wakes up only for a few milliseconds** to deliver your chit (advertising/data events).
- **Knows the exact seat number** of the recipient (**UUID-based addressing** – Service & Characteristic).
- **Returns a signed receipt** confirming delivery (**acknowledgement** and **notification**).

This is precisely how BLE works in an Arduino network – the radio is **asleep 99.9 % of the time**, waking only to transmit short bursts, making it ideal for **battery-operated robots, IoT nodes, and wearable sensors**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Board Exam Favorite):**
> - **Bluetooth Classic (BR/EDR):** Continuous streaming, high throughput (~2–3 Mbps), high power (~1 W peak), suited for audio.
> - **BLE (Bluetooth Smart):** Short bursts, low throughput (~0.27 Mbps – 2 Mbps), ultra-low power (~0.01–0.05 W), suited for sensors and control.

## 1.3 Standard Physical & Protocol Constants

| Parameter | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Frequency Band** | **2.4 GHz ISM** (2.402 – 2.480 GHz) | Globally license-free |
| **Number of RF Channels** | **40 channels** (3 advertising + 37 data) | Channel spacing = **2 MHz** |
| **Advertising Channels** | **37, 38, 39** | Used for device discovery |
| **Modulation** | **GFSK** (Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying) | Index = 0.5 |
| **Symbol Rate** | **1 Msym/s** (LE 1M) or **2 Msym/s** (LE 2M) | Per Bluetooth 5.x |
| **Range** | **10 – 100 m** (line of sight) | Class dependent |
| **Peak Current** | **< 15 mA** during TX | Vs. ~40 mA for Classic |
| **Sleep Current** | **< 1 µA** | Enables coin-cell operation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 BLE Protocol Stack – The Operational Logic

The BLE stack is logically partitioned into two halves by a host-controller interface (**HCI**). Understanding this stack is **mandatory** for KTU viva questions.

### Layer 1: **Controller Layer (Radio + Link Layer)**
1. **Physical Layer (PHY):** Operates in the **2.4 GHz ISM band**, hopping across 40 channels. Uses **GFSK** modulation.
2. **Link Layer (LL):** Defines the **packet structure**, channel selection algorithm, and device roles.
3. **HCI (Host Controller Interface):** A standardized transport (UART/SPI) between the BLE chip and the host MCU.

### Layer 2: **Host Layer (Protocol Stack above HCI)**
4. **L2CAP (Logical Link Control & Adaptation Protocol):** Multiplexes higher-layer protocols, handles **MTU** (Maximum Transmission Unit) segmentation.
5. **ATT (Attribute Protocol):** The **data-handling core**. Exposes data as **Attributes** in a lookup table on the server.
6. **GATT (Generic Attribute Profile):** Defines how ATT attributes are grouped into **Services** and **Characteristics** for the application.
7. **GAP (Generic Access Profile):** Defines **roles, modes, and procedures** (advertising, scanning, connection initiation).
8. **SM (Security Manager):** Handles pairing, bonding, and encryption (LE Secure Connections).
9. **Application Layer:** Your Arduino sketch (`loop()`, `setup()`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two profiles?**
> - **GAP** answers: *"Who am I, who are you, how do we connect?"* → **Roles & Discovery**
> - **GATT** answers: *"Now that we are connected, what data can we exchange and in what format?"* → **Data Transactions**

## 2.2 GAP Roles in an Arduino Network

| Role | Function | Typical Arduino |
|---|---|---|
| **Broadcaster** | Advertises data, no connection | Sensor tag |
| **Observer** | Scans advertisements, no connection | Data logger |
| **Peripheral** (Server) | Advertises, accepts connections, holds data | Arduino Nano 33 BLE (running sensor) |
| **Central** (Client) | Scans, initiates connection, reads/writes data | Arduino Nano 33 BLE (running controller) |

> **In the KTU lab experiment, one Arduino is configured as the Peripheral (e.g., sensor node) and the other as the Central (e.g., robotic controller).**

## 2.3 GATT Architecture: Services & Characteristics

GATT organizes data in a **strictly hierarchical** structure:

```
Profile
   └── Service(s)  (identified by 128-bit UUID)
         └── Characteristic(s)  (identified by 128-bit UUID)
               ├── Value (the actual data, 0–512 bytes)
               ├── Descriptor(s) (e.g., CCCD for notifications)
               └── Properties (Read, Write, Notify, Indicate)
```

### 2.3.1 Standard 128-bit UUID Format

A Universally Unique Identifier (**UUID**) is a **128-bit** number. In BLE, custom UUIDs are typically generated as:

$$\text{UUID} = 128\text{-bit} = 32 \text{ hexadecimal digits}$$

For KTU lab purposes, the **Bluetooth SIG base UUID** `00000000-0000-1000-8000-00805F9B34FB` is used as a prefix, and you only define the first 16 bits (a 16-bit UUID, e.g., `0x180D` for Heart Rate). Internally, the stack pads it to a 128-bit value.

### 2.3.2 Standard Pre-Defined Services (Memorize These)

| 16-bit UUID | Official Service |
|---|---|
| `0x180D` | Heart Rate |
| `0x180A` | Device Information |
| `0x180F` | Battery Service |
| `0x181A` | Environmental Sensing |
| `0xFFE0` | HM-10 / HC-08 default (vendor) |

## 2.4 BLE Connection Procedure – The "Why" & "How"

### **Step 1: Advertising (Peripheral)**
The peripheral transmits **ADV_IND** packets on the three advertising channels (37, 38, 39) at intervals defined by the **Advertising Interval** parameter:

$$T_{adv} \in [20 \text{ ms},\ 10240 \text{ ms}]$$

Default: $T_{adv} = 100 \text{ ms}$ (results in $\sim 16$ advertisements per 1.6 s).

### **Step 2: Scanning (Central)**
The central scans for ADV_IND packets. **Scan Window** $W_s$ and **Scan Interval** $I_s$ are key parameters:

$$W_s \le I_s, \quad I_s \in [2.5 \text{ ms},\ 10240 \text{ ms}]$$

If $W_s = I_s$, the radio **continuously scans** (highest discovery rate, highest power). If $W_s < I_s$, the radio **duty-cycles** (lower power).

### **Step 3: Connection Initiation**
On detecting a target advertisement, the central sends a **CONNECT_IND** packet. The peripheral acknowledges, and a **connection event** is established. The **Connection Interval** is negotiated:

$$T_c \in [7.5 \text{ ms},\ 4000 \text{ ms}], \quad \text{multiples of 1.25 ms}$$

### **Step 4: Data Exchange (GATT Transactions)**
Within each connection event, the master–slave pair can exchange **0, 1, or more packets**, depending on the application data length extension (LE 2M PHY + LE Coded PHY in Bluetooth 5.0+):

$$\text{Throughput} \approx \frac{\text{Payload per event}}{T_c}$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Advertising Interval | $T_{adv}$ | ms | 20 – 10240 |
| 2 | Scan Interval | $I_s$ | ms | 2.5 – 10240 |
| 3 | Scan Window | $W_s$ | ms | $W_s \le I_s$ |
| 4 | Connection Interval | $T_c$ | ms | 7.5 – 4000 |
| 5 | Supervisory Timeout | $T_{sup}$ | ms | 100 – 32000 |
| 6 | Slave Latency | $L$ | events | 0 – 499 |
| 7 | Effective Connection Period | $T_{eff} = T_c \times (1 + L)$ | ms | Slave may skip $L$ events |
| 8 | Channel Spacing | $\Delta f$ | **2 MHz** | 40 channels |
| 9 | Symbol Rate (LE 1M) | $R_s$ | **1 Msym/s** | 1 bit/symbol |
| 10 | Bit Rate (LE 1M) | $R_b$ | **1 Mbps** | — |
| 11 | Bit Rate (LE 2M) | $R_b$ | **2 Mbps** | Bluetooth 5.0+ |
| 12 | Bit Rate (LE Coded) | $R_b$ | **125/500 kbps** | Long range, Bluetooth 5.0+ |
| 13 | Hop Frequency Step | $\Delta f$ | **2 MHz** | Adaptive hopping |
| 14 | Average Current (duty-cycled) | $I_{avg} = I_{tx} \cdot d + I_{sleep} \cdot (1-d)$ | mA | $d$ = duty cycle |
| 15 | Power (TX, 0 dBm) | $P_{tx}$ | **0 dBm = 1 mW** | Configurable |
| 16 | Energy per bit | $E_b = P_{tx} / R_b$ | J/bit | Energy efficiency metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Connection Interval** with **Scan Interval**. The former governs data exchange after connection; the latter governs discovery before connection. Exam questions frequently test this distinction.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Wearable Robotics** | IMU data streaming from exoskeleton to controller |
| **Swarm Robotics** | Inter-robot coordination via BLE mesh |
| **Healthcare** | BLE pulse oximeter, BLE insulin pen |
| **Smart Home** | BLE-controlled lights, locks, thermostats |
| **Industrial IoT** | Wireless sensor networks in factory floors |
| **Assistive Tech** | BLE-controlled wheelchair, prosthetics |
| **Indoor Positioning** | BLE beacons (iBeacon, Eddystone) for robot localization |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: BLE Power Budget vs. Bluetooth Classic

The **single most important derivation** for the KTU exam is the average current consumption of a BLE node, which justifies its "Low Energy" nomenclature.

### **Step 1: Define the duty cycle**

The radio wakes up only during advertising or connection events. Let $t_{on}$ be the active transmission time per period, and $T$ the total period:

$$d = \frac{t_{on}}{T}$$

### **Step 2: Average current**

$$I_{avg} = I_{tx} \cdot d + I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - d)$$

### **Step 3: Numerical evaluation (standard advertising scenario)**

**Assumptions:**
- Peak TX current: $I_{tx} = 15 \text{ mA}$
- Sleep current: $I_{sleep} = 1 \text{ \mu A} = 0.001 \text{ mA}$
- Advertisement duration: $t_{on} = 3 \text{ ms}$ (3 channels × 1 ms each)
- Advertising interval: $T = 100 \text{ ms}$

**Step 3a: Compute the duty cycle:**

$$d = \frac{t_{on}}{T} = \frac{3 \text{ ms}}{100 \text{ ms}} = 0.03 = 3\%$$

**Step 3b: Substitute into the average current equation:**

$$I_{avg} = 15 \text{ mA} \times 0.03 + 0.001 \text{ mA} \times 0.97$$

**Step 3c: Evaluate the first term:**

$$15 \times 0.03 = 0.45 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3d: Evaluate the second term:**

$$0.001 \times 0.97 = 0.00097 \text{ mA} \approx 0.001 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3e: Final sum:**

$$I_{avg} = 0.45 + 0.001 = 0.451 \text{ mA}$$

### **Step 4: Compare with Bluetooth Classic**

Bluetooth Classic maintains a continuous Piconet presence with $d \approx 1$ (always connected, streaming):

$$I_{avg,Classic} \approx 15 \text{ mA} \times 1.0 + 0.001 \text{ mA} \times 0 \approx 15 \text{ mA}$$

### **Step 5: Compute the power ratio**

$$\frac{I_{avg,BLE}}{I_{avg,Classic}} = \frac{0.451}{15} \approx 0.030 \approx 3\%$$

**Conclusion:** BLE consumes **only ~3%** of the current consumed by Bluetooth Classic under comparable conditions – a **~30× power reduction**. This is the mathematical basis for BLE's name.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Data Throughput per Connection Event

### **Step 1: Identify the bottleneck**

The throughput is limited by the smaller of:
- The **application data rate** requested by the user.
- The **physical layer symbol rate**.
- The **connection event duration**.

### **Step 2: Maximum payload per event (LE 1M PHY)**

For a single connection event of duration $T_{event}$:

$$D_{max} = \text{MTU} - 3 \text{ bytes (L2CAP header)} - 4 \text{ bytes (L2CAP SDU)}$$

With the **default MTU = 23 bytes**:

$$D_{max} = 23 - 7 = 16 \text{ bytes per packet}$$

### **Step 3: Packets per event**

If the slave has data to send and the master has data to send, both can transmit **1 packet per event** in each direction (with LE 1M PHY). Hence:

$$N_{pkts} = 1 \text{ packet/event/direction}$$

### **Step 4: Average throughput**

$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{D_{max} \times N_{pkts}}{T_c}$$

**Numerical example:** If $T_c = 7.5 \text{ ms}$ (minimum) and $D_{max} = 16$ bytes:

$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{16 \text{ bytes}}{7.5 \text{ ms}} = 2133 \text{ bytes/s} \approx 17 \text{ kbps}$$

### **Step 5: Effect of connection interval**

If $T_c$ is increased to **100 ms** (low-power mode):

$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{16}{0.1} = 160 \text{ bytes/s} = 1280 \text{ bps}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off observed:** **Lower $T_c$ → higher throughput, higher power.** **Higher $T_c$ → lower throughput, lower power.** This is the central engineering trade-off the KTU examiner will test.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Arduino BLE-Compatible Boards

| Feature | Arduino Nano 33 BLE | Arduino Nano 33 IoT | ESP32-DevKitC | HM-10 + Arduino Uno |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BLE Module** | nRF52840 (onboard) | u-blox NINA-B306 (onboard) | ESP32 (onboard) | HM-10 (CC2540) |
| **Library (Arduino)** | `BLE` (built-in) | Not native BLE (Wi-Fi) | `BLEArduino` / `NimBLE` | SoftwareSerial + AT commands |
| **BLE Version** | **5.0** | — | **4.2 / 5.0** | **4.0** |
| **Roles** | Peripheral / Central | Wi-Fi only | Both | Peripheral only |
| **Default Service UUID** | Customizable | — | Customizable | `0xFFE0` (vendor) |
| **Default Char UUID** | Customizable | — | Customizable | `0xFFE1` (vendor) |
| **Range (open air)** | ~50 m | — | ~80 m | ~10 m |
| **Flash / RAM** | 1 MB / 256 KB | 1 MB / 256 KB | 4 MB / 520 KB | 256 KB / 8 KB |
| **KTU Lab Suitability** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (ideal) | ❌ (Wi-Fi only) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ (older boards) |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Experimental Walkthrough (KTU Lab Procedure)

### **Experiment: Wireless LED Control via BLE (Peripheral–Central Architecture)**

#### **Hardware Required**
1. 2 × Arduino Nano 33 BLE (or equivalent) – *or* 1 × Arduino + 1 × smartphone with nRF Connect app.
2. 1 × LED (with 220 Ω current-limiting resistor).
3. 1 × Breadboard and jumper wires.
4. 1 × USB cable per board.
5. 1 × PC with Arduino IDE 2.x installed.
6. (Optional) HC-05/HC-08 BLE module if using Arduino Uno.

#### **Step 1: Arduino IDE Setup**
- Install the **ArduinoBLE** library (Tools → Manage Libraries → search "ArduinoBLE" → Install).
- For ESP32, install the **ESP32 board package** (Boards Manager URL: `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/espressif/arduino-esp32/gh-pages/package_esp32_index.json`).
- Select board: *Tools → Board → Arduino Nano 33 BLE* (or *ESP32 Dev Module*).

#### **Step 2: Program the Peripheral (Server) – Arduino with LED**
The peripheral exposes a service with a writable characteristic.

```cpp
// Peripheral Sketch – "BLE_LED_Peripheral"
#include <ArduinoBLE.h>

// Define a custom 16-bit UUID for the LED service
#define LED_SERVICE_UUID  "19B10000-E8F2-537E-4F6C-D104768A1214"
#define LED_CHAR_UUID     "19B10001-E8F2-537E-4F6C-D104768A1214"

BLEService ledService(LED_SERVICE_UUID);
BLEByteCharacteristic ledChar(LED_CHAR_UUID, BLERead | BLEWrite);

const int ledPin = 13;  // Onboard LED
bool oldState = false;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT);
  digitalWrite(ledPin, LOW);

  if (!BLE.begin()) {
    Serial.println("BLE init failed!");
    while (1);
  }

  BLE.setLocalName("KTU_LED_Bot");
  BLE.setAdvertisedService(ledService);
  ledService.addCharacteristic(ledChar);
  BLE.addService(ledService);
  ledChar.writeValue(0);          // initial value = 0
  BLE.advertise();
  Serial.println("BLE Peripheral advertising...");
}

void loop() {
  BLEDevice central = BLE.central();
  if (central) {
    Serial.print("Connected to: ");
    Serial.println(central.address());
    while (central.connected()) {
      if (ledChar.written()) {
        uint8_t val = ledChar.value();
        digitalWrite(ledPin, val ? HIGH : LOW);
        Serial.print("LED set to: "); Serial.println(val);
      }
    }
    Serial.println("Disconnected.");
  }
}
```

#### **Step 3: Program the Central (Client) – Arduino or Smartphone**
The central scans for `KTU_LED_Bot`, connects, and writes `1` or `0` to toggle the LED.

```cpp
// Central Sketch – "BLE_LED_Central"
#include <ArduinoBLE.h>

extern const char* LED_SERVICE_UUID = "19B10000-E8F2-537E-4F6C-D104768A1214";
extern const char* LED_CHAR_UUID    = "19B10001-E8F2-537E-4F6C-D104768A1214";

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  if (!BLE.begin()) { while(1); }
  BLE.scanForName("KTU_LED_Bot");
  Serial.println("Scanning...");
}

void loop() {
  BLEDevice peripheral = BLE.available();
  if (peripheral) {
    if (peripheral.localName() == "KTU_LED_Bot") {
      BLE.stopScan();
      if (peripheral.connect()) {
        Serial.println("Connected.");
        if (peripheral.discoverAttribute(LED_CHAR_UUID)) {
          // Toggle LED every 2 s
          uint8_t cmd = 0;
          while (peripheral.connected()) {
            cmd ^= 1;
            peripheral.writeCharacteristic(LED_CHAR_UUID, &cmd, 1);
            Serial.print("Sent: "); Serial.println(cmd);
            delay(2000);
          }
        }
        peripheral.disconnect();
        BLE.scanForName("KTU_LED_Bot");
      }
    }
  }
}
```

#### **Step 4: Compilation & Upload**
- Compile each sketch (`Ctrl+R` / `Cmd+R`).
- Upload to the respective boards (`Ctrl+U` / `Cmd+U`).
- Open **Serial Monitor** at **115200 baud**.

#### **Step 5: Observation & Verification**
- The peripheral's onboard **LED 13** should **toggle ON/OFF every 2 seconds**.
- The Serial Monitor of the central should print `Sent: 1` and `Sent: 0` alternately.
- Use the **nRF Connect** mobile app (free, by Nordic Semiconductor) to scan, connect, and visualize the same UUIDs as a verification cross-check.

#### **Step 6: Extended Variant – Sensor Telemetry**
Replace the LED with an **LM35 temperature sensor** on `A0`. Modify the peripheral to use `BLEFloatCharacteristic` and `notify()` to push readings every 1 second to the central (which can then actuate a motor or fan). This satisfies the "robot control" intent of PCCAL607.

---

## 3.5 Expected Viva Questions (Conceptual Drilling)

| # | Question | Expected Answer (Key Terms) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | What is the difference between GAP and GATT? | GAP: discovery & roles; GATT: data structure (Services/Characteristics). |
| 2 | Why does BLE use 3 advertising channels (37, 38, 39) only? | To avoid Wi-Fi interference (channels 1–13) and to enable quick discovery. |
| 3 | What is a UUID in BLE? | A 128-bit unique identifier for a Service or Characteristic. |
| 4 | What is CCCD? | Client Characteristic Configuration Descriptor – enables Notify/Indicate. |
| 5 | What is the Slave Latency parameter? | The number of connection events a slave may skip to save power. |
| 6 | What is MTU? | Maximum Transmission Unit – max payload bytes per L2CAP packet. |
| 7 | Why is GFSK used? | Robust, power-efficient, well-suited to low-cost 2.4 GHz radios. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BLE Protocol Stack (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Application Layer - Arduino Sketch] --> B[Generic Attribute Profile GATT]
    A --> C[Generic Access Profile GAP]
    B --> D[Attribute Protocol ATT]
    D --> E[Security Manager SM]
    D --> F[Logical Link Control L2CAP]
    F --> G[Host Controller Interface HCI]
    G --> H[Link Layer LL]
    H --> I[Physical Layer PHY - 2.4 GHz GFSK]
    I --> J[Air Interface - 40 RF Channels]
```

## 4.2 BLE Connection Sequence (Timing Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Peripheral
    participant C as Central
    P->>C: ADV_IND on Ch 37
    P->>C: ADV_IND on Ch 38
    P->>C: ADV_IND on Ch 39
    C->>C: Scan Window Active
    C->>P: CONNECT_IND
    P->>C: Connection Established
    loop Every Connection Interval Tc
        C->>P: Data Read Write Request
        P->>C: Data Response Notification
    end
    C->>P: Disconnect
```

## 4.3 GATT Data Hierarchy (Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[BLE Profile] --> S1[Service - LED Control - 19B10000]
    S1 --> S2[Characteristic - LED State - 19B10001]
    S2 --> S2a[Value 0 or 1]
    S2 --> S2b[Property - Read Write]
    S2 --> S2c[Descriptor - CCCD 0x2902]
    S1 --> S3[Characteristic - LED PWM Duty - 19B10002]
    S3 --> S3a[Value 0 to 255]
    S3 --> S3b[Property - Read Write Notify]
```

## 4.4 Peripheral–Central Hardware Wiring

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Arduino Peripheral] -->|USB| P1[PC - Serial Monitor]
    A1 -->|GPIO 13| L1[LED 220 ohm]
    A1 -->|2.4 GHz Radio| RF1[(BLE Air Interface)]
    RF1 -->|2.4 GHz Radio| A2[Arduino Central]
    A2 -->|USB| P2[PC - Serial Monitor]
    A2 -->|GPIO 9| M1[Servo Motor Signal]
    M1 -->|5V GND| V1[External 5V Supply]
```

## 4.5 Connection State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Standby
    Standby --> Advertising: BLE.begin + advertise
    Advertising --> Connected: CONNECT_IND received
    Connected --> Connected: GATT Read Write Notify
    Connected --> Standby: Disconnect or Timeout
    Advertising --> Standby: BLE.end
```

## 4.6 Power State Machine (Ultra-Low Power Operation)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Sleep
    Sleep --> TX: Wake on Event
    TX --> RX: Immediate Listen
    RX --> Sleep: Ack Received or Timeout
    RX --> TX: Retransmit
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Define BLE. Compare it with classical Bluetooth in terms of (i) power consumption, (ii) data rate, and (iii) typical application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) is a wireless PAN technology operating in the 2.4 GHz ISM band, designed for short-burst, low-power communication defined by the Bluetooth SIG.
2. **Comparison (2 marks):**
   - **Power:** BLE consumes ~0.01–0.05 W vs. ~1 W for Classic → **~30× lower**.
   - **Data Rate:** BLE ~0.27–2 Mbps (asymmetric, bursty) vs. Classic ~2–3 Mbps (continuous).
   - **Application:** BLE – sensors, wearables, IoT; Classic – audio streaming, file transfer.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model QP]**
**Explain the roles of GAP and GATT in the BLE stack with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **GAP (1 mark):** *Generic Access Profile* defines device **roles** (Peripheral/Central/Broadcaster/Observer) and procedures such as **advertising, scanning, and connection initiation**. Example: An Arduino Nano 33 BLE advertises its name `"KTU_LED_Bot"` every 100 ms.
2. **GATT (2 marks):** *Generic Attribute Profile* defines the **data structure** (Services and Characteristics) for post-connection transactions. Example: A custom service `19B10000-...` contains a writable characteristic `19B10001-...` that the central uses to toggle the LED.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A – 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Set A]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **BLE protocol stack**. Differentiate clearly between **GAP and GATT** with their functional responsibilities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An Arduino Nano 33 BLE peripheral advertises every **100 ms** with each advertisement lasting **3 ms**. The TX current is **15 mA** and the sleep current is **1 µA**. Compute the **average current consumption** and compare it with a Bluetooth Classic radio of identical TX current but with 100 % duty cycle. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution – Question A**

**Part (a) – 7 marks**

| Step | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Protocol Stack Diagram:** Draw and label the layers – Application → GATT/GAP → ATT → SM → L2CAP → HCI → Link Layer → PHY. | 2 |
| 2 | **GAP Functions:** Device roles, advertising, scanning, connection establishment, security mode selection. | 2 |
| 3 | **GATT Functions:** Service/Characteristic definition, data transactions (Read/Write/Notify/Indicate), descriptors. | 2 |
| 4 | **Distinction Table:** GAP = connection lifecycle; GATT = data transactions post-connection. | 1 |

**Part (b) – 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $t_{on} = 3 \text{ ms}$
- $T = 100 \text{ ms}$
- $I_{tx} = 15 \text{ mA}$
- $I_{sleep} = 1 \text{ \mu A} = 0.001 \text{ mA}$

**Step 1: Duty cycle (1 mark)**

$$d = \frac{t_{on}}{T} = \frac{3}{100} = 0.03$$

**Step 2: BLE average current (2 marks)**

$$I_{avg,BLE} = I_{tx} \cdot d + I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - d)$$

$$I_{avg,BLE} = 15 \times 0.03 + 0.001 \times 0.97$$

$$I_{avg,BLE} = 0.45 + 0.00097 = 0.45097 \text{ mA} \approx 0.451 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3: Classic Bluetooth average current (1 mark)**

For Classic with 100 % duty cycle, $d = 1$:

$$I_{avg,Classic} = 15 \times 1 + 0.001 \times 0 = 15 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 4: Comparison (2 marks)**

$$\text{Power Ratio} = \frac{0.451}{15} = 0.0301 \approx 3.0\%$$

**Step 5: Conclusion (1 mark):** BLE consumes only **~3 %** of the power of Bluetooth Classic under the given operating conditions – justifying the "Low Energy" nomenclature. This enables **coin-cell operation** for years.

---

### **Question B – 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Set B]**

**(a)** Explain the **GATT data hierarchy** (Profile → Service → Characteristic → Descriptor) with a suitable example for an Arduino-based temperature monitoring robotic system. Mention the role of **CCCD**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the **Arduino sketch (Peripheral role)** to advertise a temperature reading on a custom BLE service and **notify** a central device every 1 second. Specify the UUIDs, service definition, and characteristic properties used. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution – Question B**

**Part (a) – 7 marks**

| Step | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **GATT Hierarchy Explanation:** Profile (top) → Services (functional grouping) → Characteristics (data points) → Descriptors (metadata). | 2 |
| 2 | **Example:** Profile: *Robot Telemetry* → Service: *Temperature Service* (UUID `0x181A` or custom `19B10000-...`) → Characteristic: *Temp Value* (`19B10001-...`, float, Notify) → Descriptor: *CCCD* (`0x2902`), *User Description* (`0x2901`). | 3 |
| 3 | **CCCD Role:** Client Characteristic Configuration Descriptor (UUID `0x2902`) allows the **client to enable/disable server-initiated notifications** (`0x0001`) or indications (`0x0002`). Without it, `notify()` is silent. | 2 |

**Part (b) – 7 marks**

```cpp
// BLE Temperature Peripheral
#include <ArduinoBLE.h>

#define TEMP_SERVICE_UUID "19B10010-E8F2-537E-4F6C-D104768A1214"
#define TEMP_CHAR_UUID    "19B10011-E8F2-537E-4F6C-D104768A1214"

BLEService tempService(TEMP_SERVICE_UUID);
BLEFloatCharacteristic tempChar(TEMP_CHAR_UUID, BLERead | BLENotify);

const int sensorPin = A0;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  if (!BLE.begin()) { while(1); }
  BLE.setLocalName("KTU_TempBot");
  BLE.setAdvertisedService(tempService);
  tempService.addCharacteristic(tempChar);
  BLE.addService(tempService);
  BLE.advertise();
  Serial.println("Advertising Temperature Service...");
}

void loop() {
  BLEDevice central = BLE.central();
  if (central && central.connected()) {
    float voltage = analogRead(sensorPin) * (5.0 / 1023.0);
    float temperatureC = voltage * 100.0;       // LM35: 10 mV/°C
    tempChar.writeValue(temperatureC);
    tempChar.notify();
    Serial.print("Notified: "); Serial.print(temperatureC); Serial.println(" °C");
    delay(1000);
  }
}
```

**Mark distribution:**

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Correct `#include` and UUID definitions | 1 |
| `BLEService` and `BLEFloatCharacteristic` instantiation with `BLERead \vert BLENotify` | 2 |
| `setup()`: `BLE.begin`, set local name, advertised service, add characteristic, advertise | 2 |
| `loop()`: detect central, read LM35, convert to °C, `writeValue`, `notify`, 1 s delay | 2 |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks (and how to avoid it):**
> 1. **Confusing "BLE" with "Wi-Fi":** BLE is for short bursts of small data; Wi-Fi is for high throughput. Examiners will deduct **1–2 marks** for not distinguishing.
> 2. **Wrong unit for current:** Always state average current in **mA** or **µA** explicitly. Writing "$0.45$" without units costs a mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the duty-cycle explanation:** The derivation $I_{avg} = I_{tx} d + I_{sleep} (1-d)$ must be **derived**, not merely stated, to earn full marks.
> 4. **Missing CCCD explanation:** If a question on notifications is asked, students often forget to explain that the CCCD (`0x2902`) must be written by the client first.
> 5. **UUID confusion:** Standard 16-bit UUIDs (e.g., `0x180D`) are **not** the same as custom 128-bit UUIDs. Mention both forms in the answer.
> 6. **No code-compilation verification:** Always run the sketch on actual hardware before submission. A non-compiling sketch will be marked **zero** in the lab record.
> 7. **Serial Monitor baud rate mismatch:** Ensure **115200 baud** is set on both Serial Monitors; otherwise, only garbage characters will appear.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before the exam):**
- **BLE** = Bluetooth Low Energy, **2.4 GHz**, 40 channels, **GFSK** modulation.
- **40 channels** = **3 advertising (37, 38, 39)** + **37 data (0–36)**.
- **GAP** = Roles & Discovery; **GATT** = Data Structure.
- **GATT Hierarchy:** Profile → Service → Characteristic → Descriptor.
- **UUID** is **128-bit**; custom services often use 16-bit aliases.
- **CCCD (`0x2902`)** is required for **notifications/indications** to work.
- **Connection Interval** $T_c \in [7.5 \text{ ms},\ 4000 \text{ ms}]$ – key trade-off parameter.
- **Slave Latency** $L$ allows the slave to skip events for power saving.
- **Average current** $I_{avg} = I_{tx} d + I_{sleep} (1-d)$ – derivation is **frequently asked**.
- **LE 1M PHY:** 1 Mbps; **LE 2M PHY:** 2 Mbps; **LE Coded:** 125/500 kbps (long range).
- **Default MTU** = 23 bytes → **16 bytes** usable payload.
- **Arduino Nano 33 BLE** (nRF52840) is the most common KTU lab board; library = `ArduinoBLE`.
- **HM-10** uses **UART AT commands** and is a **peripheral-only** module.
- **ESP32** supports **both** peripheral and central roles.
- **Range:** 10–100 m (line of sight, class dependent).
- **Power:** ~30× lower than Bluetooth Classic; coin-cell operation possible.
- **Lab demo tip:** Always have a **photo of the working hardware** and a **Serial Monitor screenshot** in your record to earn full lab marks.
- **Viva tip:** Be ready to explain **why 3 advertising channels only** (to dodge Wi-Fi interference on channels 1, 6, 11).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Writing a Simple Publisher and Subscriber
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCAL607 - ROBOTICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Writing a Simple Publisher and Subscriber ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) In the **Robot Operating System (ROS)** framework, a **Publisher** is a node that continuously transmits messages over a named **Topic** to the ROS communication middleware, while a **Subscriber** is a node that registers itself to receive messages from that Topic. Together, they implement the **asynchronous, many-to-many, anonymous, message-passing paradigm** that forms the backbone of ROS inter-process communication, enabling decoupled, distributed robotic software architectures. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a radio station and a radio set: - The **radio station (Publisher)** broadcasts on a fixed **frequency (Topic)**, say 98.3 FM. It does not know or care who is listening — it just keeps pushing audio. - The **radio set (Subscriber)** tunes itself to **98.3 FM (Topic)** and starts receiving. Many listeners can tune to the same frequency without the station knowing. - The **airwaves (ROS Master / DDS layer)** carries the signal between them. - The **audio content (Message)** is a structured package (e.g., a string "Hello ROS!" with a sequence number). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Takeaway:** The Publisher and Subscriber never call each other directly. They are **loosely coupled**, **anonymous**, and communicate **only through the ROS Topic bus**. ### Standard Metrics & Constants | Metric | Standard Value / Type | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Topic Name** | `/topic_name` | Unique identifier on the ROS graph | | **Message Type** | `std_msgs/String`, `geometry_msgs/Twist` | Defines data schema | | **Queue Size** | `10` (default) | Buffer for asynchronous delivery | | **Rate** | `10 Hz` (typical) | Publishing frequency | | **Communication** | **TCPROS** (default) / **UDPROS** | Transport protocol | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Pattern:** Examiners frequently ask for the *exact line of code* that creates a publisher/subscriber, the *callback signature*, and the role of `rospy.init_node()`. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### Operational Logic — Broken Down **A. System Architecture Components** - **ROS Master**: The naming service that allows nodes to find one another at runtime. It stores the publisher/subscriber registry. - **Publisher Node**: Calls `rospy.Publisher(topic, type, queue_size)` and publishes via `.publish(msg)`. - **Subscriber Node**: Calls `rospy.Subscriber(topic, type, callback)` and processes messages inside the callback. - **Topic**: The named bus over which messages of a specific type flow. - **Message**: A strictly typed data structure (`.msg` file) compiled into Python/C++ classes. **B. Publisher Lifecycle Steps** 1. Initialize the node (`rospy.init_node('talker')`) — registers with the Master. 2. Create a `Publisher` object — declares intent + registers with the Master. 3. Construct a `Message` object of the correct type. 4. Enter a loop (rate-limited by `rospy.Rate(Hz)`). 5. Log/print and call `.publish(msg)`. 6. Sleep until next cycle via `rate.sleep()`. 7. On `rospy.is_shutdown()`, the node exits cleanly. **C. Subscriber Lifecycle Steps** 1. Initialize the node (`rospy.init_node('listener')`). 2. Create a `Subscriber` object — registers with the Master and triggers XMLRPC handshake. 3. The Master matches this Subscriber to existing Publishers on the same Topic. 4. A **TCPROS** connection is established directly between the two nodes (peer-to-peer). 5. Each incoming message triggers the user-defined **callback function** automatically. 6. `rospy.spin()` enters an infinite loop so the callback can be invoked repeatedly. **D. The "Why" Behind Each Step** - `init_node` is mandatory because every ROS process must have a **unique graph identity**. - `queue_size` prevents unbounded memory growth if the subscriber is slow — old messages get dropped. - The callback **must not block** the main thread for long durations; long work should be offloaded. - `rospy.spin()` prevents the script from exiting — without it, the callback would never fire. ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Symbol / Syntax | Description | |---|---|---| | Node Initialization | `rospy.init_node(name, anonymous=False)` | Registers node with ROS Master | | Publisher | `rospy.Publisher(topic, MsgType, queue_size)` | Creates a publishing handle | | Subscriber | `rospy.Subscriber(topic, MsgType, callback)` | Creates a subscription handle | | Message Construction | `msg = MsgType()` then assign fields | e.g., `msg.data = "hello"` | | Publish Action | `pub.publish(msg)` | Pushes one message to the Topic | | Loop Rate | `rate = rospy.Rate(10)` | 10 messages per second | | Blocking Loop | `rospy.spin()` | Keeps node alive for callbacks | | Time Stamp | `rospy.Time.now()` or `rospy.get_time()` | Wall-clock time in seconds | | Logging | `rospy.loginfo(...)`, `logwarn`, `logerr` | ROS-aware console output | | Shutdown Check | `rospy.is_shutdown()` | Boolean flag for clean exit | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** This pattern is used in industrial mobile robots (publishing odometry), collaborative arms (subscribing to trajectory commands), and autonomous vehicles (subscribing to LIDAR point clouds). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Complete Working Code — Publisher (talker.py) ```python #!/usr/bin/env python3 import rospy from std_msgs.msg import String def talker(): # --- Step 1: Initialize the publisher node --- # anonymous=True appends a random number so multiple instances don't collide. pub = rospy.Publisher('chatter', String, queue_size=10) rospy.init_node('talker', anonymous=True) # --- Step 2: Set the publishing rate --- rate = rospy.Rate(10) # 10 Hz # --- Step 3: Loop until ROS is shut down --- while not rospy.is_shutdown(): hello_str = "hello world %s" % rospy.get_time() # --- Step 4: Log to the ROS console (rosout + terminal) --- rospy.loginfo(hello_str) # --- Step 5: Publish the message onto the Topic --- pub.publish(hello_str) # --- Step 6: Sleep for the remainder of the cycle --- rate.sleep() if __name__ == '__main__': try: talker() except rospy.ROSInterruptException: # Raised when Ctrl+C or node shutdown occurs mid-sleep pass ``` ### Complete Working Code — Subscriber (listener.py) ```python #!/usr/bin/env python3 import rospy from std_msgs.msg import String # --- Step 1: Define the callback function --- # IMPORTANT: This runs on EVERY incoming message. def callback(data): rospy.loginfo(rospy.get_caller_id() + " I heard %s", data.data) def listener(): # --- Step 2: Initialize the subscriber node --- rospy.init_node('listener', anonymous=True) # --- Step 3: Subscribe to the topic --- rospy.Subscriber("chatter", String, callback) # --- Step 4: Block and keep the node alive --- rospy.spin() if __name__ == '__main__': listener() ``` ### Step-by-Step Execution Procedure 1. **Source the workspace** in every terminal: ``` source /opt/ros//setup.bash source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash ``` 2. **Start the ROS Master** in Terminal 1: ``` roscore ``` 3. **Make the scripts executable**: ``` chmod +x talker.py listener.py ``` 4. **Run the Publisher** in Terminal 2: ``` rosrun talker.py ``` 5. **Run the Subscriber** in Terminal 3: ``` rosrun listener.py ``` 6. **Inspect the graph** (optional verification): ``` rqt_graph rostopic echo /chatter rostopic list rostopic info /chatter rosnode list ``` ### Communication Protocol Derivation — The Handshake $$ \text{Connection Establishment} \;=\; \text{REGISTRATION} \;\to\; \text{DISCOVERY} \;\to\; \text{TRANSPORT} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{1. REGISTRATION:} \quad & \text{Publisher} \xrightarrow{\text{XMLRPC}} \text{Master: "I publish to /chatter (std\_msgs/String)"} \\ \text{2. SUBSCRIPTION:} \quad & \text{Subscriber} \xrightarrow{\text{XMLRPC}} \text{Master: "I subscribe to /chatter"} \\ \text{3. DISCOVERY:} \quad & \text{Master} \rightarrow \text{Subscriber: "Publisher URI is \texttt{rosrpc://host:port}"} \\ \text{4. HANDSHAKE:} \quad & \text{Subscriber} \xrightarrow{\text{TCPROS}} \text{Publisher: data channel opened} \\ \text{5. DATA FLOW:} \quad & \text{Publisher} \xrightarrow{\text{TCPROS}} \text{Subscriber: msg, msg, msg, ...} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic:** The Master only does *name resolution*. The actual message data flows **peer-to-peer** over TCPROS. This is why ROS scales — the Master is not a bottleneck. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Mermaid Diagram 1 — Publisher/Subscriber Data Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PubNode["Publisher Node: talker"] A1["rospy.init_node talker"] --> A2["rospy.Publisher chatter String queue 10"] A2 --> A3["rospy.Rate 10Hz"] A3 --> A4["pub.publish msg"] A4 --> A5{"rospy.is_shutdown"} A5 -- No --> A3 A5 -- Yes --> A6["Exit cleanly"] end T["ROS Topic: chatter std_msgs/String"] A4 -- "TCPROS msg.data" --> T subgraph SubNode["Subscriber Node: listener"] T -- "callback data" --> B1["rospy.init_node listener"] B1 --> B2["rospy.Subscriber chatter String callback"] B2 --> B3["callback msg invoked"] B3 --> B4["rospy.loginfo I heard"] B4 --> B5["rospy.spin keeps alive"] B5 --> B3 end M["ROS Master roscore"] --- A2 M --- B2 ``` ### Mermaid Diagram 2 — Topic/Message Lifecycle ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant M as ROS Master participant P as talker.py participant S as listener.py P->>M: Register Publisher on /chatter (String, q=10) S->>M: Register Subscriber on /chatter M-->>S: Return Publisher URI (rosrpc://host:port) S->>P: TCPROS connection request P-->>S: Connection accepted loop Every 100 ms (10 Hz) P->>P: msg = String() P->>P: msg.data = hello + t P->>S: publish(msg) S->>S: callback(msg) invoked S->>S: loginfo I heard hello + t end Note over P,S: Process continues until Ctrl+C
or rospy.is_shutdown() == True ``` ### Mermaid Diagram 3 — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Dev["Development Environment"] IDE["talker.py / listener.py"] CMake["CMakeLists.txt + package.xml"] Src["catkin_ws/src//scripts/"] end subgraph Run["Runtime ROS Graph"] Master["roscore : ROS Master"] T["Topic: chatter"] Pub["Publisher Node"] Sub["Subscriber Node"] end subgraph Tools["Diagnostic Tools"] Echo["rostopic echo /chatter"] Info["rostopic info /chatter"] Hz["rostopic hz /chatter"] Graph["rqt_graph"] Rqt["rqt_console"] end IDE --> Src CMake --> Src Src -- rosrun --> Pub Src -- rosrun --> Sub Pub --> T Sub --> T Master -.registers.-> Pub Master -.registers.-> Sub T --> Echo T --> Info T --> Hz T --> Graph Sub --> Rqt ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** *What is the role of the ROS Master in Publisher–Subscriber communication? What happens to message flow if the Master crashes?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **ROS Master** (started by `roscore`) acts as the **naming and registration service** for all nodes. It maintains a registry of which nodes publish or subscribe to which topics, and on request provides the **URI of publishers** so subscribers can establish direct TCPROS connections. **Role breakdown:** 1. Receives publisher registration (topic name + message type). 2. Receives subscriber subscription request. 3. Responds to subscriber with the publisher's network URI. 4. Enables peer-to-peer data handshake. **If Master crashes after connections are established**, the *existing* TCPROS links continue to deliver messages, but **no new nodes can discover each other**, and *parameter server* access is lost. --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`** *Differentiate between `rospy.spin()` and `rospy.Rate().sleep()`. Why is `spin()` mandatory in a subscriber-only node?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | `rospy.Rate().sleep()` | `rospy.spin()` | |---|---|---| | Purpose | Enforces fixed publishing frequency | Blocks main thread to allow callbacks | | Used by | Publisher (loops actively) | Subscriber (loops passively) | | Prevents exit? | Only inside the while loop | Yes — entire script stays alive | | Calls callbacks? | No | Yes — invokes them on every msg | **`spin()` is mandatory** in a subscriber-only node because without it, the Python interpreter reaches the end of `listener()` and the process terminates. Subscribers receive messages **only via callbacks**, and callbacks run **only inside `spin()`** (or its multi-threaded alternatives). --- ### Part B — Full Descriptive Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Write the complete Python code for a ROS **Publisher node** that publishes a `std_msgs/String` message containing the text `"KTU Robotics Lab"` along with a counter that increments on every publish, at a rate of **5 Hz**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Write the corresponding **Subscriber node** that logs every received message to the ROS console, and explain what `queue_size=10` means. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution (a):** ```python #!/usr/bin/env python3 import rospy from std_msgs.msg import String def ktu_talker(): pub = rospy.Publisher('ktu_chatter', String, queue_size=10) rospy.init_node('ktu_talker', anonymous=True) rate = rospy.Rate(5) # 5 Hz counter = 0 while not rospy.is_shutdown(): msg = String() msg.data = "KTU Robotics Lab #%d" % counter rospy.loginfo("Publishing: %s", msg.data) pub.publish(msg) counter += 1 rate.sleep() if __name__ == '__main__': try: ktu_talker() except rospy.ROSInterruptException: pass ``` **Explanation of key lines (board points):** - `queue_size=10` → ROS keeps at most 10 unconsumed messages in the outbound buffer. - `anonymous=True` → avoids name collision when multiple instances are launched. - `rospy.Rate(5)` → enforces 200 ms between publishes. - `ROSInterruptException` → caught so that `Ctrl+C` exits cleanly. --- **Model Solution (b):** ```python #!/usr/bin/env python3 import rospy from std_msgs.msg import String def callback(data): rospy.loginfo("Listener received: %s", data.data) def ktu_listener(): rospy.init_node('ktu_listener', anonymous=True) rospy.Subscriber('ktu_chatter', String, callback) rospy.spin() if __name__ == '__main__': ktu_listener() ``` **Meaning of `queue_size=10` (board points):** - It is the **size of the outgoing message queue** for the Publisher. - If the subscriber is slow and the queue is full, **oldest messages are dropped** (FIFO with bounded buffer). - Setting it to `None` or `0` is special — it means an **infinite queue** (legacy compatibility; not recommended for real-time systems). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Publisher–Subscriber communication architecture** in ROS. Identify the three roles involved and the protocol used for data transfer. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Write a Python subscriber that listens to a topic named `/sensor_data` of type `std_msgs/Int32` and computes the **running average** of the last 5 received values. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution (a):** The three roles are: 1. **ROS Master** — runs `roscore`; maintains the parameter server and the registration table of publishers and subscribers. 2. **Publisher Node** — registers itself with the Master on a topic and pushes typed messages. 3. **Subscriber Node** — registers with the Master, receives the publisher's URI, opens a direct TCPROS connection, and processes messages via a callback. **Protocol:** After the XMLRPC discovery handshake with the Master, the actual message stream is carried by **TCPROS** (a TCP-based, length-prefixed, binary protocol) — directly between nodes, **not** through the Master. *Refer to Mermaid Diagram 2 above for the visual flow.* --- **Model Solution (b):** ```python #!/usr/bin/env python3 import rospy from std_msgs.msg import Int32 from collections import deque WINDOW = 5 buffer = deque(maxlen=WINDOW) def callback(msg): buffer.append(msg.data) avg = sum(buffer) / len(buffer) rospy.loginfo("Received %d | Running Avg of last %d = %.2f", msg.data, len(buffer), avg) def avg_listener(): rospy.init_node('avg_listener', anonymous=True) rospy.Subscriber('/sensor_data', Int32, callback) rospy.spin() if __name__ == '__main__': avg_listener() ``` **Logic explanation:** - `deque(maxlen=5)` automatically discards the oldest value when a 6th arrives — perfect for a sliding window. - The average is recomputed on every callback using O(5) work. - The subscriber only needs `spin()` because all logic happens inside the callback. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting `rospy.init_node()`** → Master has no record of the node; publish/subscribe silently fails. *(−2 marks)* > 2. **Mismatched message types** between publisher and subscriber (e.g., `String` vs `Int32`) → ROS throws a `TypeMismatch` exception at connection time. *(−2 marks)* > 3. **No `rospy.spin()` in subscriber** → script exits immediately, callback never fires. *(−2 marks)* > 4. **Forgetting `rate.sleep()` in publisher** → infinite tight loop pegs the CPU at 100%. *(−1 mark)* > 5. **Not wrapping talker in `try/except ROSInterruptException`** → unclean exit on `Ctrl+C`. *(−1 mark)* --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Three mandatory lines** every publisher needs: `init_node`, `Publisher`, `publish`. - ✅ **Three mandatory lines** every subscriber needs: `init_node`, `Subscriber`, `spin`. - ✅ The **ROS Master does NOT carry data** — only does name resolution. Data flows peer-to-peer via TCPROS. - ✅ **`queue_size`** is for publishers; subscribers have an implicit in-process queue. - ✅ **`anonymous=True`** is best practice in labs to avoid node-name collisions. - ✅ **`rospy.Rate(Hz).sleep()`** controls publisher frequency — never use `time.sleep()`. - ✅ **Callbacks must be non-blocking** — long computations should be offloaded. - ✅ Always **run `roscore` first** before launching any node. - ✅ Use `rqt_graph` and `rostopic echo` to *debug* — they are the examiner's favorite verification tools. - ✅ Common message packages: `std_msgs`, `geometry_msgs`, `sensor_msgs`, `nav_msgs`. - ✅ For a 14-mark lab viva question, expect: code + explanation of `queue_size` + diagram of Master-mediated discovery + difference between `spin` and `Rate.sleep`.

topics.title: Simple Service and Client
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAL607 - ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simple Service and Client

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the **Robot Operating System (ROS)** architecture, communication between nodes is achieved through two fundamental paradigms: the **Topic (Publisher–Subscriber)** model and the **Service (Service–Client)** model. A **Simple Service and Client** experiment in the KTU Robotics Lab demonstrates the **synchronous, request/reply communication pattern** in which a *Client* node sends a structured *Request* message to a *Service* node, which executes a callback, and returns a structured *Response* message to the originating client. The exchange is **blocking** (the client halts execution until the response is received) and **one-to-one** (one client ↔ one service instance per call).

The service interface is formally declared in a **`.srv` file** consisting of two parts separated by `---`:
$$\text{Request Message} \;|\; \text{Response Message}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCAL607 – Module 2):**
> Students must be able to *create a ROS service node*, *create a ROS client node*, *define a custom service message*, *modify `package.xml` and `CMakeLists.txt`*, and *execute the service using the `rosservice call` terminal command*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Restaurant Counter

Imagine walking into a busy restaurant:

| Role in Restaurant | Role in ROS |
|---|---|
| **You** (customer placing an order) | **Client Node** |
| **Waiter** taking the order slip to the kitchen | **Service Request** (TCP packet) |
| **Chef** in the kitchen cooking the dish | **Service Server Callback** |
| **Waiter** returning with the prepared dish | **Service Response** |
| **Menu Card** listing what can be ordered | **`.srv` Service Definition** |

You do **not** start cooking yourself — you *place a request* and **wait (synchronously)** until the dish arrives. The kitchen (service) processes the request and returns a *single, structured response*. This is exactly how ROS Services operate: a **client issues a request and blocks until the service node returns a response**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** *Topics* are like a **radio broadcast** (many subscribers listen continuously, fire-and-forget). *Services* are like a **phone call** (one-to-one, request-reply, blocking). Examiners frequently test this comparison.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Blocking Call:** $\text{wait time} \geq \text{server processing time}$
- **One-to-One Topology:** $1 \text{ Client} \leftrightarrow 1 \text{ Service Handler}$
- **Transport Protocol:** **TCP** (default) or **UDP** (declared in service definition)
- **Default Master URI:** `http://localhost:11311` (port **11311**)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown – How a Service-Client Exchange Works

The lifecycle of a single service call consists of **6 sequential steps**:

1. **Service Registration:** The *Service Node* registers itself with the **ROS Master** (`roscore`) using `advertiseService()`. The master records the service name, node URI, and message type.
2. **Service Lookup:** The *Client Node* queries the ROS Master via `wait_for_service()` to confirm the service is alive and obtain its endpoint URI.
3. **Persistent Connection:** A direct **TCP connection** is established between Client and Service, *bypassing the master for the actual data transfer* (the master only brokers the initial handshake).
4. **Request Transmission:** The client serializes the request message and sends it. The client thread **blocks** (suspends).
5. **Callback Execution:** The service node deserializes the request, invokes the user-defined callback function, which returns a response object.
6. **Response Delivery:** The response is serialized and sent back. The client's blocking call unblocks and returns the response object.

### 2.2 Why Service-Client? (The Engineering "Why")

| Scenario | Recommended Paradigm | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous sensor stream (e.g., LiDAR) | **Topic (Pub/Sub)** | Asynchronous, lossy is acceptable, high frequency |
| Compute a value on demand (e.g., kinematics inversion) | **Service** | Synchronous, guaranteed response, low frequency |
| Trigger an action (e.g., "open gripper") | **Service** | Discrete, one-shot, needs acknowledgement |
| Broadcast robot state (e.g., odometry) | **Topic** | Many subscribers may need it simultaneously |

### 2.3 The `.srv` File Anatomy

A service description file (e.g., `AddTwoInts.srv`) is structured as:

```text
int64 a          <-- Request Field 1
int64 b          <-- Request Field 2
---              <-- Mandatory Separator
int64 sum        <-- Response Field 1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule:** The `---` separator is **mandatory**. Request fields are above; response fields are below. Data types mirror standard ROS message primitives (`int32`, `int64`, `float32`, `string`, `bool`, etc.).

### 2.4 KTU Formula & Syntax Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum command/vocabulary set** the KTU examiner expects you to reproduce from memory in the lab record and viva.

| **Element** | **Syntax / Command (Python `rospy`)** | **Purpose** |
|---|---|---|
| Import Service Class | `from package_name.srv import ServiceName, ServiceNameRequest, ServiceNameResponse` | Loads the generated service message |
| Wait for Service | `rospy.wait_for_service('service_name')` | Blocks until service is up |
| Create Proxy | `proxy \= rospy.ServiceProxy('service_name', ServiceName)` | Client-side handle to the service |
| Send Request | `resp \= proxy(req_a, req_b)` | Sends request, blocks, returns response |
| Server Declaration | `rospy.Service('service_name', ServiceName, handler_callback)` | Advertises service to master |
| Build Code | `catkin_make` / `catkin build` | Compiles `.srv` and Python files |
| Source Workspace | `source ~/catkin_ws/devel/setup.bash` | Overlays workspace into environment |
| Launch Master | `roscore` | Starts the ROS Master + `rosout` + parameter server |
| List Active Services | `rosservice list` | Displays all registered services |
