# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
**What is a ROS bag file? List any two methods to inspect its contents from the command line.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A ROS bag file is a **binary log file** used in ROS/ROS 2 to record serialized messages published on topics over a period of time, enabling offline replay and analysis. **(1 mark)**
Two CLI inspection methods:
1. `ros2 bag info <bag_path>` — prints topic list, message count, duration, and storage type. **(1 mark)**
2. `ros2 bag play <bag_path> --topics /odom` — replays (and thus exposes) only the specified topic for live visualization. **(1 mark)**

> *Alternate accepted*: `sqlite3 my_bag.db3 ".tables"` to peek inside the SQLite schema (ROS 2 bags).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
**Differentiate between `SequentialReader` and `SequentialWriter` in `rosbag2_py`. When would you use each?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `SequentialReader` is used to **read** an existing bag file sequentially, iterating through recorded messages topic by topic. **(1 mark)**
- `SequentialWriter` is used to **create** a new bag file and write live messages from a running ROS 2 graph into it. **(1 mark)**
- Reader is used in **offline post-processing** (analysis, ML dataset extraction, debugging); Writer is used during **data collection** sessions (e.g., recording a robot's sensor stream while it drives). **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern**: Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each. We provide **two full alternative questions** below — answer **any one**.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Python Implementation Focus

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**With a neat Python script, explain how you would read messages from a ROS 2 bag file named `~/bags/lab_demo` containing the topic `/turtle1/pose` of type `turtlesim/msg/Pose`. Show how you would extract the `(x, y, theta)` fields and print them along with the timestamp.**

**Model Solution:**

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import rclpy
from rclpy.serialization import deserialize_message
from rosidl_runtime_py.utilities import get_message
import rosbag2_py

# (1) Configure storage
storage_options   = rosbag2_py.StorageOptions(
    uri='/root/bags/lab_demo', storage_id='sqlite3')
converter_options = rosbag2_py.ConverterOptions('cdr', 'cdr')

# (2) Open the bag
reader = rosbag2_py.SequentialReader()
reader.open(storage_options, converter_options)             # 1 mark

# (3) Discover topics
topic_md = reader.get_all_topics_and_types()                # 1 mark
print("Topics:", [(t.name, t.type) for t in topic_md])

# (4) Apply filter
reader.set_filter(rosbag2_py.StorageFilter(
    topics=['/turtle1/pose']))                              # 1 mark

# (5) Resolve type
Pose = get_message('turtlesim/msg/Pose')                    # 1 mark

# (6) Iterate, deserialize, print
while reader.has_next():                                   # 1 mark
    topic, data, ts = reader.read_next()
    pose = deserialize_message(data, Pose)                  # 1 mark
    print(f"[{ts/1e9:.3f}s] x={pose.x:.3f} "
          f"y={pose.y:.3f} theta={pose.theta:.3f}")         # 1 mark
```

**Mark Allocation (7):**
- Bag open & filter setup — **2 marks**
- Type resolution & deserialization — **2 marks**
- Correct field access and timestamp formatting — **2 marks**
- Output comment / neatness — **1 mark**

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**Extend the script above to compute the total distance traveled by the turtle. The relevant fields are `linear_velocity` (m/s) and the time difference between successive messages. Show the formula used.**

**Model Solution:**

The instantaneous distance covered between two messages $i$ and $i+1$ is:

$$
\Delta s_i = v_i \cdot \Delta t_i = v_i \cdot \frac{t_{i+1} - t_i}{10^9}
$$

The total distance is the sum:

$$
S_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \Delta s_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} v_i \cdot \frac{t_{i+1} - t_i}{10^9}
$$

```python
prev_ts_ns   = None
total_dist   = 0.0
while reader.has_next():
    topic, data, ts = reader.read_next()
    pose = deserialize_message(data, Pose)
    if prev_ts_ns is not None:                              # 1 mark
        dt = (ts - prev_ts_ns) / 1e9                       # 1 mark
        total_dist += pose.linear_velocity * dt            # 2 marks
    prev_ts_ns = ts

print(f"Total distance traveled = {total_dist:.4f} m")     # 1 mark
# Error handling for negative dt, NaN — 1 mark
```

**Mark Allocation (7):**
- Correct formula written in exam — **2 marks**
- Correct implementation of $\Delta t$ — **1 mark**
- Correct accumulation of distance — **2 marks**
- Final print and edge-case handling — **2 marks**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — C++ Implementation Focus

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Write a complete C++ program using `rosbag2_cpp::readers::SequentialReader` to read all messages from the topic `/imu/data` of type `sensor_msgs/msg/Imu` in a bag stored at `~/bags/imu_record`. Print the `orientation` (quaternion) and `angular_velocity` (vector) for each message.**

**Model Solution (Key Code Excerpts — full file in §3.2):**

```cpp
#include "rclcpp/rclcpp.hpp"
#include "rclcpp/serialization.hpp"
#include "rosbag2_cpp/readers/sequential_reader.hpp"
#include "sensor_msgs/msg/imu.hpp"

int main() {
    rosbag2_cpp::StorageOptions so;
    so.uri = "/root/bags/imu_record"; so.storage_id = "sqlite3";
    rosbag2_cpp::ConverterOptions co{"cdr", "cdr"};

    auto reader = std::make_shared<rosbag2_cpp::readers::SequentialReader>();
    reader->open(so, co);                                    // 1 mark

    rosbag2_storage::StorageFilter f;
    f.topics.push_back("/imu/data");
    reader->set_filter(f);                                   // 1 mark

    rclcpp::Serialization<sensor_msgs::msg::Imu> ser;        // 1 mark
    while (reader->has_next()) {
        auto bm = reader->read_next();                       // 1 mark
        rclcpp::SerializedMessage sm(*bm);
        sensor_msgs::msg::Imu imu;
        ser.deserialize_message(&sm, &imu);                  // 1 mark
        std::cout << "  q=(" << imu.orientation.x << ","
                  << imu.orientation.y << ","
                  << imu.orientation.z << ","
                  << imu.orientation.w << ")"
                  << "  w=(" << imu.angular_velocity.x << ","
                  << imu.angular_velocity.y << ","
                  << imu.angular_velocity.z << ")\n";        # 1 mark
    }
    return 0;                                                // 1 mark
}
```

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**Explain how you would convert the quaternion orientation to Euler angles (roll, pitch, yaw) for each message. Provide the conversion formula and show where in the code it would be inserted.**

**Model Solution:**

The quaternion-to-Euler conversion (ZYX intrinsic convention) is:

$$
\phi   = \text{atan2}\big(2(w x + y z),\ 1 - 2(x^2 + y^2)\big)
$$

$$
\theta = \text{asin}\big(2(w y - z x)\big)
$$

$$
\psi   = \text{atan2}\big(2(w z + x y),\ 1 - 2(y^2 + z^2)\big)
$$

Where $(w, x, y, z)$ are the quaternion components. Insertion point in the read loop:

```cpp
double w = imu.orientation.w, x = imu.orientation.x,
       y = imu.orientation.y, z = imu.orientation.z;

double roll  = std::atan2(2.0*(w*x + y*z),
                          1.0 - 2.0*(x*x + y*y));   // 1 mark
double pitch = std::asin (2.0*(w*y - z*x));          // 1 mark
double yaw   = std::atan2(2.0*(w*z + x*y),
                          1.0 - 2.0*(y*y + z*z));   // 1 mark
std::cout << "roll=" << roll << " pitch=" << pitch
          << " yaw=" << yaw << std::endl;            // 1 mark
```

**Mark Allocation (7):**
- Three correct formulas — **3 marks**
- Correct C++ function calls (`std::atan2`, `std::asin`) — **2 marks**
- Correct field access from `imu.orientation` — **1 mark**
- Proper insertion into the read loop and final output — **1 mark**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students LOSE marks in this experiment:**
>
> 1. **Wrong `uri` path** — the bag is a *folder*, not a file. If you point to `~/bags/lab_demo/lab_demo.db3` it fails. Use the parent directory.
> 2. **Storage ID mismatch** — Recording with `mcap` but reading with `sqlite3` (or vice-versa) throws an exception. Always check `ros2 bag info` first.
> 3. **Forgetting `set_filter`** — Iterating the whole bag is **slow** and may run out of memory; examiners deduct 1–2 marks for inefficiency.
> 4. **Not closing the reader** (Python: no `with` block) — In Python, prefer `with rosbag2_py.SequentialReader() as r:` or explicitly call cleanup. In C++, the destructor handles it but mention it in the record.
> 5. **Wrong timestamp unit** — `read_next()` returns **nanoseconds**, not seconds. Dividing by $10^9$ is mandatory for human-readable output. Forgetting this loses a full mark.
> 6. **Hard-coding message types** — In C++, you must `#include` the message header. Forgetting `#include "turtlesim/msg/pose.hpp"` causes linker errors. In Python, forgetting to `source /opt/ros/<distro>/setup.bash` causes `get_message` to fail.
> 7. **No `package.xml` / `setup.py` update** — In the lab record, you must show that the package was rebuilt with `colcon build` after adding the script.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the lab exam.**

- **A ROS bag file is a binary, indexed log of ROS topic messages** used for offline analysis, debugging, ML dataset extraction, and algorithm replay.
- **Python API** uses `rosbag2_py.SequentialReader` (ROS 2) or `rosbag.Bag` (ROS 1). **C++ API** uses `rosbag2_cpp::readers::SequentialReader` or `rosbag::Bag`.
- **The three-stage pipeline**: *Open* → *Filter* → *Iterate & Deserialize*. Memorize the order.
- **Always call `get_all_topics_and_types()` first** to know the available topics and their message types.
- **Apply a topic filter** with `set_filter(StorageFilter(topics=[...]))` for performance.
- **Timestamps are in nanoseconds** in ROS 2. Divide by $10^9$ to get seconds.
- **Python is dynamic** — `get_message(string)` resolves types at runtime. **C++ is static** — you must `#include` the message header and templatize `rclcpp::Serialization<MsgT>`.
- **Deserialization** is the function $\mathcal{D}(B_i, T_{c(i)}) = M_i$ that converts stored bytes into typed message objects.
- **Storage formats**: `.db3` (SQLite) for ROS 2 Foxy/Humble, `.mcap` for modern ROS 2 (Iron+) and tools like Foxglove.
- **CLI commands** worth memorizing: `ros2 bag record`, `ros2 bag info`, `ros2 bag play`.
- **Common lab evaluation topics**: filtering by topic, filtering by time window, computing statistics (mean, distance, max), writing results to CSV.
- **Don't confuse**: `StorageOptions` (for opening) vs. `RecordOptions` (for writing).
- **Time complexity** of a filtered read is $\mathcal{O}(\log N + k)$ thanks to the bag's B-tree index.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Localization of a Mobile Robot Using LIDAR (ROS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCAL607 - ROBOTICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Localization of a Mobile Robot Using LIDAR (ROS) # 🟢 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Localization** is the process by which a mobile robot determines its pose (position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$) relative to a known environment map, using onboard sensors such as **LIDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)**, wheel odometry, and IMU data, executed within the **Robot Operating System (ROS)** framework. In the context of **PCCAL607 — Robotics Lab**, this experiment implements **2D Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** — specifically the **AMCL (Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization)** package in ROS — to track a differential-drive mobile robot (typically the *TurtleBot3 Burger/Waffle Pi*) in a pre-built occupancy-grid map. **LIDAR** is an active remote-sensing device that emits laser pulses (typically in the **905 nm** or **1550 nm** infrared band) and measures the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** to compute distances to surrounding obstacles with sub-centimeter accuracy. | Term | Full Form | KTU Standard Definition | |------|-----------|-------------------------| | ROS | Robot Operating System | Open-source meta-operating system for robot software development | | AMCL | Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization | Probabilistic localization algorithm in `ros-navigation` stack | | TF | Transform | ROS coordinate-frame management system | | ToF | Time-of-Flight | Time taken by laser pulse to travel to target and return | | MCL | Monte Carlo Localization | Particle-filter-based pose estimation method | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "How a Robot 'Knows' Where It Is" > [!NOTE] > Imagine you wake up blindfolded inside your own house. You hear echoes of your voice bouncing off walls, furniture, and doors. With every echo, your brain compares the acoustic signature to a mental map of your home. After a few claps, you instinctively know: *"I am in the living room, facing the sofa."* That is **exactly** what LIDAR-based localization does: | Human Action | Robot Equivalent | |--------------|------------------| | Clapping & listening to echoes | LIDAR firing laser beams & measuring returns | | Mental map of house | Pre-loaded occupancy-grid map in ROS | | Reasoning about room location | Particle filter computing pose probabilities | | Updating belief after each echo | Bayesian update of particle weights | A laser pulse travels at the speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$, so even a tiny timing error of **1 nanosecond** translates to a range error of **0.15 m**. This is why high-grade automotive LIDARs use **905 nm** silicon-compatible lasers, while defense-grade systems use eye-safe **1550 nm** sources. --- ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics (Highlighted) > The following values are **KTU Board Examiner favorites** — memorize them: > > - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ > - LIDAR wavelength (typical): $\lambda = 905 \text{ nm}$ > - LIDAR field of view (2D scanner): $360^\circ$ or $270^\circ$ > - Standard ROS scan rate: **10 Hz** to **40 Hz** > - TurtleBot3 LDS-01 range: **0.12 m to 3.5 m** > - Map resolution (occupancy grid): **0.05 m/cell** (default) --- # 🟢 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept: How AMCL Localization Works in ROS The localization pipeline in ROS follows a strict **publish–subscribe** messaging architecture. The following sequence describes a single localization cycle: 1. **Map Server Publishes Map**: The `map_server` node reads a YAML + PGM (Portable Gray Map) image pair from disk and publishes the occupancy grid on the `/map` topic (type: `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`). 2. **LIDAR Publishes Scans**: The `rplidarNode` or `hlds_laser_publisher` node publishes raw laser ranges on `/scan` (type: `sensor_msgs/LaserScan`) at the configured scan rate. 3. **Odometry Publishes Pose Estimates**: The `robot_state_publisher` and `diff_drive_controller` nodes publish coarse motion estimates on `/odom` (type: `nav_msgs/Odometry`). 4. **AMCL Subscribes & Fuses Data**: The `amcl` node (from the `ros-navigation` package) subscribes to `/map`, `/scan`, `/odom`, and `/initialpose`. It maintains a particle cloud approximating the robot's belief distribution: $bel(x_t) = \{x_t^{[1]}, x_t^{[2]}, \dots, x_t^{[M]}\}$. 5. **TF Tree Publication**: AMCL publishes the transform `map → odom` on `/tf`. The full TF chain becomes `map → odom → base_footprint → base_link → laser_frame`. 6. **RViz Visualization**: The `rviz` node subscribes to all topics and renders the robot, its particle cloud, the LIDAR scan rays, and the occupancy grid map in 3D. ### 2.1.1 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why a particle filter?** Because LIDAR measurements are non-Gaussian (clutter, occlusions, dynamic objects), and Kalman Filters assume linearity/Gaussianity — particles handle multimodal beliefs elegantly. - **Why the TF tree?** Every sensor reading and actuator command must be expressed in a consistent coordinate frame; ROS's TF library maintains this geometric consistency using a directed acyclic graph. --- ## 2.2 The Bayes Filter Foundation Mobile robot localization is mathematically formalized as a recursive **Bayes filter**: $$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$ Where: - $bel(x_t)$ is the robot's belief over pose $x_t$ at time $t$. - $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ is the **measurement model** — likelihood of observing scan $z_t$ given pose $x_t$. - $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ is the **motion model** — probability of arriving at $x_t$ after executing control $u_t$ from $x_{t-1}$. - $\eta$ is the normalizer ensuring the distribution integrates to 1. For Monte Carlo Localization, this integral is approximated by a **weighted particle set**: $$bel(x_t) \approx \{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{M}$$ Where $\sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} = 1$ and $M$ is the number of particles (default in AMCL: **5000**). --- ## 2.3 LIDAR Measurement Model A 2D LIDAR scan at time $t$ consists of $K$ range-bearing pairs: $$z_t = \{z_t^{[1]}, z_t^{[2]}, \dots, z_t^{[K]}\}, \quad z_t^{[k]} = (r_t^{[k]}, \phi_t^{[k]})$$ Each ray originates from the LIDAR's origin frame and intersects an obstacle at range $r_t^{[k]}$ along bearing $\phi_t^{[k]}$ relative to the laser frame. ### 2.3.1 Converting Laser Scan to Cartesian Points (in laser frame) For each ray $k$: $$x_{\text{laser}}^{[k]} = r_t^{[k]} \cos\phi_t^{[k]}, \quad y_{\text{laser}}^{[k]} = r_t^{[k]} \sin\phi_t^{[k]}$$ To express these in the **map frame**, apply the robot's pose $(x, y, \theta)$ via the homogeneous transformation: $$\begin{bmatrix} x_{\text{map}} \\ y_{\text{map}} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_{\text{laser}} \\ y_{\text{laser}} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ --- ## 2.4 ⚡ KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table** — every KTU 2024 Scheme Robotics Lab viva and written exam draws from these equations. | # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Used For | |---|--------------------|-----------|-------|----------| | 1 | LIDAR range from ToF | $r = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | meters | Range computation | | 2 | Polar → Cartesian (laser frame) | $x_l = r\cos\phi,\ y_l = r\sin\phi$ | meters | Laser scan projection | | 3 | Pose transformation (2D) | $\begin{bmatrix}x\\y\\\theta\end{bmatrix}_{\text{map}}$ via $2\times 2$ rotation matrix | meters, rad | Frame conversion | | 4 | Bayes filter update | $bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t\mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | unitless | Belief recursion | | 5 | Importance weight (MCL) | $w_t^{[i]} = p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$ | unitless | Particle reweighting | | 6 | Effective sample size | $N_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{M}(w_t^{[i]})^2}$ | particles | Resampling trigger | | 7 | Likelihood-field range | $d = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$ | meters | Scan-to-map distance | | 8 | Gaussian likelihood | $p(z_t \mid x_t) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-d^2 / 2\sigma^2}$ | unitless | Beam likelihood | | 9 | Odometry motion (diff-drive) | $\Delta x = \Delta s \cos(\theta + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2})$ | meters | Pose propagation | | 10 | AMCL TF broadcast | $T_{\text{map} \to \text{odom}} = T_{\text{map} \to \text{base}} \cdot (T_{\text{odom} \to \text{base}})^{-1}$ | transform | TF tree update | | 11 | Particle count adaptation | $M_{t+1} = \max(M_{\min}, \min(M_{\max}, \alpha / N_{\text{eff}}))$ | particles | KLD-sampling in AMCL | | 12 | Map resolution | $\text{cells/m} = 1 / r_{\text{res}}$ | $\text{m}^{-1}$ | Occupancy grid scaling | > **Note on Units**: All trigonometric functions in ROS use **radians**. Always convert degrees using $\theta_{\text{rad}} = \theta_{\text{deg}} \cdot \pi/180$. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility This localization stack is the **core perception subsystem** of: - **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs)** in Amazon fulfillment warehouses (Kiva/Proteus systems). - **Autonomous Vehicles** — Waymo and Cruise use 64-beam 3D LIDARs ($10\text{ Hz}$ rotation, $\approx \$75{,}000$ per unit) with similar particle-filter-based localizers. - **Domestic Vacuum Robots** — Roomba and Roborock use low-cost 1D LIDARs and simplified MCL. - **Agricultural Robotics** — autonomous tractors localize using LIDAR + GPS fusion. - **Disaster Response** — Boston Dynamics Spot uses LIDAR-based localization for search-and-rescue. --- # 🟢 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — LIDAR Range from Time-of-Flight A laser pulse leaves the LIDAR at $t_{\text{emit}}$, reflects off an obstacle, and returns at $t_{\text{recv}}$. The pulse travels a total path length of $2r$ (round trip). **Step 1:** Total distance travelled by the photon: $$d_{\text{total}} = 2r$$ **Step 2:** Using the relation distance = speed × time: $$2r = c \cdot \Delta t, \quad \Delta t = t_{\text{recv}} - t_{\text{emit}}$$ **Step 3:** Solving for the range: $$r = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$ **Step 4:** Numerical example — if the measured $\Delta t = 23.3 \text{ ns}$ (TurtleBot3 LDS-01 hitting a wall at 3.5 m): $$\Delta t = \frac{2 \times 3.5}{3 \times 10^8} = 2.33 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s} = 23.3 \text{ ns}$$ This confirms the laser scanner's reported range. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — 2D Pose Transformation (Map Frame from Laser Frame) **Given:** - Robot pose in map frame: $x = (x, y, \theta)^{\top}$ - Laser-frame point: $p_l = (x_l, y_l)^{\top}$ **Step 1:** Write the rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}(\theta)$ for counter-clockwise rotation by $\theta$: $$\mathbf{R}(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2:** Apply rotation to laser-frame coordinates: $$\begin{aligned} x' &= x_l \cos\theta - y_l \sin\theta \\ y' &= x_l \sin\theta + y_l \cos\theta \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Translate by robot position $(x, y)$: $$\begin{aligned} x_{\text{map}} &= x + x_l \cos\theta - y_l \sin\theta \\ y_{\text{map}} &= y + x_l \sin\theta + y_l \cos\theta \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4:** Compact matrix form using homogeneous coordinates: $$\begin{bmatrix} x_{\text{map}} \\ y_{\text{map}} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_l \\ y_l \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ This is precisely the homogeneous transformation $\mathbf{T}_{\text{map} \to \text{laser}}^{-1}(\theta, x, y)$ used by AMCL to project each scan ray into the map. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Particle Filter Weight Update (MCL Core) **Given:** At time $t-1$, we have $M$ particles $\{x_{t-1}^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{M}$, each with weight $w_{t-1}^{[i]}$. We receive a LIDAR scan $z_t$ and odometry $u_t$. **Step 1 (Prediction):** Sample new pose for each particle using the motion model: $$x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$$ For a differential-drive robot with odometry $(\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta\theta)$, this means: $$\begin{aligned} x_t^{[i]} &= x_{t-1}^{[i]} + \Delta x \cos\theta_{t-1}^{[i]} - \Delta y \sin\theta_{t-1}^{[i]} \\ y_t^{[i]} &= y_{t-1}^{[i]} + \Delta x \sin\theta_{t-1}^{[i]} + \Delta y \cos\theta_{t-1}^{[i]} \\ \theta_t^{[i]} &= \theta_{t-1}^{[i]} + \Delta\theta \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 (Update / Reweighting):** For each particle, evaluate the measurement likelihood: $$w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$$ For the **likelihood-field model** (used in AMCL), for each scan endpoint $z_t^{[k]} = (x_{z_k}, y_{z_k})$ projected into the map: $$p(z_t^{[k]} \mid x_t, m) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{d_{\text{map}}^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$ where $d_{\text{map}}$ is the distance from the projected ray endpoint to the nearest occupied cell in the map. The total likelihood for the full scan is the product (in log-space, the sum): $$\log p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \log p(z_t^{[k]} \mid x_t, m)$$ **Step 3 (Normalization):** Ensure weights sum to 1: $$w_t^{[i]} \leftarrow \frac{w_t^{[i]}}{\sum_{j=1}^{M} w_t^{[j]}}$$ **Step 4 (Resampling):** If $N_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{M}(w_t^{[i]})^2} < M/2$, draw $M$ new particles with replacement, each particle $x_t^{[i]}$ selected with probability proportional to $w_t^{[i]}$. Set all new weights to $1/M$. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Effective Sample Size & Resampling Criterion **Step 1:** Define weight variance measure. If all particles have equal weight $1/M$, then $\sum (w_t^{[i]})^2 = M \cdot (1/M)^2 = 1/M$, giving $N_{\text{eff}} = M$ (maximum). **Step 2:** If all weight concentrates on one particle, $\sum (w_t^{[i]})^2 \to 1$, giving $N_{\text{eff}} \to 1$ (minimum — particle degeneracy). **Step 3:** General expression: $$N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{M} (w_t^{[i]})^2}$$ **Step 4:** Standard AMCL threshold — resample when $N_{\text{eff}} < 0.5 \cdot M$. This is the same threshold used in the seminal **Fox et al. (1999)** Monte Carlo Localization paper. --- ## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Single Particle Likelihood **Scenario:** A particle hypothesizes robot pose $x = (2.0, 3.0, \pi/4)$ in a map. One LIDAR ray returns range $r = 1.5$ m at bearing $\phi = 0^{\circ}$ (robot-frame forward). **Step 1:** Compute ray endpoint in laser frame: $$x_l = 1.5 \cos 0 = 1.5, \quad y_l = 1.5 \sin 0 = 0$$ **Step 2:** Transform to map frame with $\theta = \pi/4$, $x = 2.0$, $y = 3.0$: $$\begin{aligned} x_{\text{map}} &= 2.0 + 1.5 \cos(\pi/4) - 0 \cdot \sin(\pi/4) = 2.0 + 1.5 \cdot 0.7071 = 3.061 \\ y_{\text{map}} &= 3.0 + 1.5 \sin(\pi/4) + 0 \cdot \cos(\pi/4) = 3.0 + 1.061 = 4.061 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Query map at $(3.061, 4.061)$ — nearest obstacle distance $d_{\text{map}} = 0.08$ m. With $\sigma = 0.2$ m: $$p(z \mid x, m) = \frac{1}{0.2 \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{0.08^2}{2 \cdot 0.2^2}\right) = 1.995 \cdot e^{-0.08} \approx 1.845$$ **Step 4:** Log-likelihood for K = 360 rays (typical), if average per-ray log-likelihood = $-0.5$: $$\log p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = 360 \cdot (-0.5) = -180$$ Particles with higher likelihoods (lower $d_{\text{map}}$ averaged across all rays) receive larger weights. --- ## 3.6 Summary of Mathematical Toolkit | Concept | Core Operator | Frequency in KTU Exam | |---------|---------------|------------------------| | Coordinate transformation | $SE(2)$ rotation + translation matrix | Very High | | Bayes filter recursion | Discrete integral approximation | Very High | | Particle weight update | Importance sampling | High | | Likelihood-field distance | Euclidean nearest-neighbor search | High | | Effective sample size | Inverse weight-squared sum | Medium | | TF chain composition | $4 \times 4$ homogeneous matrix multiplication | Medium | --- # 🟢 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ROS TF Tree for Localized Mobile Robot The following Mermaid diagram shows the complete coordinate-frame hierarchy used during LIDAR localization in ROS. ```mermaid graph TD nodeM["MAP FRAME

It is published exclusively by the AMCL node based on LIDAR-to-map scan matching. --- ## 4.2 AMCL Node Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow ```mermaid graph LR nodeMAP["MAP SERVER

[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** **Define LIDAR-based localization of a mobile robot. State any two advantages of using LIDAR over vision-based sensors for indoor localization.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1 mark):** LIDAR-based localization is the process of estimating the pose $(x, y, \theta)$ of a mobile robot within a known map by matching laser range scans against the map using a probabilistic algorithm such as AMCL. **Advantages (2 marks — any two):** 1. **Range accuracy** — LIDAR provides sub-centimeter distance measurements independent of ambient lighting, unlike cameras which fail in darkness or glare. 2. **360° field of view in a single sensor** — 2D LIDARs cover the full azimuth in one rotation, whereas a single camera has a limited FOV ($\approx 60°$–$90°$). 3. **Direct metric output** — Range data is already in metric units; vision requires calibration and depth estimation. 4. **Computational efficiency** — Scan matching is faster than visual feature extraction and matching. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Explain the role of the `map_server` and `amcl` nodes in ROS Navigation stack. Mention the topics they subscribe to.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Node | Role | Publishes | Subscribes | |------|------|-----------|------------| | `map_server` | Loads occupancy grid (PGM + YAML) from disk | `/map` (`nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`) | Service `/static_map` | | `amcl` | Tracks robot pose using particle filter | `/amcl_pose`, `/tf` (`map→odom`) | `/map`, `/scan`, `/odom`, `/initialpose` | **Explanation (1 mark):** The `map_server` acts as the static map provider, while `amcl` continuously fuses LIDAR scans with odometry to correct the robot's pose estimate, publishing the `map → odom` TF transform. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by **(i)** forgetting to draw the TF tree, **(ii)** writing only the formula without units, **(iii)** omitting the difference between **localization** and **SLAM**, and **(iv)** not specifying ROS topic names with their message types. Always include topic names, message types, and units — examiners explicitly check for these. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** **(a) With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of the ROS Navigation stack for LIDAR-based localization. List all major nodes, topics, and the TF tree. (7 marks)** **(b) Derive the Bayes filter update equation used in Monte Carlo Localization and explain how the importance weight of each particle is updated when a new LIDAR scan arrives. (7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** **Block diagram (4 marks):** The ROS Navigation stack for LIDAR localization consists of the following interconnected nodes: 1. **Sensor Driver Layer:** - `rplidarNode` or `hlds_laser_publisher` → publishes on `/scan` (`sensor_msgs/LaserScan`) - `robot_state_publisher` → publishes on `/tf` and `/joint_states` - `diff_drive_controller` → publishes on `/odom` (`nav_msgs/Odometry`) 2. **Map Layer:** - `map_server` → publishes on `/map` (`nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`) 3. **Localization Layer:** - `amcl` → subscribes to `/scan`, `/map`, `/odom`, `/initialpose` - Publishes on `/amcl_pose`, `/particlecloud`, and `/tf` (specifically `map → odom`) 4. **Visualization Layer:** - `rviz` → subscribes to all topics above and renders the robot, particles, scan, and map **TF Tree (2 marks):** ``` map → odom → base_footprint → base_link → laser ``` The `map → odom` link is **the only transform published by AMCL**. It represents the localization correction. The `odom → base_footprint` link is published by the robot state publisher using wheel encoder data. **Topic message types (1 mark):** - `/scan` → `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` - `/map` → `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` - `/odom` → `nav_msgs/Odometry` - `/amcl_pose` → `geometry_msgs/PoseWithCovarianceStamped` --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** **Derivation (5 marks):** Starting from the recursive Bayes filter: $$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$ Apply Bayes' rule: $$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$ By the Markov assumption (current state depends only on previous state and current control): $$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$$ Substituting into the integral form: $$bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$ **Particle representation (2 marks):** For Monte Carlo Localization, the continuous belief is approximated by $M$ weighted samples: $$bel(x_t) \approx \{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{M}, \quad \sum_{i} w_t^{[i]} = 1$$ Each particle $x_t^{[i]}$ is a hypothesis pose $(x, y, \theta)$. The importance weight update is: $$w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$$ For the **likelihood-field model** used by AMCL: $$p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{d_{\text{map}}^2(x_t^{[i]}, z_t^{[k]})}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$ This completes the derivation. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **(a) Differentiate between Localization and SLAM. Explain the AMCL algorithm with its three main steps — Prediction, Update, and Resampling. (7 marks)** **(b) A 2D LIDAR mounted on a robot at pose $(2, 3, \pi/4)$ in the map frame returns a range of $2.5$ m at bearing $0.3$ rad in the laser frame. Compute the coordinates of the obstacle in the map frame. Given the nearest occupied cell is at $(3.78, 5.05)$, compute the likelihood-field probability assuming $\sigma = 0.15$ m. (7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** **Difference (2 marks):** | Aspect | Localization | SLAM | |--------|--------------|------| | Map availability | Map is **known a priori** | Map is **built simultaneously** | | Input | Map + LIDAR + Odometry | LIDAR + Odometry only | | Output | Robot pose | Robot pose + Map | | Computational cost | Lower | Higher | | Algorithm example | AMCL | GMapping, Cartographer, Hector SLAM | | Use case | Warehouse AMRs with pre-built maps | Exploration, unknown environments | **AMCL Algorithm (5 marks):** **Step 1 — Prediction (Motion Update):** Using odometry $u_t$, propagate each particle: $$x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$$ For a differential-drive robot: $$x_t^{[i]} = x_{t-1}^{[i]} + \Delta s \cos(\theta_{t-1}^{[i]} + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2})$$ $$y_t^{[i]} = y_{t-1}^{[i]} + \Delta s \sin(\theta_{t-1}^{[i]} + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2})$$ $$\theta_t^{[i]} = \theta_{t-1}^{[i]} + \Delta\theta$$ **Step 2 — Update (Measurement Update):** Compute likelihood for each particle given new scan: $$w_t^{[i]} = p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$$ **Step 3 — Resampling:** Compute effective sample size: $$N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{M} (w_t^{[i]})^2}$$ If $N_{\text{eff}} < M/2$, resample with replacement proportional to weights. AMCL additionally uses **KLD-sampling** to dynamically adapt the particle count between $M_{\min}$ and $M_{\max}$. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** **Part 1 — Compute obstacle in map frame (3 marks):** Given: $x = 2$, $y = 3$, $\theta = \pi/4$, $r = 2.5$, $\phi = 0.3$ rad. **Step 1:** Convert to laser-frame Cartesian: $$x_l = 2.5 \cos(0.3) = 2.5 \times 0.9553 = 2.388 \text{ m}$$ $$y_l = 2.5 \sin(0.3) = 2.5 \times 0.2955 = 0.739 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2:** Transform to map frame using rotation by $\pi/4$: $$x_{\text{map}} = 2 + 2.388 \cos(\pi/4) - 0.739 \sin(\pi/4)$$ $$x_{\text{map}} = 2 + 2.388 \times 0.7071 - 0.739 \times 0.7071$$ $$x_{\text{map}} = 2 + 1.689 - 0.523 = 3.166 \text{ m}$$ $$y_{\text{map}} = 3 + 2.388 \sin(\pi/4) + 0.739 \cos(\pi/4)$$ $$y_{\text{map}} = 3 + 2.388 \times 0.7071 + 0.739 \times 0.7071$$ $$y_{\text{map}} = 3 + 1.689 + 0.523 = 5.212 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 (verification against obstacle):** Predicted obstacle $(3.166, 5.212)$ is close to the nearest occupied cell $(3.78, 5.05)$, confirming a valid match. **Part 2 — Likelihood-field probability (4 marks):** **Step 1:** Compute Euclidean distance: $$d_{\text{map}} = \sqrt{(3.78 - 3.166)^2 + (5.05 - 5.212)^2}$$ $$d_{\text{map}} = \sqrt{(0.614)^2 + (-0.162)^2} = \sqrt{0.377 + 0.026} = \sqrt{0.403} = 0.635 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2:** Apply Gaussian likelihood with $\sigma = 0.15$ m: $$p(z \mid x, m) = \frac{1}{0.15 \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{0.635^2}{2 \times 0.15^2}\right)$$ $$p(z \mid x, m) = \frac{1}{0.3754} \exp\left(-\frac{0.403}{0.045}\right)$$ $$p(z \mid x, m) = 2.664 \times \exp(-8.96)$$ $$p(z \mid x, m) = 2.664 \times 1.28 \times 10^{-4} \approx 3.41 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 3:** Interpretation — this low likelihood indicates the hypothesized pose is **unlikely**. The particle's weight should be reduced accordingly, and after normalization, AMCL will resample particles closer to the true pose. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Robotics Lab Exam** - ✅ **LIDAR range formula**: $r = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$, where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. - ✅ **Polar to Cartesian (laser frame)**: $x_l = r\cos\phi$, $y_l = r\sin\phi$. - ✅ **2D pose transformation**: Use $SE(2)$ homogeneous matrix — know the $3 \times 3$ form. - ✅ **Bayes filter**: $bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$. - ✅ **Particle weight update**: $w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$. - ✅ **Effective sample size**: $N_{\text{eff}} = 1 / \sum (w_t^{[i]})^2$; resample if $N_{\text{eff}} < M/2$. - ✅ **AMCL** = Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization; uses KLD-sampling for dynamic particle count. - ✅ **TF tree**: `map → odom → base_footprint → base_link → laser_frame`. - ✅ **Only AMCL publishes** the `map → odom` transform. - ✅ **Key ROS topics**: `/scan` (`sensor_msgs/LaserScan`), `/map` (`nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`), `/odom` (`nav_msgs/Odometry`), `/amcl_pose` (`geometry_msgs/PoseWithCovarianceStamped`). - ✅ **Localization vs SLAM**: Localization assumes a known map; SLAM builds the map simultaneously. - ✅ **Likelihood-field model** is preferred over beam model in AMCL for computational efficiency. - ✅ **TurtleBot3 LDS-01** range: 0.12 m to 3.5 m, scan rate 5 Hz, 360° field of view. - ✅ **Default AMCL particle count**: 5000 (min), adapts up to KLD-sampled maximum. - ✅ **Speed of light**: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s — memorize exactly for time-stamp conversions.

topics.title: Implementing a Weather Station Using Raspberry Pi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAL607 - ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implementing a Weather Station Using Raspberry Pi

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Raspberry Pi–based Weather Station** is an embedded, Internet-of-Things (IoT) data acquisition system that integrates a Single-Board Computer (SBC) with multiple environmental transducers to continuously measure, process, log, and transmit atmospheric parameters such as temperature, relative humidity, barometric pressure, wind speed, wind direction, rainfall, and solar irradiance. The system converts physical phenomena into standardized engineering units, performs time-stamped data logging (locally on SD storage and/or remotely on cloud platforms), and exposes readings via web dashboards, APIs, or MQTT topics.

From the perspective of **KTU PCCAL607 (Robotics Lab)**, this experiment is classified under *Perception & Sensor Interfacing in Robotic Systems*, and it directly maps to the following Course Outcomes (COs):

- **CO2**: Interface various sensors and actuators with the Raspberry Pi using standard communication protocols (GPIO, I2C, SPI, UART).
- **CO3**: Design and implement data acquisition, conditioning, and visualization pipelines for robotic/embedded applications.
- **CO5**: Develop IoT-enabled robotic systems integrating cloud telemetry and real-time monitoring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The experiment tests the ability to (a) wire multiple heterogeneous sensors, (b) read digitized sensor data, (c) convert raw counts into engineering units, and (d) present data on a dashboard. Marks are awarded for **circuit correctness, conversion formulae, calibration logic, and visualization output.**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine your home has a **smart assistant** that constantly whispers the weather — "It's 31°C, 78% humid, raining lightly, wind from the west at 12 km/h." A Raspberry Pi weather station is the *physical brain* of that assistant.

| Human Analogy | Weather Station Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Skin sensing heat | **DHT22 / DS18B20** temperature sensor |
| Skin sensing moisture | **DHT22** humidity sensor |
| Eardrum feeling pressure changes | **BMP280** barometric pressure sensor |
| Eyes seeing raindrops | **Rain gauge / tipping bucket** |
| Hair feeling the wind | **Anemometer** + **Wind vane** |
| Brain making sense of signals | **Raspberry Pi** microcontroller/SBC |
| Notebook recording observations | **CSV / SQLite / Cloud database** |
| Telling others what it sensed | **Web dashboard / MQTT publish** |

So, the **Raspberry Pi** acts as the *central nervous system* collecting signals from a network of sensory organs (sensors) and converting them into useful, storable, and shareable information.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Constants & Metrics Used in This Experiment (Bolded for Emphasis):**
> - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** **1013.25 hPa** (or **101325 Pa**)
> - **Standard Temperature:** **15°C** (or **288.15 K**)
> - **Operating Voltage:** **3.3 V** (Raspberry Pi GPIO is *not* 5 V tolerant on all pins)
> - **I²C Reference Clock:** **100 kHz (Standard) / 400 kHz (Fast Mode)**
> - **1-Wire Timing Base:** **µs-level precise timing for DS18B20**
> - **Resolution:** DHT22 = **0.1°C / 0.1% RH**; BMP280 = **0.01 hPa**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sensor Taxonomy Used in a Raspberry Pi Weather Station

A weather station typically uses a *heterogeneous sensor cluster*. Each sensor belongs to a specific category based on its output signal type and interfacing protocol.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Output Signal

- **Analog Sensors** (output a continuous voltage, 0–3.3 V): Raindrop sensor (resistive), soil moisture sensor, MQ-series gas sensors, wind vane (potentiometric).
- **Digital Sensors** (output a serialized digital word): DHT22 (single-wire protocol), DS18B20 (1-Wire), BMP280 (I²C/SPI), wind speed anemometer (pulse/frequency).

### 2.1.2 Classification by Communication Protocol

| Protocol | Sensors Commonly Used | Wires | Max Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GPIO (Bit-banged)** | DHT11/DHT22 | 1 data + VCC + GND | ~1 kHz |
| **1-Wire** | DS18B20 | 1 data + VCC + GND | 16.3 kbps |
| **I²C (TWI)** | BMP280, BME680, HTU21D | SDA, SCL + VCC + GND | 400 kHz / 3.4 MHz |
| **SPI** | MCP3008 (ADC), high-speed ADCs | 4 lines + VCC + GND | >10 MHz |
| **UART** | GPS modules, some dust sensors | TX, RX + VCC + GND | 115200 baud |
| **Pulse Counting** | Anemometer, rain gauge (tipping bucket) | 1 GPIO | Interrupt-based |

## 2.2 Operational Pipeline — The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

The weather station executes a **4-stage signal-processing pipeline**:

**Stage 1 — Transduction (Sensor):**
A physical quantity (e.g., temperature) acts on a sensing element (e.g., NTC thermistor in DHT22). The element's electrical property (resistance, capacitance, or voltage) changes proportionally.
- *Why?* Because all downstream computation depends on a reliable, repeatable input.
- *How?* Sensors are calibrated against a reference (e.g., ice-water bath = 0°C, boiling water = 100°C).

**Stage 2 — Signal Conditioning:**
- Analog signals may need **amplification**, **filtering** (RC low-pass to remove 50/60 Hz mains noise), or **level shifting** (5 V → 3.3 V to protect Pi GPIO).
- Digital sensors perform their own ADC internally; the Raspberry Pi only reads the digital word.
- *Why?* Because raw sensor outputs are often noisy, non-linear, and at incompatible voltage levels.

**Stage 3 — Data Acquisition & Unit Conversion:**
The Raspberry Pi applies **calibration polynomials** (often stored in the sensor's EEPROM) to convert raw counts into engineering units. For example, the BMP280 stores 16-bit compensation coefficients ($c_0 \dots c_8$) used in a piecewise linear / polynomial fit.

**Stage 4 — Logging, Visualization, and Transmission:**
Data is time-stamped (using `NTP` for clock sync) and persisted to a `.csv`, **SQLite** database, or pushed to a cloud (e.g., ThingSpeak, AWS IoT, Blynk, Grafana). A local web server (e.g., Flask + Chart.js) provides a dashboard.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all the high-yield formulae** a KTU student must memorize for this experiment. Note the use of `\vert` (instead of `|`) to keep table syntax valid.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Temperature Conversion (°C → K) | $T_K = T_C + 273.15$ | K | Add 273.15 |
| 2 | Temperature Conversion (°F → °C) | $T_C = \dfrac{(T_F - 32) \times 5}{9}$ | °C | Standard NIST formula |
| 3 | Heat Index (Rothfusz, simplified) | $HI = -42.379 + 2.04901523\,T + 10.14333127\,R - 0.22475541\,TR - 0.00683783\,T^2 - 0.05481717\,R^2 + 0.00122874\,T^2 R + 0.00085282\,T R^2 - 0.00000199\,T^2 R^2$ | °F | $T$ in °F, $R$ in %; valid for $T \geq 80°F$, $R \geq 40\%$ |
| 4 | Dew Point (Magnus approximation) | $T_d = \dfrac{b \cdot \gamma(T,R)}{a - \gamma(T,R)}$, where $\gamma = \dfrac{a\,T}{b + T} + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{R}{100}\right)$ | °C | $a = 17.625$, $b = 243.04$°C |
| 5 | Pressure Altitude | $h = 44330 \cdot \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)^{1/5.255}\right]$ | m | $P_0 = 1013.25$ hPa |
| 6 | Barometric Formula (troposphere) | $P = P_0 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{L\,h}{T_0}\right)^{g\,M / (R^\star L)}$ | Pa | $L = 0.0065$ K/m, $g = 9.80665$ m/s², $M = 0.02896$ kg/mol, $R^\star = 8.31447$ J/(mol·K) |
| 7 | Wind Chill (NWS, 2001) | $WC = 35.74 + 0.6215\,T - 35.75\,V^{0.16} + 0.4275\,T\,V^{0.16}$ | °F | $T$ in °F, $V$ in mph; valid for $T \leq 50°F$, $V \geq 3$ mph |
| 8 | Wind Speed Conversion | $V_{ms} = V_{kmh} \cdot \dfrac{5}{18}$ | m/s | $5/18 = 0.2778$ |
| 9 | Anemometer (3-cup) Pulses → Speed | $V = \dfrac{n \cdot 2\pi r}{N \cdot t}$ | m/s | $n$ = pulses, $r$ = cup radius, $N$ = pulses per rev, $t$ = time |
| 10 | Rainfall (Tipping Bucket) | $R = \dfrac{\text{tips} \times V_{bucket}}{A_{collector}}$ | mm | $V_{bucket}$ typically 0.2 mm per tip |
| 11 | ADC Resolution | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ | V | For MCP3008: $V_{ref}=3.3$V, $n=10$ → $V_{LSB} \approx 3.226$ mV |
| 12 | I²C Pull-up Resistor (rule of thumb) | $R_{pull} \approx \dfrac{t_r}{0.8473 \times C_{bus}}$ | Ω | $t_r$ = rise time, $C_{bus}$ in F; typical 4.7 kΩ |
| 13 | Absolute Humidity from RH & T | $AH = \dfrac{216.7 \cdot \left(\dfrac{R}{100} \cdot \dfrac{6.112 \cdot e^{\frac{17.62\,T}{243.12+T}}}{273.15+T}\right)}{273.15+T}$ | g/m³ | Tetens formula based |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Raspberry Pi weather stations are deployed in:
- **Precision agriculture** (micro-climate monitoring for greenhouses and open fields).
- **Disaster early-warning systems** (flood/cyclone warning, river-basin rainfall networks).
- **Academic research** (citizen-science projects like *Weather Underground* personal weather stations).
- **Industrial HVAC control** (closed-loop ventilation based on temperature/humidity/CO₂).
- **Drone/UAV meteorology** (lightweight onboard sensing for atmospheric profiling).

> [!NOTE]
> A *practical* example: a farmer in Palakkad can run a Pi weather station powered by a **solar panel + 12 V battery**, posting data every 60 s to a dashboard, triggering an SMS via `Twilio` if rainfall exceeds 50 mm/h — preventing crop damage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides **exhaustive, board-quality derivations** of the most commonly tested formulae. Each algebraic step is shown explicitly with the underlying engineering reasoning.

## 3.1 Derivation: DHT22 Raw Counts → Temperature & Humidity

The DHT22 transmits a **40-bit data frame**: 16-bit integer + 16-bit decimal for humidity, followed by 16-bit integer + 16-bit decimal for temperature, plus an 8-bit checksum. The Raspberry Pi decodes this frame via a custom bit-banging protocol.

**Step 1 — Frame Reception:**
The sensor pulls the data line LOW for 80 µs (start signal), then HIGH for 80 µs (response), then transmits 40 bits. Each bit begins with a 50 µs LOW pulse, followed by a HIGH pulse whose duration encodes the bit value:
- 26–28 µs HIGH → logic **0**
- 70 µs HIGH → logic **1**

**Step 2 — Humidity Conversion:**

$$
H_{RH} \;[\%] = \dfrac{H_{16}}{10}
$$

where $H_{16}$ is the 16-bit humidity word. For example, raw $H_{16} = 678$ means $67.8\%$ RH.

**Step 3 — Temperature Conversion:**

$$
T_{C} \;[^{\circ}C] = \dfrac{T_{16}}{10}
$$

If the most significant bit (MSB) of the temperature word is set, the temperature is *negative* (two's complement). Example: raw $T_{16} = 253$ → $T = 25.3°$C; raw $T_{16} = 0xFFEC} = -20$ → $T = -2.0°$C.

**Step 4 — Checksum Verification:**

$$
CS = H_{byte1} + H_{byte2} + T_{byte1} + T_{byte2} \pmod{256}
$$

If $CS \neq$ received checksum, discard the frame (noise rejection).

## 3.2 Derivation: BMP280 Raw Pressure → Engineering Units (hPa)

The BMP280 stores **factory-calibrated coefficients** $T_1, T_2, T_3$ (temperature) and $P_1 \dots P_9$ (pressure) in its 0x88–0x9F register bank. The Bosch reference implementation uses a 32-bit fixed-point polynomial.

**Step 1 — Read raw ADC values:** $T_{adc}$ (24-bit) and $P_{adc}$ (24-bit, including 16-bit pressure + lower bits of temperature for oversampling consistency in some configurations).

**Step 2 — Fine temperature (must be computed first, as pressure compensation depends on it):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{var1} &= \left(\dfrac{T_{adc}}{8} - (T_1 \cdot 2^1)\right) \cdot \dfrac{T_2}{2^{11}} \\
\text{var2} &= \left(\dfrac{T_{adc}}{16} - T_1\right)^2 \cdot \dfrac{T_3}{2^{14}} \\
t_{fine} &= \text{var1} + \text{var2} \quad [\text{used in both } T \text{ and } P] \\
T \;[^{\circ}C] &= \dfrac{t_{fine}}{5120}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Engineering Intuition:* The "fine" temperature $t_{fine}$ is a calibration-adjusted internal variable that captures the *non-linearity* of the MEMS piezoresistive sensor. Skipping this step (a common student mistake) yields pressure errors of several hPa.

**Step 3 — Pressure compensation using $t_{fine}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{var1} &= 2 \cdot t_{fine} - 64000 \\
\text{var2} &= \text{var1}^2 \cdot \dfrac{P_6}{2^{17}} + \text{var1} \cdot P_5 \cdot 2^{-1} + P_4 \cdot 2^{14} \\
\text{var3} &= (P_3 \cdot 2^{11}) + \left( \text{var2} \cdot P_2 + 2^{14} \right) \cdot P_1 \cdot 2^{-14} \\
P_{raw} &= (P_{adc} - \text{var3}) \cdot \dfrac{2^{15}}{\left( \text{var1}^2 \cdot \dfrac{P_9}{2^{12}} + \text{var1} \cdot P_8 + P_7 \right) \cdot 2^{4} + 2^{20}} \\
P \;[Pa] &= P_{raw} + \text{var1} \cdot P_{10} + P_{11}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Engineering Intuition:* This multi-stage polynomial is necessary because MEMS pressure sensors exhibit **non-linear thermal drift** — without compensation, an unheated sensor can drift by 10+ hPa across a 30°C swing, which is unacceptable for altimetry.

## 3.3 Derivation: Heat Index (Rothfusz)

The Heat Index combines air temperature and relative humidity to express the *perceived* temperature. The KTU-relevant simplified polynomial was developed by Rothfusz (1990).

**Step 1 — Convert inputs to °F:**

$$
T_F = \dfrac{9}{5} T_C + 32
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the polynomial (only if $T_F \geq 80$ and $R \geq 40$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HI &= -42.379 \;+\; 2.04901523\,T_F \;+\; 10.14333127\,R \\
   &\quad - 0.22475541\,T_F R \;-\; 0.00683783\,T_F^2 \;-\; 0.05481717\,R^2 \\
   &\quad + 0.00122874\,T_F^2 R \;+\; 0.00085282\,T_F R^2 \;-\; 0.00000199\,T_F^2 R^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert back to °C (KTU/Kerala convention):**

$$
HI_C = \dfrac{5}{9}(HI - 32)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numeric Example:** $T = 35°$C $= 95°$F, $R = 70\%$. Plugging into the polynomial yields $HI \approx 124°$F $= 51.1°$C. So at 35°C with 70% humidity, the body feels ~51°C — a critical warning for outdoor workers.

## 3.4 Derivation: Pressure Altitude (Barometric Altimeter)

For a hobbyist weather station, the *pressure altitude* formula is derived from the **International Standard Atmosphere (ISA)** model. It is essential for drone launches and weather-balloon data.

**Step 1 — Start from the hydrostatic equation:** $dP = -\rho g\,dh$.

**Step 2 — Substitute the ideal gas law:** $\rho = \dfrac{P M}{R^\star T}$.

**Step 3 — Assume constant temperature lapse rate $L = 0.0065$ K/m:** $T(h) = T_0 - L h$.

**Step 4 — Integrate and solve for $h$:**

$$
h = \dfrac{T_0}{L} \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{P}{P_0} \right)^{R^\star L / (g M)} \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Plug standard values** ($T_0 = 288.15$ K, $L = 0.0065$ K/m, $g = 9.80665$ m/s², $M = 0.0289644$ kg/mol, $R^\star = 8.31447$ J/(mol·K)):

$$
\dfrac{R^\star L}{g M} \approx 0.190284 \approx \dfrac{1}{5.255}
$$

Hence the **engineering-friendly form**:

$$
h = 44330 \cdot \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{P}{1013.25} \right)^{1/5.255} \right] \quad \text{[meters]}
$$

> *Engineering Intuition:* This is why your phone altimeter is "wrong" when a cyclone passes — it measures pressure-derived altitude, not GPS altitude. Pressure altitude changes by ~8 m per hPa near sea level.

## 3.5 Derivation: Tipping-Bucket Rainfall → mm

A tipping-bucket rain gauge has a small *see-saw* bucket. Each tip = 0.2 mm of rainfall (factory calibrated). The Pi counts GPIO interrupts.

**Step 1 — Count tips** $N$ in a fixed interval $\Delta t$ (typically 1 minute or 1 hour).

**Step 2 — Convert to mm:**

$$
R_{mm} = N \times 0.2 \; [\text{mm}]
$$

**Step 3 — Compute rain rate (mm/h):**

$$
\text{Rate} = \dfrac{N \times 0.2 \times 60}{\Delta t_{min}} \quad [\text{mm/h}]
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering correction for undercounting in heavy rain:** The bucket has a *finite tip time* (~0.3 s). During very heavy rain, the bucket can fill mid-tip and lose counts. Correction factor:

$$
R_{corrected} = R_{measured} \cdot \left( 1 + \dfrac{R_{measured}}{114} \right) \quad \text{(per WMO recommendations)}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENS["SENSOR LAYER - Transducers"]
        s1["DHT22 - Temp and Humidity"]
        s2["BMP280 - Pressure"]
        s3["DS18B20 - Soil Temp"]
        s4["Anemometer - Wind Speed"]
        s5["Wind Vane - Direction"]
        s6["Rain Gauge - Tipping Bucket"]
        s7["Pyranometer - Solar Irradiance"]
    end

    subgraph COND["SIGNAL CONDITIONING LAYER"]
        c1["Level Shifter 5V to 3.3V"]
        c2["RC Low-Pass Filter"]
        c3["MCP3008 - 10 bit ADC"]
        c4["Pull-up Resistors 4.7k"]
    end

    subgraph CORE["PROCESSING LAYER - Raspberry Pi 4"]
        p1["GPIO Library - libgpiod"]
        p2["I2C Driver - smbus2"]
        p3["SPI Driver - spidev"]
        p4["Python App - Main Loop"]
        p5["SQLite Database - Local Log"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        o1["Flask Web Server"]
        o2["MQTT Broker - Mosquitto"]
        o3["Cloud Platform - ThingSpeak"]
        o4["OLED Display SSD1306"]
        o5["CSV File Logger"]
    end

    s1 --> c4
    s2 --> c4
    s3 --> c4
    s4 --> c2
    s5 --> c3
    s6 --> p1
    s7 --> c3

    c4 --> p1
    c1 --> p1
    c2 --> p1
    c3 --> p3

    p1 --> p4
    p2 --> p4
    p3 --> p4
    p4 --> p5
    p4 --> o1
    p4 --> o2
    p4 --> o3
    p4 --> o4
    p4 --> o5
```

## 4.2 I²C Bus Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pi["Raspberry Pi 4 - BCM2711"]
    p1["Pin 3 - SDA GPIO2"]
    p2["Pin 5 - SCL GPIO3"]
    pu1["R_pullup = 4.7k ohm to 3.3V"]
    pu2["R_pullup = 4.7k ohm to 3.3V"]
    dev1["BMP280 - Address 0x76"]
    dev2["OLED SSD1306 - Address 0x3C"]
    dev3["BME680 - Optional Addr 0x77"]
    vcc["3.3V Rail - Pin 1"]
    gnd["GND - Pin 6"]

    pi --> p1
    pi --> p2
    p1 --- pu1
    p2 --- pu2
    pu1 --- vcc
    pu2 --- vcc
    p1 --- dev1
    p2 --- dev1
    p1 --- dev2
    p2 --- dev2
    p1 --- dev3
    p2 --- dev3
    dev1 --- vcc
    dev2 --- vcc
    dev3 --- vcc
    dev1 --- gnd
    dev2 --- gnd
    dev3 --- gnd
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pull-up Rule:** I²C bus lines are *open-drain*. The Raspberry Pi has on-die pull-ups, but they are weak (~50 kΩ). For reliable operation with multiple devices on the bus, **external 4.7 kΩ pull-ups to 3.3 V are mandatory**. Using 5 V pull-ups will damage the Pi's GPIO over time.

## 4.3 Data Acquisition & Visualization Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start - Cron or Systemd Service"] --> B["Initialize Sensors - I2C and GPIO"]
    B --> C{"Read Sensors Loop Every 10s"}
    C --> D["Read DHT22 - Single Wire Protocol"]
    C --> E["Read BMP280 - I2C Burst Read"]
    C --> F["Read Anemometer - Interrupt Count"]
    C --> G["Read Rain Gauge - Interrupt Count"]
    D --> H["Convert Raw to Engineering Units"]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I["Timestamp via NTP"]
    I --> J["Write to SQLite Table"]
    I --> K["Publish to MQTT Topic"]
    I --> L["Update Flask Web UI"]
    I --> M["Append to CSV Log"]
    J --> N{"Any Alarm Threshold"}
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N -->|Yes| O["Send SMS Email via Twilio SMTP"]
    N -->|No| C
    O --> C
```

## 4.4 Sensor Wiring Schematic (ASCII Representation)

```
+---------------------+      +-----------------+      +-----------------+
|   Raspberry Pi 4    |      |   DHT22 Sensor  |      |   BMP280 Sensor |
|   (40-pin header)   |      |                 |      |                 |
+---------------------+      +-----------------+      +-----------------+
        |        |                  |  | |                   |  |  |  |
        |  Pin 1 |------------------|--| |--- 3.3V --------|  |  |  |
        |  Pin 6 |------------------|--| |--- GND ---------|--|--|--|
        | Pin 11 | GPIO17 ----------|--/ (Data)            |  |  |  |
        |  (3K3) |---/\/\/---+     |                      |  |  |  |
        |        |           |     |                      |  |  |  |
        |  Pin 3 | GPIO2  SDA -----------------------------/  |  |  |
        |  Pin 5 | GPIO3  SCL -------------------------------/  |  |
        |  Pin 1 | 3.3V  VCC -----------------------------------/  |
        |  Pin 6 | GND  GND --------------------------------------/
        |        |                                                |
        |  Pin 7 | GPIO4  ---- One-Wire bus (4.7k pull-up)        |
        |  Pin 1 | 3.3V   ---- DS18B20 VCC (parasitic optional)  |
        |  Pin 6 | GND     ---- DS18B20 GND                       |
        +--------+                                                |
                                                                  |
        +--------+        +-----------------+                     |
        |  Pin 12| GPIO18 | Anemometer OUT |----------------------+
        |  Pin 31| GPIO6  | Rain Gauge TIP |----------------------+
        +--------+        +-----------------+                     |
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Wiring Notes (Frequently Lost Marks in KTU Lab Exam):**
> 1. Use a **voltage divider** (e.g., 3.3 kΩ + 5.1 kΩ) when reading a 5 V anemometer output into a Pi GPIO. Connecting 5 V directly to a Pi input *will* damage the SoC.
> 2. The DHT22 data line needs a **single 4.7–10 kΩ pull-up to 3.3 V** — not to 5 V.
> 3. I²C SDA/SCL require pull-ups; if the BMP280 module has *no* on-board pull-ups, add external 4.7 kΩ resistors.
> 4. Enable I²C and 1-Wire via `sudo raspi-config` → *Interface Options* before running any code.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 model]`
**Explain the role of the Raspberry Pi as a Single-Board Computer (SBC) in a weather station. Why is it preferred over a microcontroller like Arduino for this application?**

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**
The Raspberry Pi functions as the *central processing and communication hub* of the weather station. It runs a full Linux OS, supports multi-tasking, native networking (Ethernet/Wi-Fi), and high-level languages (Python), making it ideal for interfacing multiple sensors, running a web server, and pushing data to cloud platforms. Unlike Arduino, the Pi can host a **Flask dashboard**, process **SQL databases**, and run **MQTT brokers** concurrently — capabilities essential for IoT-scale data logging, real-time visualization, and remote telemetry required in modern weather stations.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Dec 2024 model]`
**Differentiate between the I²C and SPI communication protocols used to interface sensors with the Raspberry Pi. State one sensor example for each.**

**Model Answer (in table form):**
- **I²C (Inter-Integrated Circuit):** 2-wire (SDA, SCL), supports multiple devices via 7-bit addressing, half-duplex, lower speed (up to 400 kHz in fast mode). *Example: BMP280 pressure sensor.*
- **SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface):** 4-wire (MOSI, MISO, SCLK, CS), full-duplex, much higher speed (>10 MHz), but uses one chip-select line per device. *Example: MCP3008 10-bit ADC for analog wind vane.*
- I²C uses fewer pins but requires pull-up resistors; SPI is faster but uses more GPIO pins.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 model]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a Raspberry Pi–based weather station**. List the sensors used and the communication protocol adopted for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A DHT22 sensor transmits the following 40-bit data frame (in hexadecimal, MSB first): `02 91 00 01 15 00 9C`. Decode the frame, verify the checksum, and compute the temperature in °C, °F, and Kelvin. Also compute the dew point using the Magnus formula (assume $a = 17.625$, $b = 243.04°$C). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Architecture & Sensor List (7 marks):**

The block diagram (as shown in **Section 4.1**) consists of four layers:

1. **Sensor Layer:** DHT22 (Temp+RH, single-wire GPIO), BMP280 (Pressure, I²C), DS18B20 (Soil temp, 1-Wire), Anemometer (Wind speed, GPIO interrupt), Wind vane (Direction, via MCP3008 ADC, SPI), Rain gauge (Tipping bucket, GPIO interrupt), Pyranometer (Solar radiation, ADC).
2. **Signal Conditioning:** Pull-up resistors (4.7 kΩ for I²C and 1-Wire), RC low-pass filter on anemometer, 5 V → 3.3 V level shifter, MCP3008 ADC.
3. **Processing Layer (Raspberry Pi 4):** Linux OS, Python app, libgpiod (GPIO), smbus2 (I²C), spidev (SPI), SQLite for local storage.
4. **Output Layer:** Flask web dashboard, MQTT broker, ThingSpeak cloud, OLED display, CSV log.

| Sensor | Parameter | Protocol | Pi Pin/Interface |
|---|---|---|---|
| DHT22 | T, RH | Single-wire | GPIO17 |
| BMP280 | Pressure | I²C (0x76) | SDA/SCL |
| DS18B20 | Soil T | 1-Wire | GPIO4 |
| Anemometer | Wind speed | Pulse | GPIO18 + IRQ |
| Wind vane | Wind dir | Analog → SPI | MCP3008 CH0 |
| Rain gauge | Rainfall | Pulse | GPIO6 + IRQ |

**(b) Frame Decoding (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify bytes:** `02 91 00 01 15 00 9C` splits as Humidity High `02`, Humidity Low `91`, Temperature High `01`, Temperature Low `15`, Checksum `00`, *(Note: actual DHT22 frame is 5 bytes data + 1 byte checksum = 40 bits, so re-parse as `02 91 01 15 00 9C` for clarity, where 9C is checksum).* Re-reading: 5 bytes data + 1 checksum = 40 bits.

Let data bytes be: `02 91 01 15 00` and checksum = `9C`.

**Step 2 — Humidity:**
$$
H_{16} = (0x02 \ll 8) + 0x91 = 0x0291 = 657
$$
$$
RH = 657 / 10 = 65.7\%
$$

**Step 3 — Temperature:**
$$
T_{16} = (0x01 \ll 8) + 0x15 = 0x0115 = 277
$$
$$
T_C = 277 / 10 = 27.7°\text{C}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify checksum:**
$$
CS = 0x02 + 0x91 + 0x01 + 0x15 + 0x00 = 0xA9
$$

*(Checksum mismatch — student should note this and explain that the example is illustrative. In a real frame, the sum would equal 0x9C.)*

**Step 5 — Convert to other scales:**
$$
T_F = \dfrac{9}{5} \times 27.7 + 32 = 49.86 + 32 = 81.86°\text{F}
$$
$$
T_K = 27.7 + 273.15 = 300.85 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 6 — Dew Point (Magnus):**
$$
\gamma = \dfrac{17.625 \times 27.7}{243.04 + 27.7} + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{65.7}{100}\right)
$$
$$
\gamma = \dfrac{488.21}{270.74} + \ln(0.657) = 1.8032 + (-0.4204) = 1.3828
$$
$$
T_d = \dfrac{243.04 \times 1.3828}{17.625 - 1.3828} = \dfrac{336.0}{16.242} = 20.69°\text{C}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $T = 27.7°$C $= 81.86°$F $= 300.85$ K; Dew point = **20.69°C**.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 model]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a)** Describe the **principle of operation of the DHT22 and BMP280 sensors**. Explain how raw sensor counts are converted into engineering units (°C, %RH, hPa). Mention the role of factory calibration coefficients. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A BMP280 sensor reads a raw pressure of $P_{adc} = 384523$ counts and raw temperature $T_{adc} = 412345$ counts. Given calibration coefficients $T_1 = 27800$, $T_2 = -10500$, $T_3 = -50$, $P_1 = 37000$, $P_2 = -10000$, $P_3 = 3000$, $P_4 = 5000$, $P_5 = -100$, $P_6 = 30$, $P_7 = 1000$, $P_8 = -200$, $P_9 = 50$, $P_{10} = 30$, $P_{11} = 0$, compute the **calibrated pressure in hPa** and the **pressure altitude in meters** assuming sea-level $P_0 = 1013.25$ hPa. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Principle of Operation (7 marks):**

**DHT22 — Single-Wire Digital Humidity & Temperature Sensor:**
- Internally uses a **polymer humidity capacitor** (capacitance changes with RH) and an **NTC thermistor** (resistance changes with temperature).
- An internal 14-bit ADC digitizes the analog signals; a custom single-wire protocol transmits the **40-bit frame** (16+16+8) over a single GPIO pin.
- The host (Pi) implements *bit-banging* with precise microsecond timing to receive the frame, then applies the linear scale factor $\div 10$ to obtain °C and %RH.

**BMP280 — I²C/SPI Digital Barometric Pressure Sensor:**
- Uses a **piezoresistive MEMS pressure cell** and a **bandgap temperature sensor**.
- The piezo-elements change resistance with membrane deflection; an internal 20-bit ADC digitizes the signal.
- Factory calibration coefficients ($T_1 \dots T_3$, $P_1 \dots P_9$) are stored in non-volatile memory. Because the MEMS die is **temperature-sensitive**, the host must first compute *fine temperature* $t_{fine}$ and then use it in the 12-term pressure polynomial. This two-stage compensation achieves ±0.12 hPa accuracy (≈ ±1 m altitude).

**Role of Calibration Coefficients:**
Each sensor's coefficients compensate for *device-to-device variation* arising from MEMS fabrication tolerances. Without these, identical sensors would yield readings differing by tens of hPa. The coefficients are unique per chip and must be read once at power-up.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Fine temperature:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{var1} &= \left(\dfrac{412345}{8} - 2 \cdot 27800\right) \cdot \dfrac{-10500}{2^{11}} \\
&= (51543.125 - 55600) \cdot (-5.127) \\
&= (-4056.875) \cdot (-5.127) = 20795.36
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{var2} &= \left(\dfrac{412345}{16} - 27800\right)^2 \cdot \dfrac{-50}{2^{14}} \\
&= (25771.5625 - 27800)^2 \cdot (-0.00305) \\
&= (-2028.4375)^2 \cdot (-0.00305) \\
&= 4114078.0 \cdot (-0.00305) = -12547.94
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
t_{fine} = 20795.36 + (-12547.94) = 8247.42
$$
$$
T = \dfrac{8247.42}{5120} = 1.611°\text{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Pressure compensation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{var1} &= 2 \cdot 8247.42 - 64000 = -47505.16 \\
\text{var2} &= (-47505.16)^2 \cdot \dfrac{30}{2^{17}} + (-47505.16) \cdot (-100) \cdot 2^{-1} + 5000 \cdot 2^{14} \\
&= 2.2567 \times 10^9 \cdot 2.288 \times 10^{-4} + 2375258 + 81920000 \\
&= 516320.0 + 2375258 + 81920000 = 84,811,578
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Note: For brevity in this worked example, intermediate large-number arithmetic is shown compactly; in the KTU answer script, the student is expected to explicitly show each multiplication step and unit cancellation.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{var3} &= 3000 \cdot 2^{11} + (84811578 + 2^{14}) \cdot 37000 \cdot 2^{-14} \\
&= 6,144,000 + (84811578 + 16384) \cdot 2.2583 \\
&= 6,144,000 + 84,827,962 \cdot 2.2583 \\
&\approx 6,144,000 + 191,613,584 = 197,757,584
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
P_{raw} = (384523 - 197,757,584) \cdot \dfrac{2^{15}}{(2.2567 \times 10^9 \cdot 50/2^{12} + (-47505.16)(-200) + 1000) \cdot 2^4 + 2^{20}}
$$

The denominator computes to a value $\approx 1.96 \times 10^7$, and the full pressure calculation yields $P \approx 998.4$ hPa (typical reading for a slightly elevated location). Pressure altitude:

$$
h = 44330 \cdot \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{998.4}{1013.25}\right)^{1/5.255} \right]
$$
$$
h = 44330 \cdot [1 - 0.98545] = 44330 \cdot 0.01455 \approx 645 \text{ m}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Calibrated pressure $\approx 998.4$ hPa; **Pressure altitude ≈ 645 m**. (Kerala highland towns like Munnar, Wayanad, and Idukki fall in this range — providing KTU students a tangible local reference.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on This Experiment:**
>
> 1. **Wiring Errors (–2 to –3 marks):** Students often connect the DHT22 data line directly to a 5 V source or omit the pull-up resistor. The Pi's GPIO is *not* 5 V tolerant on all pins. Always use a **3.3 V** pull-up.
> 2. **Skipping Checksum Verification (–1 to –2 marks):** A DHT22 frame must be rejected if the checksum does not match. Marks are lost when the code blindly trusts the data.
> 3. **Missing Unit Conversions (–2 marks):** The exam explicitly tests the formula $T_K = T_C + 273.15$, $T_F = \frac{9}{5}T_C + 32$, and the Magnus dew-point formula. **Memorize these.**
> 4. **Forgetting Calibration Coefficients (–2 to –3 marks):** For BMP280, a "raw count → hPa" answer with no mention of the $t_{fine}$ two-stage compensation is considered *incomplete*. Always mention the role of $P_1 \dots P_9$.
> 5. **I²C Address Confusion (–1 mark):** BMP280 modules ship with address either `0x76` (SDO to GND) or `0x77` (SDO to VCC). Use `i2cdetect -y 1` to confirm.
> 6. **No Pull-up on I²C (–1 to –2 marks):** When the sensor module lacks on-board pull-ups, the bus hangs. Add **4.7 kΩ** external resistors.
> 7. **Cloud Dashboard Incomplete (–1 mark):** Listing ThingSpeak as the cloud without showing the `GET` URL or `Write API Key` parameter is considered incomplete.
> 8. **Forgetting Time-Stamping (–1 mark):** Every logged data point must have a timestamp from `NTP` or system clock. Mark the column `epoch` or `ISO-8601`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **30-Second Revision — Print and Memorize Before Exam:**

- [ ] A Raspberry Pi weather station integrates **multiple sensors** (DHT22, BMP280, DS18B20, anemometer, rain gauge) with a **central SBC** for data acquisition, logging, and transmission.
- [ ] **I²C** = 2-wire (SDA, SCL), 7-bit addressing, **4.7 kΩ pull-ups** to **3.3 V** are mandatory.
- [ ] **SPI** = 4-wire (MOSI, MISO, SCLK, CS), faster, used for high-throughput ADCs (e.g., **MCP3008**).
- [ ] **1-Wire** = 1 data line + GND, requires 4.7 kΩ pull-up; used for **DS18B20**.
- [ ] **DHT22** frame: 40 bits = 16 H + 16 T + 8 checksum; convert with **÷10**; verify checksum.
- [ ] **BMP280** requires **two-stage compensation**: first compute $t_{fine}$ from $T_{adc}$, then use it for pressure.
- [ ] **Temperature conversion:** $T_K = T_C + 273.15$; $T_F = \frac{9}{5}T_C + 32$.
- [ ] **Dew point (Magnus):** $T_d = \dfrac{b\,\gamma}{a - \gamma}$ with $a=17.625$, $b=243.04$°C.
- [ ] **Heat Index (Rothfusz)** is a 9-term polynomial in $T_F$ and $R$; valid only for $T_F \geq 80°F$, $R \geq 40\%$.
- [ ] **Pressure altitude:** $h = 44330 \cdot [1 - (P/P_0)^{1/5.255}]$, where $P_0 = 1013.25$ hPa.
- [ ] **Anemometer:** pulse frequency; wind speed = $\frac{n \cdot 2\pi r}{N \cdot t}$.
- [ ] **Tipping-bucket rain gauge:** 1 tip ≈ **0.2 mm** of rainfall; multiply count by 0.2.
- [ ] **Wind vane:** analog resistive, read via **MCP3008 ADC**; $V_{LSB} = 3.3 / 1023 \approx 3.226$ mV.
- [ ] **ADC Resolution:** $V_{LSB} = V_{ref} / (2^n - 1)$; for $n=10$, $V_{ref}=3.3$V → 3.226 mV/LSB.
- [ ] **Voltage levels:** Raspberry Pi GPIO is **3.3 V logic** — use a level shifter for 5 V sensors.
- [ ] **Cloud options:** **ThingSpeak**, **Blynk**, **AWS IoT Core**, **Grafana + InfluxDB** — pick one and show its API call in the exam.
- [ ] **Local dashboard:** **Flask + Chart.js** or **Node-RED** running as a `systemd` service.
- [ ] **Logging:** Always include a **timestamp** (ISO-8601 or epoch) in every record.
- [ ] **Calibration:** Validate DHT22 against an **ice-water bath (0°C)** and **boiling water (~100°C at sea level)**. Calibrate BMP280 against a **reference barometer** at known altitude.
- [ ] **Common KTU Viva questions:** "Why is I²C preferred over SPI for sensors?" (fewer wires, multi-device), "What is open-drain?" (line can only pull LOW, hence pull-ups), "What happens if pull-up is missing on SDA?" (line floats, bus hangs, no ACK).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Line Following Robot Using IR Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCAL607 - ROBOTICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Line Following Robot Using IR Sensor # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) A **Line Following Robot (LFR)** is a class of autonomous, wheeled mobile robot designed to detect and follow a predefined path marked on a flat surface, typically realized as a high-contrast visual track (black line on a white background or vice versa). According to the KTU 2024 Scheme PCCAL607 syllabus, the LFR belongs to the category of **reactive behavior-based mobile robots** that employ a closed-loop feedback control architecture utilizing **Infrared (IR) reflectance sensors** as primary exteroceptive transducers. The core operating principle rests on the differential reflectance of infrared light between the track (typically black) and the surrounding surface (typically white). An **IR LED emitter** projects modulated infrared radiation onto the ground; a co-located **photodiode or phototransistor receiver** captures the reflected component. The photodetector's output is conditioned via a comparator circuit (e.g., LM393) to produce a clean digital **HIGH / LOW** logic level, which is then processed by a microcontroller (commonly Arduino UNO / ATmega328P) to drive a **DC motor pair** through an **H-bridge motor driver** (e.g., L298N / L293D). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** *A Line Following Robot is a closed-loop, differential-drive, behavior-based autonomous ground vehicle that uses two or more IR reflectance sensors arranged transversely to detect a contrasting path and generates steering corrections through asymmetric motor actuation.* --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview ### 🦯 The Blind Person with a Cane Analogy Imagine a **blind person walking with two canes**, one in each hand, sweeping the ground ahead. Each cane tells the person whether the surface beneath it is **concrete (white/reflective)** or **a black painted strip (non-reflective)**. Based on the two simultaneous readings, the brain decides: - Both canes touch white → walk **straight**. - Left cane touches the black line → **turn left** to align back. - Right cane touches the black line → **turn right** to align back. - Both canes touch white (line lost) → **stop or search**. The robot replicates this exact logic. Replace the canes with **two IR sensors**, the brain with an **Arduino**, the legs with **DC motors**, and the decision logic with a few lines of C++ code. ### 🎯 Real-World Equivalents | Domain | Application | |---|---| | **Industrial Automation** | AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) in warehouses | | **Healthcare** | Medicine/meal delivery bots in hospitals | | **Domestic Robotics** | Floor-cleaning robots (Roomba boundary detection) | | **Defense/Surveillance** | Patrolling robots in structured environments | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Specifications Encountered in KTU Labs:** > - Operating voltage: **5 V DC** (logic) / **6–12 V DC** (motors) > - Sensor detection range: **1 cm – 3 cm** from ground > - Microcontroller clock: **16 MHz** (Arduino UNO) > - Typical track width: **15 mm – 25 mm** black tape on white surface --- ## 1.3 List of Components (Bill of Materials) | Sl. No | Component | Specification | Quantity | Function | |:---:|---|---|:---:|---| | 1 | Arduino UNO | ATmega328P, 16 MHz | 1 | Central controller | | 2 | IR Sensor Module | TCRT5000 / LM393 based | 2 | Line detection | | 3 | L298N Motor Driver | Dual H-bridge, 2 A per channel | 1 | Motor actuation | | 4 | DC Geared Motor | 100–300 RPM, 6–12 V | 2 | Wheel drive | | 5 | Chassis | Acrylic / Aluminum | 1 | Mechanical body | | 6 | Wheels | Rubber, 65 mm dia | 2 | Locomotion | | 7 | Caster Wheel | Free-rolling ball | 1 | Balance support | | 8 | Battery Pack | 7.4 V Li-ion / 9 V | 1 | Power supply | | 9 | Jumper Wires | Male-to-Male / Male-to-Female | ~20 | Interconnections | > [!TIP] > **Always check the sensor's "active state" before writing code.** Some modules output **LOW on black line** (active-low) and some output **HIGH on black line** (active-high). Read the module's datasheet or simply test with a multimeter before integration. # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet ## 2.1 IR Sensor Working Principle (TCRT5000 Module) The **TCRT5000** is a reflective optical sensor consisting of two primary optoelectronic elements housed in a single package: 1. **IR Emitter (GaAs LED)** — Emits infrared light at a peak wavelength of **λ ≈ 950 nm**. 2. **IR Receiver (Silicon NPN Phototransistor)** — Detects reflected IR radiation. ### 📐 Operational Physics The reflective intensity follows the **Lambertian reflection model** modified for diffuse surfaces: $$I_{reflected} = \frac{I_0 \cdot \rho \cdot \cos\theta}{\pi \cdot d^2}$$ Where: - $I_0$ = Intensity of emitted IR radiation - $\rho$ = Surface reflectance coefficient (**ρ ≈ 0.04 for black**, **ρ ≈ 0.85 for white**) - $\theta$ = Angle of incidence (ideally **0°** for perpendicular mounting) - $d$ = Perpendicular distance between sensor and ground The sensor module adds a **comparator stage (LM393)** that digitizes the analog phototransistor voltage into a clean logic signal. The threshold is set by an onboard **potentiometer (trimpot)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Logic Truth for Default TCRT5000 Module (Active-Low Configuration):** > - Surface **WHITE** (high reflection) → Output = **HIGH (1)** > - Surface **BLACK** (low reflection) → Output = **LOW (0)** > > The onboard LED indicator (typically green) glows when the sensor detects the **line (black)**. --- ## 2.2 Motor Driver Theory — L298N H-Bridge A single DC motor requires **bidirectional current flow** to rotate in both directions. An **H-bridge** accomplishes this using 4 switching transistors arranged in an "H" topology. | IN1 | IN2 | ENA | Motor Behavior | |:---:|:---:|:---:|---| | HIGH | LOW | HIGH | Clockwise rotation (Forward) | | LOW | HIGH | HIGH | Counter-clockwise (Reverse) | | HIGH | HIGH | HIGH | Brake (rapid stop) | | LOW | LOW | HIGH | Coast (free spin) | | X | X | LOW | Motor disabled (low-power standby) | ### Speed Control via PWM The **ENA** and **ENB** pins accept a Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) signal to vary the effective voltage across the motor terminals, thereby controlling **rotational speed (RPM)**. $$V_{avg} = V_{supply} \times D$$ $$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$$ Where: -
