$D$ = Duty cycle (0 to 1) - $T_{on}$ = Time HIGH per cycle - $T_{period}$ = Total PWM period (**~2.048 ms for Arduino analogWrite at 490 Hz**) - Arduino's `analogWrite(pin, value)` maps value from **0 (0%)** to **255 (100%)** --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility | |:---:|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Reflected IR Intensity | $I_r = \dfrac{I_0 \cdot \rho \cdot \cos\theta}{\pi d^2}$ | $I_0$ [W/sr], $\rho$ [–], $d$ [m] | Sensor placement height optimization | | 2 | Voltage Divider (Phototransistor) | $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \dfrac{R_L}{R_{L} + R_{photo}}$ | $V_{cc}=5V$, $R_L$ [Ω], $R_{photo}$ [Ω] | Analog sensor readout | | 3 | Comparator Threshold | $V_{th} = V_{cc} \cdot \dfrac{R_{2}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}$ | $V_{th}$ [V] | Trimpot adjustment for sensitivity | | 4 | PWM Average Voltage | $V_{avg} = V_{s} \cdot D$ | $V_s$ [V], $D$ [0–1] | Motor speed regulation | | 5 | Motor Back-EMF | $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$ | $E_b$ [V], $K_e$ [V·s/rad], $\omega$ [rad/s] | Load compensation | | 6 | Motor Speed (No-load) | $N = V_{avg} - I_a R_a \over K_e$ | $N$ [RPM] | Speed estimation under load | | 7 | Robot Linear Velocity | $v = \omega \cdot r = \dfrac{2\pi N r}{60}$ | $v$ [m/s], $r$ [m] | Track traversal time | | 8 | Angular Velocity (Differential Turn) | $\omega_{robot} = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{L}$ | $L$ = wheelbase [m] | Turning radius | | 9 | Turning Radius | $R = \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$ | $R$ [m] | Path curvature tracking | | 10 | Battery Life | $t = \dfrac{C_{battery}}{I_{total}}$ | $C$ [mAh], $I$ [mA] | Operational endurance | > [!WARNING] > **Units & Notation:** In all KTU valuation scripts, examiners expect **SI units** with **clear variable definitions**. Never use the bare symbol "$v$" without specifying whether it is linear velocity, voltage, or instantaneous value. Always use subscripts ($v_L$, $v_R$, $V_{avg}$) for clarity. --- ## 2.4 Decision Logic — Core Algorithm (KTU Theory Backbone) A two-sensor LFR implements a **4-state finite state machine (FSM)**: | State | Left Sensor (S_L) | Right Sensor (S_R) | Robot Action | |:---:|:---:|:---:|---| | S0 | LOW (White) | LOW (White) | **STOP / Search** (line lost) | | S1 | HIGH (Black) | HIGH (Black) | **MOVE FORWARD** (centered) | | S2 | HIGH (Black) | LOW (White) | **TURN LEFT** (line shifted right) | | S3 | LOW (White) | HIGH (Black) | **TURN RIGHT** (line shifted left) | > [!NOTE] > **Logic Convention Used Below:** Assuming the standard TCRT5000 module wired in active-low configuration (black = HIGH after inverting in code, OR black = LOW directly). > For this experiment, we adopt: **S = 1 (HIGH) when sensor is ON BLACK LINE**. # 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal Sensor Mounting Height To guarantee reliable line detection, the sensor must be mounted at a height where the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** is maximized. ### Step 1: Define the Phototransistor Output Voltage The phototransistor and load resistor $R_L$ form a voltage divider. As reflected IR intensity $I_r$ increases, phototransistor conductivity increases, so its collector-emitter resistance $R_{photo}$ decreases. $$V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_L + R_{photo}(I_r)}$$ ### Step 2: Compute the Output Swing Between Black and White $$V_{out}^{white} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_L + R_{photo}^{white}}$$ $$V_{out}^{black} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_L + R_{photo}^{black}}$$ Since white reflects strongly → high $I_r$ → low $R_{photo}^{white}$ → $V_{out}^{white}$ is HIGH. Since black absorbs → low $I_r$ → high $R_{photo}^{black}$ → $V_{out}^{black}$ is LOW. ### Step 3: Calculate Optimal Height The recommended mounting height is the distance at which the difference $(V_{out}^{white} - V_{out}^{black})$ is maximum. For TCRT5000: $$h_{optimal} \approx 1.0 \text{ cm to } 1.5 \text{ cm}$$ Below 1 cm: specular reflection dominates, SNR degrades. Above 3 cm: $I_r$ falls off as $1/h^2$ (inverse-square law), signal becomes weak. ### Step 4: Verify with Numerical Example Let $V_{cc} = 5V$, $R_L = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, and assume: - $R_{photo}^{white} = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$ - $R_{photo}^{black} = 50\ \text{k}\Omega$ $$V_{out}^{white} = 5 \cdot \frac{10000}{10000 + 1000} = 4.55\ \text{V}$$ $$V_{out}^{black} = 5 \cdot \frac{10000}{10000 + 50000} = 0.91\ \text{V}$$ $$\Delta V = 4.55 - 0.91 = 3.64\ \text{V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Excellent discrimination} \quad \checkmark$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Differential Drive Kinematics For a two-wheeled differential-drive robot, the kinematic model is derived from the no-slip rolling constraint. ### Step 1: Define Variables - $r$ = wheel radius [m] - $L$ = distance between wheels (wheelbase) [m] - $v_L$ = linear velocity of left wheel [m/s] - $v_R$ = linear velocity of right wheel [m/s] - $R$ = radius of instantaneous turning path [m] - $\omega$ = angular velocity of robot body [rad/s] ### Step 2: Express Wheel Velocities in Terms of Path Radius For pure rolling without slip, the outer wheel travels a longer arc than the inner wheel. $$v_L = \omega \cdot (R - L/2)$$ $$v_R = \omega \cdot (R + L/2)$$ ### Step 3: Solve Simultaneously for $R$ and $\omega$ Subtract equations: $$v_R - v_L = \omega \cdot L \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}$$ Add equations: $$v_R + v_L = 2 \omega R \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Insight:** A line-following robot executes a **turn by asymmetrically setting $v_L$ and $v_R$**. A pure left turn can be approximated by setting $v_L = 0$ and $v_R > 0$, which gives a turning radius of $R = L/2$ (pivoting about the stopped wheel). ### Step 4: Numerical Example — Sharp Left Turn Given $L = 0.15\ \text{m}$, $v_L = 0\ \text{m/s}$, $v_R = 0.20\ \text{m/s}$: $$R = \frac{0.15}{2} \cdot \frac{0.20 + 0}{0.20 - 0} = 0.075 \cdot 1 = 0.075\ \text{m} = 7.5\ \text{cm}$$ $$\omega = \frac{0.20 - 0}{0.15} = 1.33\ \text{rad/s} \approx 76.4\ ^\circ/\text{s}$$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — PWM-to-Robot Behavior Mapping The following analytical table consolidates the complete mapping from **sensor reading → motor command → physical behavior**. | Left Sensor | Right Sensor | Left Motor (PWM) | Right Motor (PWM) | Physical Behavior | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|---| | LOW (white) | LOW (white) | 0 (stop) | 0 (stop) | Line lost — stop or rotate to search | | HIGH (black) | HIGH (black) | 200 (fwd) | 200 (fwd) | Line centered — move forward | | HIGH (black) | LOW (white) | 0 (stop) | 180 (fwd) | Line on left — pivot left | | LOW (white) | HIGH (black) | 180 (fwd) | 0 (stop) | Line on right — pivot right | > [!NOTE] > **PWM Values Rationale:** Using `analogWrite(value)` where 0 = stop, 255 = max speed. A differential of ~20 PWM units between motors during straight motion compensates for manufacturing asymmetries (one motor being slightly stronger). This is a **calibration trick** examiners love to test. --- ## 3.4 Full Arduino Implementation (Step-by-Step Code Walkthrough) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Programming is mandatory in PCCAL607 (Robotics Lab).** The following is the reference code structure for KTU record submission. ### Step 1: Pin Declaration ```cpp // === IR Sensor Pins === #define IR_LEFT 2 // Digital pin for left IR sensor #define IR_RIGHT 3 // Digital pin for right IR sensor // === L298N Motor Driver Pins === #define ENA 9 // Left motor speed (PWM) #define IN1 8 // Left motor direction 1 #define IN2 7 // Left motor direction 2 #define ENB 10 // Right motor speed (PWM) #define IN3 6 // Right motor direction 1 #define IN4 5 // Right motor direction 2 // === Speed Constants === #define SPEED_NORMAL 200 #define SPEED_TURN 180 ``` ### Step 2: Setup Function ```cpp void setup() { pinMode(IR_LEFT, INPUT); pinMode(IR_RIGHT, INPUT); pinMode(ENA, OUTPUT); pinMode(IN1, OUTPUT); pinMode(IN2, OUTPUT); pinMode(ENB, OUTPUT); pinMode(IN3, OUTPUT); pinMode(IN4, OUTPUT); Serial.begin(9600); } ``` ### Step 3: Motor Driver Helper Functions ```cpp void moveForward() { digitalWrite(IN1, HIGH); digitalWrite(IN2, LOW); digitalWrite(IN3, HIGH); digitalWrite(IN4, LOW); analogWrite(ENA, SPEED_NORMAL); analogWrite(ENB, SPEED_NORMAL); } void turnLeft() { digitalWrite(IN1, LOW); digitalWrite(IN2, LOW); // Stop left digitalWrite(IN3, HIGH); digitalWrite(IN4, LOW); // Right forward analogWrite(ENA, 0); analogWrite(ENB, SPEED_TURN); } void turnRight() { digitalWrite(IN1, HIGH); digitalWrite(IN2, LOW); // Left forward digitalWrite(IN3, LOW); digitalWrite(IN4, LOW); // Stop right analogWrite(ENA, SPEED_TURN); analogWrite(ENB, 0); } void stopRobot() { digitalWrite(IN1, LOW); digitalWrite(IN2, LOW); digitalWrite(IN3, LOW); digitalWrite(IN4, LOW); analogWrite(ENA, 0); analogWrite(ENB, 0); } ``` ### Step 4: Main Loop — Decision Engine ```cpp void loop() { int sL = digitalRead(IR_LEFT); // 1 = on black line int sR = digitalRead(IR_RIGHT); // 1 = on black line if (sL == 0 && sR == 0) { // No sensor on line — both on white stopRobot(); } else if (sL == 1 && sR == 1) { // Both sensors on line — go straight moveForward(); } else if (sL == 1 && sR == 0) { // Left sensor detects line — turn left turnLeft(); } else if (sL == 0 && sR == 1) { // Right sensor detects line — turn right turnRight(); } delay(10); // Debounce } ``` > [!TIP] > **Line Following Robot Using IR Sensor — Enhancement:** Add a third (center) sensor for a 5-state FSM (S00, S01, S11, S10, S11) to achieve smoother, higher-speed tracking. This is often asked as a **Part B sub-question (b)** in KTU exams. # 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Master Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Power Supply
7.4V Li-ion] --> B[Voltage Regulator
5V Arduino Logic] A --> C[L298N Motor Driver
Motor Power Input] B --> D[Arduino UNO
ATmega328P] E[IR Sensor Left
TCRT5000] --> D F[IR Sensor Right
TCRT5000] --> D D -->|PWM and Direction Signals| C C --> G[Left DC Motor] C --> H[Right DC Motor] G --> I[Left Wheel] H --> J[Right Wheel] K[Caster Wheel
Front Support] --> L[Chassis Assembly] I --> L J --> L ``` --- ## 4.2 Sensor-to-Actuator Signal Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[IR Sensor Left
Detects Reflected IR] --> MUX[Arduino Digital Input] S2[IR Sensor Right
Detects Reflected IR] --> MUX MUX --> DEC{Decision Logic
4-State FSM} DEC -->|S1=1 S2=1| ACT1[Both Motors FORWARD] DEC -->|S1=1 S2=0| ACT2[Stop Left
Run Right] DEC -->|S1=0 S2=1| ACT3[Run Left
Stop Right] DEC -->|S1=0 S2=0| ACT4[STOP / Search] ACT1 --> MOT[L298N Driver] ACT2 --> MOT ACT3 --> MOT ACT4 --> MOT MOT --> WH[Wheel Rotation
Closes Feedback Loop] WH -.->|Robot translates
along track| S1 WH -.->|Robot translates
along track| S2 ``` --- ## 4.3 Pin Connection Schematic (Wiring Map) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph ARD[Arduino UNO] P2[Digital Pin 2] P3[Digital Pin 3] P5[Digital Pin 5] P6[Digital Pin 6] P7[Digital Pin 7] P8[Digital Pin 8] P9[PWM Pin 9] P10[PWM Pin 10] V5[5V] GND1[GND] end subgraph IRL[IR Sensor LEFT] VCC1[VCC] GND2[GND] OUT1[OUT] end subgraph IRR[IR Sensor RIGHT] VCC2[VCC] GND3[GND] OUT2[OUT] end subgraph MOT[L298N Motor Driver] IN1A[IN1] IN2A[IN2] IN3A[IN3] IN4A[IN4] ENAA[ENA] ENBB[ENB] VCCM[12V Motor Power] GNDM[GND] OUTA1[OUT1 Motor A] OUTA2[OUT2 Motor A] OUTB1[OUT3 Motor B] OUTB2[OUT4 Motor B] end V5 --> VCC1 V5 --> VCC2 GND1 --> GND2 GND1 --> GND3 P2 --> OUT1 P3 --> OUT2 P8 --> IN1A P7 --> IN2A P6 --> IN3A P5 --> IN4A P9 --> ENAA P10 --> ENBB OUTA1 --> ML[Left Motor] OUTA2 --> ML OUTB1 --> MR[Right Motor] OUTB2 --> MR ``` --- ## 4.4 Algorithm State Diagram (FSM) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Initialize Initialize --> ReadSensors ReadSensors --> S00: Both LOW ReadSensors --> S11: Both HIGH ReadSensors --> S10: Left HIGH ReadSensors --> S01: Right HIGH S00 --> StopAction S11 --> ForwardAction S10 --> TurnLeftAction S01 --> TurnRightAction StopAction --> ReadSensors: delay 10ms ForwardAction --> ReadSensors: delay 10ms TurnLeftAction --> ReadSensors: delay 10ms TurnRightAction --> ReadSensors: delay 10ms ``` --- ## 4.5 Physical Mounting Layout (Top View) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph TOP[Top View of Robot] direction LR CW[Caster Wheel
FRONT] IR_L[IR Sensor L
~3 cm from ground] IR_R[IR Sensor R
~3 cm from ground] ARD_BOX[Arduino UNO
Mounted on top] BAT[Battery Pack
7.4V Li-ion] MD[L298N Module
Center mounted] WL[Left Wheel
Geared Motor] WR[Right Wheel
Geared Motor] end CW --> IR_L IR_L --> IR_R IR_R --> ARD_BOX ARD_BOX --> BAT ARD_BOX --> MD MD --> WL MD --> WR ``` > [!TIP] > **Sensor Spacing Rule:** The two IR sensors should be spaced **slightly wider than the line width** (e.g., for a 15 mm line, space sensors ~25 mm apart center-to-center) so that the "both on line" state is achievable when the robot is perfectly aligned. # 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** **Explain the working principle of an IR sensor module used in a line following robot. State the role of the LM393 comparator in the circuit.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **IR sensor module** consists of an IR LED (emitter) and a photodiode/phototransistor (receiver). The IR LED emits infrared radiation at ~950 nm wavelength. When this radiation strikes a **white (reflective) surface**, a significant portion is reflected back to the phototransistor, increasing its conductivity. When it strikes a **black (absorptive) surface**, very little is reflected, and the phototransistor remains in a high-resistance state. The **LM393 comparator IC** acts as a signal-conditioning stage. It compares the phototransistor's analog output voltage with a **threshold voltage** set by an onboard potentiometer. The comparator produces a clean **digital HIGH or LOW** output that can be directly read by the Arduino's digital input pin, eliminating the need for software-based analog thresholding. > **Mark Distribution (1+1+1):** Working principle (1) + Reflectance contrast (1) + LM393 role (1). --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series 2]** **What is the function of an H-bridge motor driver (L298N)? Why can't a microcontroller directly drive a DC motor?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **DC motor** requires **bidirectional current flow** to rotate in both forward and reverse directions, and typically draws **200–500 mA** of current, far exceeding the **~40 mA** sourcing capability of a microcontroller GPIO pin. An **H-bridge motor driver (L298N)** solves both problems by providing: 1. **Direction control** — Using 4 transistors in an H-configuration, it reverses current polarity through the motor windings. 2. **Current amplification** — It supplies the higher currents needed by the motor from an external power source, isolating the microcontroller. 3. **PWM speed control** — Via the ENA/ENB enable pins, which accept PWM signals from the microcontroller. > **Mark Distribution (1+1+1):** Need for driver (1) + H-bridge concept (1) + L298N features (1). --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Q-A or Q-B) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the complete architecture of a line following robot. List all major hardware and software components and state the function of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Develop the 4-state decision algorithm for a 2-sensor line follower. Construct the truth table mapping sensor states to motor actions, and explain how PWM is used for speed control. Provide the equivalent Arduino `loop()` function code. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** The line following robot architecture consists of the following functional blocks (as per the block diagram in Section 4.1): | Block | Component | Function | |---|---|---| | **Power Supply** | 7.4 V Li-ion battery | Provides energy to motors and logic circuits | | **Voltage Regulator** | Arduino onboard 5V regulator | Powers microcontroller and IR sensors | | **Sensor Array** | 2× TCRT5000 IR modules | Detects contrast between line and background | | **Controller** | Arduino UNO (ATmega328P) | Executes decision algorithm | | **Driver Stage** | L298N dual H-bridge | Amplifies control signals to drive motors | | **Actuators** | 2× DC geared motors | Convert electrical energy to mechanical rotation | | **Mechanical** | Chassis, wheels, caster | Physical structure and support | **Signal Flow:** 1. IR sensors emit and detect reflected light → produce digital output (HIGH/LOW). 2. Arduino reads these signals in real-time at digital pins 2 and 3. 3. Decision logic in `loop()` maps the (S_L, S_R) combination to motor commands. 4. Arduino sends logic HIGH/LOW to L298N input pins (IN1–IN4) and PWM to enable pins (ENA, ENB). 5. L298N drives the two DC motors accordingly → robot translates along the line. **Software Components:** - **Setup routine** — Initializes pin modes and serial communication. - **Sensor reading routine** — Polls digital inputs. - **Decision engine** — Implements the FSM. - **Motor control routines** — `moveForward()`, `turnLeft()`, `turnRight()`, `stopRobot()`. > **Examiner's Note:** A labelled block diagram earns **2 marks**; component identification earns **2 marks**; signal flow description earns **2 marks**; software breakdown earns **1 mark**. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Truth Table (2 marks):** | S_L | S_R | Action | Left Motor | Right Motor | |:---:|:---:|---|:---:|:---:| | 0 | 0 | STOP | 0 | 0 | | 1 | 1 | FORWARD | 200 | 200 | | 1 | 0 | TURN LEFT | 0 | 180 | | 0 | 1 | TURN RIGHT | 180 | 0 | **PWM Speed Control Explanation (2 marks):** PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) varies the **average voltage** applied to the motor by rapidly switching the supply ON and OFF at a fixed frequency (~490 Hz on Arduino). The duty cycle $D = T_{on} / T_{period}$ determines the average voltage: $$V_{avg} = V_{supply} \times D$$ The Arduino function `analogWrite(pin, value)` accepts an integer from **0 to 255**, mapping linearly to a duty cycle of **0% to 100%**. Thus `analogWrite(ENA, 200)` delivers $D = 200/255 \approx 78.4\%$ duty cycle, resulting in $V_{avg} \approx 0.784 \times V_{supply}$. **Arduino Code (3 marks):** ```cpp void loop() { int sL = digitalRead(IR_LEFT); int sR = digitalRead(IR_RIGHT); if (sL == 0 && sR == 0) stopRobot(); else if (sL == 1 && sR == 1) moveForward(); else if (sL == 1 && sR == 0) turnLeft(); else if (sL == 0 && sR == 1) turnRight(); delay(10); } ``` --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]** **(a)** Derive the kinematic equations for a differential-drive wheeled robot. Compute the turning radius and angular velocity for a robot with wheelbase $L = 0.20$ m, left wheel speed $v_L = 0.10$ m/s, and right wheel speed $v_R = 0.30$ m/s. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design a 3-sensor (5-state) line follower improvement over the basic 2-sensor design. State the truth table, advantages, and a critical application scenario where 3 sensors are mandatory. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** **Derivation (4 marks):** For a differential-drive robot with wheelbase $L$, the two wheels trace concentric circular arcs. Let $\omega$ be the angular velocity of the robot body and $R$ the radius of the instantaneous turn. The instantaneous velocities of the left and right wheels are: $$v_L = \omega \left(R - \frac{L}{2}\right)$$ $$v_R = \omega \left(R + \frac{L}{2}\right)$$ **Subtracting** the equations: $$v_R - v_L = \omega L \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}$$ **Adding** the equations: $$v_R + v_L = 2\omega R \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$$ **Numerical Computation (3 marks):** Given $L = 0.20$ m, $v_L = 0.10$ m/s, $v_R = 0.30$ m/s: $$\omega = \frac{0.30 - 0.10}{0.20} = \frac{0.20}{0.20} = 1.0\ \text{rad/s}$$ $$R = \frac{0.20}{2} \cdot \frac{0.30 + 0.10}{0.30 - 0.10} = 0.10 \cdot \frac{0.40}{0.20} = 0.10 \cdot 2.0 = 0.20\ \text{m}$$ $$\boxed{\omega = 1.0\ \text{rad/s}, \quad R = 0.20\ \text{m}}$$ > **Examiner's Note:** Always state the **direction of turn** explicitly. Here, $v_R > v_L$, so the robot **turns LEFT** (left wheel is inner/inner arc). --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Three-Sensor Configuration (3 marks):** Add a center sensor (S_C) between the left (S_L) and right (S_R) sensors. This gives $2^3 = 8$ possible states, but practically we use **5 meaningful states**: | S_L | S_C | S_R | State | Action | |:---:|:---:|:---:|---|---| | 0 | 0 | 0 | S0 | STOP (line lost) | | 0 | 0 | 1 | S1 | SHARP RIGHT (line far left) | | 0 | 1 | 0 | S2 | MILD LEFT (line slightly right of left sensor) | | 0 | 1 | 1 | S3 | MILD RIGHT (line slightly left of right sensor) | | 1 | 0 | 0 | S4 | SHARP LEFT (line far right) | | 1 | 0 | 1 | S5 | **CENTERED** (line covers both outer sensors) | | 1 | 1 | 0 | S6 | **CENTERED** (line covers both outer sensors) | | 1 | 1 | 1 | S7 | ON INTERSECTION (cross-junction detected) | **Advantages (2 marks):** 1. **Smoother tracking** — The center sensor detects the line earlier, enabling gentler corrections. 2. **Higher speeds** — Reduced oscillation since corrections are proportional to line offset. 3. **Intersection detection** — State S7 (1,1,1) reliably identifies junctions and T-crossings. **Critical Application (2 marks):** **Warehouse Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)** require 3-sensor (or 5-sensor) arrays to navigate complex mazes with intersections, branch points, and dock selectors. A 2-sensor robot cannot distinguish between a straight path and a junction, making it unsuitable for dynamic industrial environments. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Wrong sensor convention** — Many students assume black = HIGH without verifying. Always mention the **active-HIGH vs. active-LOW** convention of the specific module. > 2. **Missing `delay(10)`** — Skipping debounce causes erratic behavior; examiners deduct marks for unexplained flickering in the working model. > 3. **No comments in code** — KTU record submission requires **commented code** (10–15% weightage in lab report). > 4. **Forgetting to call `pinMode()`** — A frequent oversight that makes the entire code non-functional. > 5. **Not mentioning the comparator threshold** — When asked about the LM393, students forget to explain the **trimpot adjustment** for sensitivity calibration. > 6. **Wrong motor direction logic** — Reversing the motor polarity physically without flipping the code logic leads to confusion. Always verify direction first with a **simple test sketch**. --- ## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Revision — Print This Section Before the Exam** ### ✅ Key Concepts to Remember - ☐ **LFR** = Line Following Robot — autonomous, behavior-based, differential-drive ground vehicle. - ☐ **IR Sensor** uses **reflectance contrast** (white = high reflection, black = low reflection). - ☐ **TCRT5000** module = IR LED + phototransistor + **LM393 comparator** + trimpot + indicator LED. - ☐ **Active-LOW default** for most TCRT5000 modules: black line = **LOW** output. - ☐ **Microcontroller** = Arduino UNO (ATmega328P, 16 MHz, 14 digital I/O, 6 PWM pins). - ☐ **Motor Driver** = L298N dual H-bridge; needs **separate motor power supply** (6–12 V). - ☐ **PWM** uses `analogWrite(pin, value)` with `value ∈ [0, 255]`. - ☐ **Duty cycle** $D = V_{avg} / V_{supply} = value / 255$. - ☐ **4-State FSM** for 2-sensor LFR: Forward, Left, Right, Stop. - ☐ **Differential drive kinematics**: $\omega = (v_R - v_L) / L$ and $R = (L/2) \cdot (v_R + v_L) / (v_R - v_L)$. - ☐ **Optimal sensor height** = 1.0–1.5 cm above ground. - ☐ **Sensor spacing** ≈ line width + 1 cm. - ☐ **Debounce delay** of 10 ms is essential. - ☐ **Power supply isolation** — Motor power and logic power should share a **common ground** but be sourced from different rails. - ☐ **3-sensor LFR** adds center sensor for 5-state operation and intersection detection. ### ✅ Formulas to Memorize $$\boxed{I_r = \frac{I_0 \cdot \rho \cdot \cos\theta}{\pi d^2}} \quad \boxed{V_{avg} = V_s \cdot D} \quad \boxed{\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}} \quad \boxed{R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}}$$ ### ✅ Common Viva Questions 1. *What is the wavelength of IR used in TCRT5000?* → **950 nm** 2. *Why use a comparator in the IR module?* → To convert noisy analog to clean digital 3. *What is the wheelbase of your robot?* → Measure and report (typically 12–20 cm) 4. *How do you reverse a DC motor?* → Reverse the polarity via H-bridge IN1/IN2 swap 5. *What happens if both sensors are on white?* → **STOP** (line lost) 6. *What is the no-load RPM of your motor?* → Typically **100–300 RPM** for geared hobby motors 7. *How is PWM frequency set on Arduino?* → Default **~490 Hz** on pins 3, 9, 10, 11; **~980 Hz** on pins 5, 6 8. *Name an alternative to IR for line following.* → **LIDAR, Camera (OpenCV), Ultrasonic array, Magnetic line tape + Hall sensor** > [!TIP] > **Pro Tip for KTU Lab Exam:** Before demonstrating, always **calibrate both sensors** by adjusting their trimpots to the threshold voltage. Test each sensor individually with a Serial.println() debug statement to confirm correct logic levels. Examiners reward **systematic testing methodology** with bonus marks.

topics.title: Image Recognition Using ESP32 CAM Module
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAL607 - ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Image Recognition Using ESP32 CAM Module

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> *Image Recognition* is a sub-field of computer vision that involves the identification, classification, and interpretation of objects, patterns, or features within a digital image. When performed using the **ESP32-CAM** module, it refers to a real-time embedded system architecture where the low-power **Espressif ESP32** microcontroller (integrated with an **OV2640** CMOS image sensor) acquires, optionally preprocesses, and either locally classifies or transmits image frames to a remote server for inference, typically via **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)** or lightweight **TensorFlow Lite Micro** models.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept**
> The ESP32-CAM is a **dual-core Xtensa LX6** processor running at **240 MHz**, equipped with **520 KB SRAM**, **4 MB PSRAM**, integrated **Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n**, and the **OV2640 2-megapixel** image sensor. The combination of PSRAM and Wi-Fi makes it the de-facto edge-device for KTU-level image recognition prototypes.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a security guard at the entrance of a building:

| Stage | Security Guard Analogy | ESP32-CAM Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Eyes** | Two biological cameras capturing the scene. | **OV2640 CMOS Sensor** converts photons into a 2D pixel array. |
| **Memory** | Short-term memory of recent faces. | **PSRAM (4 MB)** holds the raw RGB/JPEG frame buffer. |
| **Brain** | The cerebral cortex recognizing the person. | **ESP32 Xtensa cores** run CNN convolution layers. |
| **Communication** | Shouting "It's John!" to the central office. | **Wi-Fi TCP/UDP socket** streams JSON result to server. |
| **Decision** | Letting him in or raising alarm. | **SoftMax classification output** triggers an actuator (relay/buzzer). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The OV2640 sensor outputs a **Bayer-pattern** raw image which is internally converted to **YUV422, RGB565, or JPEG**. For KTU exams, always remember that JPEG mode is the **most memory-efficient** for Wi-Fi transmission, while RGB565 is the **most CNN-friendly** for on-device inference.

### Hardware & Software Inventory for the Experiment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Required Apparatus (KTU Viva Standard):**
> - ESP32-CAM board (AI-Thinker model preferred, **3 dBi PCB antenna**)
> - **FTDI USB-to-Serial programmer** (3.3 V logic, NOT 5 V)
> - **OV2640** camera module (already onboard)
> - Jumper wires, breadboard
> - Arduino IDE 2.x with **esp32 board package v2.0.14+**
> - **Arduino ESP32 filesystem uploader** plugin
> - Optional: Edge Impulse Studio or Teachable Machine (for model training)
> - A 5 V / 2 A external supply (Wi-Fi transmission peaks at **~240 mA**)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The image recognition workflow on the ESP32-CAM is logically partitioned into **five sequential stages**:

1. **Image Acquisition (Sensor Layer):**
   - The OV2640 communicates with the ESP32 over the **SCCB (Serial Camera Control Bus)** — a two-wire protocol nearly identical to **I²C** (SIO_C = GPIO 14, SIO_D = GPIO 15).
   - Pixel data is delivered via an 8-bit parallel **D[0:7]** interface using the **DCMI (Digital Camera Interface)** peripheral, clocked by **XCLK at 20 MHz** (configurable up to 24 MHz).
   - Internal **DSP (Digital Signal Processor)** of OV2640 performs demosaicing, gamma correction, color matrix conversion, and JPEG encoding in hardware.

2. **Frame Buffer Allocation (Memory Layer):**
   - Raw **RGB565** frame at **QQVGA (160 × 120)** occupies:
     $$\text{Memory} = 160 \times 120 \times 2 = 38{,}400 \text{ bytes (37.5 KB)}$$
   - A full **UXGA (1600 × 1200)** JPEG frame is typically **120–300 KB**.
   - PSRAM (4 MB) is **mandatory** to hold frame buffers; internal SRAM is insufficient.

3. **Pre-processing (Pre-CNN Layer):**
   - Resize to model input (commonly **96 × 96** or **128 × 128** grayscale).
   - Normalize pixel values: $x_{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{x}{255.0}$ scaling to $[0, 1]$.
   - Convert RGB → Grayscale using the **luminance formula**:
     $$Y = 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114 B$$

4. **Inference (CNN Layer):**
   - The normalized tensor is fed into a lightweight model such as **MobileNetV1 (α = 0.25)** or a custom 3-layer CNN trained via **Edge Impulse**.
   - Each convolution slide computes a weighted sum plus bias, then applies a **ReLU** non-linearity.
   - Final fully-connected layer uses **SoftMax** for probability distribution:
     $$\sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$$

5. **Communication & Actuation (Output Layer):**
   - The predicted class label and confidence are sent over Wi-Fi using **HTTP REST** or **MQTT** to a dashboard (e.g., Node-RED, Blynk, or a custom Flask server).
   - On-device GPIO (e.g., GPIO 12 flash LED, GPIO 2 onboard LED) provides immediate visual feedback.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Table for KTU ESE — Memorize Every Row!**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol / Expression | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Raw RGB565 image size | $W \times H \times 2$ | bytes | Pre-allocation of PSRAM buffer |
| 2 | JPEG compressed size | $\approx 0.1 \times W \times H$ (heuristic) | bytes | Wi-Fi payload estimation |
| 3 | Normalization (input scaling) | $x_{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{x}{255}$ | dimensionless $[0,1]$ | Standardizes CNN input distribution |
| 4 | Luminance (RGB → Gray) | $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ | 0–255 | Reduces channels for tiny MCUs |
| 5 | Convolution output size | $O = \dfrac{N - K + 2P}{S} + 1$ | integer | Determines feature map dimensions |
| 6 | SoftMax probability | $\sigma(z_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | $[0,1]$ | Converts logits to class probabilities |
| 7 | Wi-Fi transfer time | $T_{\text{tx}} = \dfrac{\text{Frame Size}}{\text{Throughput}}$ | seconds | Real-time FPS budgeting |
| 8 | Effective Wi-Fi bandwidth | $\eta = 0.5 \times \text{Physical rate}$ | Mbps | Accounts for protocol overhead |
| 9 | ReLU activation | $f(z) = \max(0,z)$ | linear in $z^+$ | Introduces non-linearity in CNN |
| 10 | Max-Pooling output | $O_{\text{pool}} = \dfrac{N - K_p}{S_p} + 1$ | integer | Spatial dimension reduction |
| 11 | Pixel clock frequency | $f_{\text{XCLK}}$ | **20 MHz** nominal | Sets maximum frame rate |
| 12 | Frames per second limit | $\text{FPS}_{\max} \approx \dfrac{f_{\text{XCLK}}}{W \times H \times 1.1}$ | fps | Determines streaming viability |
| 13 | CNN parameter count | $P = (K_h \cdot K_w \cdot C_{\text{in}} + 1) \cdot C_{\text{out}}$ | weights | Fits within 4 MB flash budget |
| 14 | MAC operations per inference | $\text{MAC} = \sum_{l} M_l \cdot N_l \cdot K_l^2 \cdot C_{l,\text{in}} \cdot C_{l,\text{out}}$ | operations | Latency estimation on LX6 |

> [!NOTE]
> **In formula 5 (Convolution output size):** $N$ is input dimension, $K$ is kernel size, $P$ is zero-padding, $S$ is stride. The pipe symbols in expressions like $\vert z \vert$ are intentionally avoided — write $\lvert z \rvert$ or `abs(z)` in code.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** Detect ripe tomatoes / diseased leaves using a $5$ ESP32-CAM node in a mesh.
- **Industrial Quality Control:** PCB defect classification at the end of an SMT line.
- **Healthcare Edge AI:** Fall-detection for elderly patients without cloud round-trips (latency < **200 ms**).
- **Smart Traffic Systems:** License-plate pre-OCR before sending to the central server.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Memory Footprint of an Image Frame

**Problem:** Compute the memory required to store one RGB565 frame at **QVGA (320 × 240)** resolution on the ESP32-CAM's PSRAM.

**Step 1 — Identify input dimensions.**
$$W = 320 \text{ pixels}, \quad H = 240 \text{ pixels}, \quad \text{depth} = 16 \text{ bits (RGB565)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the frame size formula.**
$$\text{Size} = W \times H \times \dfrac{\text{bits per pixel}}{8}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**
$$\text{Size} = 320 \times 240 \times \dfrac{16}{8}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the division first.**
$$\dfrac{16}{8} = 2 \text{ bytes per pixel}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply remaining terms.**
$$\text{Size} = 320 \times 240 \times 2 = 153{,}600 \text{ bytes} = 150 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check against PSRAM capacity.**
$$\dfrac{150 \text{ KB}}{4096 \text{ KB}} \approx 3.66\% \text{ of PSRAM}$$

**Conclusion:** A single QVGA RGB565 frame uses less than 4 % of PSRAM, leaving ample headroom for double-buffering and CNN intermediate tensors. The full derivation is mandatory in KTU 14-mark answers.

### 3.2 Derivation B — Convolution Output Dimension for First Layer

**Problem:** A CNN's first layer accepts a **96 × 96** grayscale image with a **3 × 3** kernel, stride $S = 1$, no padding ($P = 0$). Compute the output feature-map size.

**Step 1 — Recall the convolution output formula.**
$$O = \frac{N - K + 2P}{S} + 1$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**
$$O = \frac{96 - 3 + 2(0)}{1} + 1$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator.**
$$96 - 3 = 93$$

**Step 4 — Divide and add 1.**
$$O = 93 + 1 = 94$$

**Step 5 — Apply to both dimensions (square input).**
$$O_W = 94, \quad O_H = 94$$

**Step 6 — Number of MACs in this layer (16 output channels).**
$$\text{MAC} = 94 \times 94 \times 3 \times 3 \times 1 \times 16 = 1{,}272{,}384 \text{ operations}$$

**Interpretation:** A single convolutional layer at 96 × 96 resolution requires over **1.2 million multiply-accumulate operations**, which justifies why ESP32 inference for a 3-layer CNN consumes **~250 ms** per frame at 240 MHz.

### 3.3 Derivation C — Wi-Fi Transmission Time for a JPEG Frame

**Problem:** Estimate the time to transmit a **20 KB** JPEG frame over **802.11n** with physical rate of **72.2 Mbps** and 50 % effective throughput.

**Step 1 — Effective bandwidth.**
$$\eta = 0.5 \times 72.2 = 36.1 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to bytes per second.**
$$\eta_{\text{B}} = \dfrac{36.1 \times 10^6}{8} = 4{,}512{,}500 \text{ bytes/s} = 4.51 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the transfer time equation.**
$$T_{\text{tx}} = \dfrac{\text{Frame Size}}{\eta_{\text{B}}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values.**
$$T_{\text{tx}} = \dfrac{20{,}000}{4{,}512{,}500}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the quotient.**
$$T_{\text{tx}} \approx 4.43 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 4.43 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 6 — Conclude the maximum frame rate bound.**
$$\text{FPS}_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{tx}} + T_{\text{inference}}} = \dfrac{1}{0.00443 + 0.250} \approx 3.93 \text{ FPS}$$

**Conclusion:** The network is **not the bottleneck**; the on-device CNN inference dominates the latency budget. This result is a frequent 7-mark sub-question in KTU exams.

### 3.4 Derivation D — SoftMax Numerical Stability

**Problem:** Compute $\sigma(z)$ for logits $z = [2.0, 1.0, 0.1]$ and demonstrate the need for the **max-subtraction trick**.

**Step 1 — Naïve exponentiation.**
$$e^{2.0} = 7.389, \quad e^{1.0} = 2.718, \quad e^{0.1} = 1.105$$

**Step 2 — Sum of exponentials.**
$$Z = 7.389 + 2.718 + 1.105 = 11.212$$

**Step 3 — SoftMax probabilities.**
$$\sigma_1 = \dfrac{7.389}{11.212} = 0.659$$

$$\sigma_2 = \dfrac{2.718}{11.212} = 0.242$$

$$\sigma_3 = \dfrac{1.105}{11.212} = 0.099$$

**Step 4 — Verify the sum equals 1.**
$$0.659 + 0.242 + 0.099 = 1.000 \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Show the stability trick for $z = [1000, 1001, 1002]$.**

Without correction, $e^{1002}$ overflows `float32`. Subtracting the max $z_{\max} = 1002$ yields logits $[-2, -1, 0]$, giving exponentials $[0.135, 0.368, 1.000]$ — safe and **mathematically identical**.

**Conclusion:** All production CNN libraries (TFLM, ONNX Runtime) implement this offset. KTU viva examiners frequently ask: *"Why is SoftMax numerically unstable and how is it fixed?"*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System Architecture — ESP32-CAM Image Recognition Node

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[OV2640 CMOS Image Sensor] -- SCCB I2C Lines GPIO 14 GPIO 15 --> node2[ESP32 Xtensa LX6 Dual Core 240 MHz]
    node2 -- Parallel DCMI Data Bus D0 to D7 --> node3[PSRAM Frame Buffer 4 MB]
    node3 -- Raw RGB565 38 KB --> node4[Preprocessing Resize 96x96 Normalize 0 to 1]
    node4 -- Tensor Input --> node5[TFLite Micro CNN MobileNetV1 alpha 0.25]
    node5 -- Class ID + Confidence --> node6[Decision Logic SoftMax Argmax]
    node6 -- GPIO 2 LED + GPIO 12 Flash --> node7[Local Actuator Feedback]
    node6 -- HTTP POST JSON Payload --> node8[WiFi 802.11 b/g/n Radio]
    node8 -- TCP IP Stack --> node9[Remote Flask or NodeRED Server]
    node9 --> node10[Cloud Dashboard Grafana or Blynk App]
    node2 -. JTAG / UART TX RX .-> node11[FTDI USB Serial Programmer]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Data flows from left (sensor) to right (cloud). The dotted arrow represents the **offline programming/debug path** used only during development.

### 4.2 Image Recognition Pipeline — Internal Software Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Board Boot]) --> initA[Init Serial 115200 baud]
    initA --> initB[Init PSRAM heap_caps_malloc 4 MB]
    initB --> initC[Init WiFi Station Mode SSID PASS]
    initC --> initD[Init Camera esp_camera_init config]
    initD --> checkA{Camera Initialized?}
    checkA -- No --> errA[Print Error Halt Loop]
    checkA -- Yes --> loopA((Start Capture Loop))
    loopA --> capA[esp_camera_fb_get allocate frame buffer]
    capA --> convA[Convert fb format to RGB565]
    convA --> dlImgA[Save JPG via fb2jpg or SD card]
    convA --> preA[Resize to 96x96 grayscale]
    preA --> normA[Normalize x divide by 255]
    normA --> tflA[tflm invoke InterpeterInvoke]
    tflA --> postA[Read output tensor float scores]
    postA --> smA[SoftMax + Argmax compute label]
    smA --> sendA[WiFi client print POST JSON to server]
    sendA --> fbR[esp_camera_fb_return release buffer]
    fbR --> loopA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Implementation Note:** `esp_camera_fb_return()` must be called *every iteration* to prevent **PSRAM memory leaks** — a common KTU viva trap.

### 4.3 ESP32-CAM Pinout (Reference Block for Lab Record)

| GPIO | Function in Camera Mode | Free in Use? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| GPIO 0 | (must be HIGH on boot for normal mode) | No |
| GPIO 1 | TX0 (debug UART) | No |
| GPIO 2 | Onboard LED / Strapping | Yes (LED) |
| GPIO 3 | RX0 (debug UART) | No |
| GPIO 4 | Camera Flash LED | Yes |
| GPIO 12 | Camera PWDN | No (used) |
| GPIO 13 | Camera RESET | No (used) |
| GPIO 14 | SCCB SIO_C | No (used) |
| GPIO 15 | SCCB SIO_D | No (used) |
| GPIO 16 | PSRAM CS | No (used) |
| GPIO 33 | Onboard Red LED (some variants) | Yes |
| 3V3, 5V, GND | Power rails | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Free GPIOs for external actuators:** Only **GPIO 2, 4, 12, 13, 15, 16** are partially usable. The most popular free pin is **GPIO 2** for status indication.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] *List any three key hardware specifications of the ESP32-CAM module that make it suitable for edge-based image recognition.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Integrated 2 MP OV2640 CMOS camera** with on-chip JPEG encoder (reduces MCU workload).
2. **4 MB external PSRAM** — essential to hold a single QVGA frame buffer (~150 KB).
3. **Dual-core 240 MHz Xtensa LX6** with **Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n** — provides sufficient compute (~600 DMIPS) and wireless uplink in a single SoC, eliminating the need for a separate MCU + Wi-Fi module.

*(1 mark each, exact KTU key-pattern phrasing.)*

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] *What is the role of the SoftMax function in an image classification model? Why is the max-subtraction trick used?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Role (1.5 marks):** SoftMax converts a vector of raw class scores (logits) into a probability distribution that sums to **1.0**, enabling interpretation as **confidence** for each class.
- **Trick (1.5 marks):** Subtracting the maximum logit $\max(z)$ from all logits before exponentiation prevents **floating-point overflow** of $e^z$ for large positive values, while the resulting probability distribution is **mathematically invariant**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of the ESP32-CAM** and the function of the OV2640 sensor. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design the **complete algorithm** for an image recognition system on the ESP32-CAM that classifies whether a captured frame contains an "Authorized Person" or "Intruder" using a 3-layer CNN. Include pre-processing, inference, and Wi-Fi notification stages. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Block Diagram (3 marks).**
Draw the architecture as in *Section 4.1*. The OV2640 sensor interfaces with ESP32 via:
- **SCCB (I²C-like)** for register configuration.
- **Parallel 8-bit DCMI** for pixel data streaming.
- **XCLK (20 MHz)** for pixel timing.

**Step 2 — OV2640 Functional Description (2 marks).**
The OV2640 houses:
- A **1632 × 1232** active pixel array.
- An **on-chip ISP** (Image Signal Processor) handling AWB, AEC, AGC, color interpolation, and JPEG compression.
- Configurable resolution from **QQVGA (160 × 120)** to **UXGA (1600 × 1200)**.

**Step 3 — ESP32 Functional Blocks (2 marks).**
- **CPU subsystem:** 2 × Xtensa LX6 cores, 448 KB ROM, 520 KB SRAM.
- **Memory:** 4 MB SPI flash for program, 4 MB PSRAM for frame buffers.
- **Peripherals:** Wi-Fi MAC/PHY, DCMI, I²C, SPI, UART, GPIO.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Algorithm Outline (3 marks).**
Present the flowchart from *Section 4.2* with explicit pre-processing:

1. Initialize camera, Wi-Fi, and TFLM interpreter.
2. Capture frame → convert to grayscale → resize 96 × 96.
3. Normalize pixel values to $[0, 1]$.
4. Invoke interpreter; read output tensor.
5. Apply SoftMax + Argmax to obtain class label.
6. If label == "Intruder" with confidence > 0.85, send HTTP POST to server with frame attached.
7. Release frame buffer; repeat.

**Step 2 — Hyper-parameter Table (2 marks).**

| Parameter | Value | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Input resolution | 96 × 96 × 1 | Fits TFLM arena < 100 KB |
| Learning rate | 0.001 | Adam optimizer default |
| Epochs | 30 | Empirically sufficient for binary tasks |
| Batch size | 32 | PSRAM-friendly |

**Step 3 — Output Format Example (2 marks).**
```text
HTTP POST /intruder HTTP/1.1
Host: 192.168.1.10
Content-Type: application/json
{"label":"Intruder","confidence":0.92,"timestamp":1700000000}
```

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the **memory size** of a QVGA (320 × 240) RGB565 image and verify whether it fits in the ESP32-CAM's PSRAM. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A CNN layer has an input feature map of size **64 × 64 × 16**, a kernel of **3 × 3**, stride = 1, padding = 1, and produces **32 output channels**. Compute the **output feature-map size** and the **total number of trainable parameters** in this layer. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Frame size formula (1 mark).**
$$\text{Size} = W \times H \times \text{bytes per pixel} = 320 \times 240 \times 2$$

**Step 2 — Numerical evaluation (2 marks).**
$$320 \times 240 = 76{,}800$$
$$76{,}800 \times 2 = 153{,}600 \text{ bytes} = 150 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 3 — PSRAM comparison (2 marks).**
PSRAM = 4 MB = 4096 KB.
$$\dfrac{150}{4096} \times 100\% \approx 3.66\%$$

**Step 4 — Verdict (1 mark).**
Yes, the image fits comfortably. **Triple-buffering** (3 × 150 KB = 450 KB) still occupies only ~11 % of PSRAM.

**Step 5 — Practical note (1 mark).**
JPEG mode reduces this to **~10–20 KB** for Wi-Fi transmission, but for CNN inference **RGB565 is mandatory** because TFLM kernels expect uint8 tensors.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Output size using the convolution formula (3 marks).**
$$O = \frac{N - K + 2P}{S} + 1 = \frac{64 - 3 + 2(1)}{1} + 1 = \frac{64 - 3 + 2}{1} + 1 = 64$$

Therefore the output is **64 × 64 × 32**.

**Step 2 — Parameter count for one kernel (2 marks).**
Each kernel has $3 \times 3 \times 16 = 144$ weights + 1 bias = **145 parameters**.

**Step 3 — Total for 32 output channels (2 marks).**
$$P_{\text{total}} = 145 \times 32 = 4{,}640 \text{ parameters}$$

This is well within the 4 MB flash constraint of the ESP32-CAM.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Write!**
> 1. **Confusing RGB565 with RGB888:** RGB565 = 2 bytes/pixel; RGB888 = 3 bytes/pixel. Wrong choice ⇒ lose **2 marks** instantly in 7-mark derivations.
> 2. **Forgetting `+1` in the convolution output formula:** A common KTU error. Marks lost in 14-mark questions: **3–4 marks**.
> 3. **Not calling `esp_camera_fb_return()`** in the algorithm ⇒ examiner marks the loop as "memory-leak prone" and deducts **1 mark** under best practices.
> 4. **Wrong PSRAM size assumption:** Some students write 520 KB (this is *internal* SRAM, not PSRAM). PSRAM is **4 MB**; SRAM is **520 KB**. Examiner deducts **2 marks** if confused.
> 5. **Using 5 V FTDI programmer:** ESP32-CAM is **3.3 V logic**. Writing 5 V in circuit diagram is an instant **-2 mark** deduction.
> 6. **Missing SoftMax numerical stability justification:** KTU key demands the *max-subtraction trick* explanation; mere formula is **insufficient for full marks**.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read the night before KTU ESE)**
> - **ESP32-CAM = ESP32 + OV2640 + PSRAM + Wi-Fi + Flash LED** on a 27 × 40 mm PCB.
> - Camera bus = **SCCB (config) + DCMI parallel (data) + XCLK (timing)**.
> - **GPIO 0 must be HIGH at boot**; pulling it LOW enters *flash mode*.
> - **PSRAM (4 MB) vs SRAM (520 KB) vs Flash (4 MB)** — never interchange.
> - Image size formula: $W \times H \times \text{BPP}$ ; **BPP = 2** for RGB565, **BPP = 1** for Grayscale, **BPP ≈ 0.1–0.3** for JPEG.
> - Convolution output: $O = \dfrac{N - K + 2P}{S} + 1$ — **always add the +1!**
> - SoftMax: $\sigma(z_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ — use **max-subtraction** for stability.
> - ReLU: $f(z) = \max(0, z)$ — introduces non-linearity, avoids vanishing gradient.
> - Max-Pooling reduces spatial dimension by factor of kernel size (typically 2 × 2, stride 2).
> - **Typical inference latency on ESP32-CAM** for MobileNetV1-α=0.25: **~200–300 ms**.
> - **Wi-Fi peak current** can reach **240 mA**; use a **5 V / 2 A** supply, not the FTDI rail.
> - **Arduino IDE workflow:** select board *"AI Thinker ESP32-CAM"* → set partition scheme *"Huge APP (3 MB No OTA)"* → upload at **115200 baud**.
> - **Viva mantra:** "We use JPEG for transmission, RGB565/Grayscale for inference, and TFLM with PSRAM-backed tensor arena."
> - **JSON payload format** is the standard KTU answer for the Wi-Fi notification stage.
> - **Memory leak prevention:** always pair `esp_camera_fb_get()` with `esp_camera_fb_return()`.
> - **Numerical stability** is a *guaranteed KTU sub-question* every semester — memorize the SoftMax trick.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Obstacle Avoidance of a Mobile Robot While Moving to a Point
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAL607 - ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Obstacle Avoidance of a Mobile Robot While Moving to a Point

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Obstacle Avoidance** in mobile robotics is defined as the real-time computational and control strategy that enables an autonomous ground robot to navigate from a start configuration to a desired goal configuration while continuously detecting, characterizing, and circumventing static or dynamic obstacles within its operational workspace, without any prior complete map information. The robot relies on **exteroceptive sensors** (e.g., ultrasonic, infrared, LiDAR) and a reactive or hybrid control law to compute collision-free velocity commands.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme PCCAL607 Robotics Lab (Module 2)**, the experiment is implemented on a differential-drive (or similar) mobile platform running an **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** or **Bug-style algorithm**, where the controller outputs linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ based on a fusion of *goal-seeking* and *obstacle-repelling* behaviors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must implement and demonstrate obstacle avoidance where the robot starts at an arbitrary point, navigates to a user-specified goal, and dynamically alters its trajectory when an obstacle enters its safety bubble (typically a circle of radius $\rho_0$ between **10 cm and 30 cm**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hiker in the Fog"

Imagine you are **hiking toward a mountain peak in thick fog**. You cannot see the destination directly, but you know its bearing. As you walk, your feet feel the slope — you are drawn "downhill" toward the goal. Suddenly, your hand touches a **rocky cliff** in front of you. Instantly, you push away from it (repulsion) and continue stepping sideways until the slope toward the peak is clear again. Your brain is computing, every second, an *attractive force* (the goal) and a *repulsive force* (the obstacle), and you walk along the resultant direction.

- The **mountain peak** = goal point $(x_g, y_g)$
- The **rocky cliff** = obstacle at distance $\rho$
- The **resultant step direction** = velocity command $(v, \omega)$
- The **fog** = partial / uncertain sensor information

This is exactly how an **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** controller works.

## 1.3 Fundamental Components of the System

| Component | Function | Typical Realization |
|-----------|----------|---------------------|
| **Localization** | Estimate robot pose $(x, y, \theta)$ | Wheel odometry, IMU |
| **Perception** | Detect obstacles and their distance | HC-SR04 Ultrasonic, LiDAR, IR array |
| **Planner (Reactive Layer)** | Compute desired motion | APF, VFH, Bug algorithm |
| **Controller** | Track desired motion | PID on $v$ and $\omega$ |
| **Actuators** | Drive the wheels | DC motors with encoder |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** True obstacle avoidance is a *closed-loop, real-time* problem. The control loop must execute at a minimum of **10 Hz** for slow indoor robots and up to **50–100 Hz** for fast outdoor platforms to guarantee stability.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Artificial Potential Field (APF) Method — The Core Theory

The APF method, introduced by **Khatib (1986)**, models the robot as a point moving in a field of forces:

- The **goal** generates an *attractive* potential $U_{att}$ that pulls the robot toward it.
- **Obstacles** generate a *repulsive* potential $U_{rep}$ that pushes the robot away.
- The **total potential** is the sum, and the **force** on the robot is the **negative gradient**.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic

1. Define the robot position vector in 2-D world frame:
$$q = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}$$
2. Define the goal position:
$$q_{goal} = \begin{bmatrix} x_g \\ y_g \end{bmatrix}$$
3. Define an obstacle's centroid:
$$q_{obs} = \begin{bmatrix} x_o \\ y_o \end{bmatrix}$$
4. Compute the Euclidean distance to the goal and to each obstacle.
5. Compute attractive and repulsive potentials.
6. Sum the potentials → take the negative gradient → obtain the control force / velocity.
7. Apply kinematic constraints (max speed, max turn rate) and send to motors.

### 2.1.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why use a potential?** It is mathematically smooth, differentiable, and computationally cheap — perfect for real-time microcontrollers (Arduino, Raspberry Pi, STM32).
- **Why use a gradient?** The gradient always points in the direction of *steepest increase*. Taking its negative gives the direction of *steepest decrease* — i.e., the direction the robot should "roll downhill" to reach the goal.
- **Why a circular safety zone $\rho_0$?** It limits the influence of obstacles to a local region, preventing distant obstacles from perturbing the global path.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Range / Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|---------|
| 1 | Euclidean distance to goal | $\rho_{g} = \sqrt{(x - x_g)^2 + (y - y_g)^2}$ | meters (m) | Computed every cycle |
| 2 | Euclidean distance to obstacle $i$ | $\rho_i = \sqrt{(x - x_{o,i})^2 + (y - y_{o,i})^2}$ | meters (m) | From sensor reading or map |
| 3 | Attractive potential | $U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\, k_{att}\, \rho_g^{\,2}$ | joules (J) | Conic for far goal, parabolic near |
| 4 | Attractive force | $F_{att} = -\, k_{att}\, (q - q_{goal})$ | newtons (N) | Points toward goal |
| 5 | Repulsive potential (active when $\rho_i \le \rho_0$) | $U_{rep,i}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\, k_{rep}\!\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho_i} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{\!2}$ | joules (J) | Zero when $\rho_i > \rho_0$ |
| 6 | Repulsive force magnitude | $\vert F_{rep,i} \vert = k_{rep}\!\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho_i} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\!\tfrac{1}{\rho_i^{\,2}}$ | newtons (N) | Applied only inside $\rho_0$ |
| 7 | Total force | $F_{total} = F_{att} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{rep,i}$ | newtons (N) | Vector sum |
| 8 | Desired heading angle | $\theta_{d} = \operatorname{atan2}(F_{total,y},\ F_{total,x})$ | radians (rad) | Converted to wheel commands |
| 9 | Heading error | $e_\theta = \theta_d - \theta$ | radians (rad) | Wrapped to $(-\pi,\pi)$ |
| 10 | Differential-drive kinematics | $v = \tfrac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$, $\ \omega = \tfrac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | m/s, rad/s | $r$ = wheel radius, $L$ = wheelbase |
| 11 | PID angular control | $\omega = K_p e_\theta + K_d \dot{e}_\theta$ | rad/s | $K_p, K_d$ tuned experimentally |
| 12 | Goal-reached condition | $\rho_g \le \rho_{tol}$ (e.g., **0.05 m**) | meters | Stops robot |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall — Local Minima:** Pure APF can get stuck in a *local minimum* where $F_{att} = -F_{rep}$. KTU examiners love to ask how to escape it. Common fixes: (a) random walk, (b) wall-following (Bug-2 algorithm), (c) virtual obstacle, (d) rotational potential field.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Obstacle Avoidance |
|--------|-----------------------------------|
| **Warehouse Automation** | Amazon Kiva robots navigate shelves; LiDAR + APF |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Tesla, Waymo use VFH* and DWA for collision avoidance |
| **Domestic Robots** | Roomba uses infrared + bump sensors with reactive avoidance |
| **Agricultural Robotics** | Row-following and weed avoidance in precision farming |
| **Search & Rescue** |废墟探索 in unknown terrain using APF + SLAM |
| **Healthcare** | Hospital delivery robots avoiding patients and staff |

> [!NOTE]
> In industrial-grade systems, APF is often combined with a **global planner (A\*, RRT)** in a *hybrid architecture*. The global planner handles long-range planning; the local APF handles real-time reactive avoidance — a concept called **"Plan-then-React"** or the **Deliberative-Reactive Paradigm**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the APF Control Law

We derive, *from first principles*, the control force and the velocity command sent to a differential-drive mobile robot.

### Step 1 — Position Representation

Let the robot's pose in the world frame be:

$$q = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}, \quad q_{goal} = \begin{bmatrix} x_g \\ y_g \end{bmatrix}, \quad q_{obs} = \begin{bmatrix} x_o \\ y_o \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Define the Attractive Potential

We choose a **quadratic (parabolic)** potential well centered at the goal. The rationale: at small distances, the force should be linear in displacement (Hooke's law), which yields a critically damped, smooth approach.

$$U_{att}(q) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, k_{att}\, \rho_g^{\,2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, k_{att}\, \big[(x-x_g)^2 + (y-y_g)^2\big]$$

where $k_{att} > 0$ is the **attractive gain** (units: N/m).

### Step 3 — Compute the Attractive Force

The force is the negative gradient of the potential energy:

$$F_{att} \;=\; -\nabla U_{att} \;=\; -\,\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial q}$$

Differentiating component-wise:

$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x} = k_{att}\,(x - x_g), \qquad \frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial y} = k_{att}\,(y - y_g)$$

Therefore the attractive force vector is:

$$F_{att} \;=\; -\, k_{att}\, \begin{bmatrix} x - x_g \\ y - y_g \end{bmatrix} \;=\; k_{att}\,(q_{goal} - q)$$

This force **points from the robot toward the goal**, with magnitude proportional to the distance. The units are in newtons (N) if positions are in meters.

### Step 4 — Define the Repulsive Potential

The repulsive potential must:
- Be **large** when $\rho \to 0$ (so the robot never collides).
- Be **zero** when $\rho \ge \rho_0$ (so distant obstacles do not affect the path).
- Be **continuously differentiable** at $\rho = \rho_0$ (for smooth control).

The classical form, proposed by **Khatib**, satisfies all three:

$$U_{rep}(q) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
\tfrac{1}{2}\, k_{rep}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{\!2}, & \rho \le \rho_0 \\[2mm]
0, & \rho > \rho_0
\end{cases}$$

where $\rho = \sqrt{(x-x_o)^2 + (y-y_o)^2}$ is the distance to the obstacle, $\rho_0$ is the **influence radius** (typically **0.15–0.30 m** in KTU lab setups), and $k_{rep} > 0$ is the repulsive gain (units: N·m²).

### Step 5 — Compute the Repulsive Force

Differentiating $U_{rep}$ with respect to $q$ and applying the chain rule:

$$F_{rep} \;=\; -\nabla U_{rep} \;=\; -\,\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial q}$$

Let us first differentiate with respect to $\rho$:

$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial \rho} \;=\; k_{rep}\!\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\!\left(-\frac{1}{\rho^{2}}\right) \;=\; -\,\frac{k_{rep}}{\rho^{2}}\!\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)$$

Now apply the chain rule, noting that $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial q} = \frac{q - q_{obs}}{\rho}$:

$$F_{rep} \;=\; -\,\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial \rho}\cdot \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial q} \;=\; \frac{k_{rep}}{\rho^{2}}\!\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\cdot \frac{q - q_{obs}}{\rho}$$

Simplifying:

$$F_{rep} \;=\; k_{rep}\!\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\!\frac{1}{\rho^{3}}\,(q - q_{obs})$$

> This vector points **from the obstacle toward the robot** (since $q - q_{obs}$ points away from the obstacle), pushing the robot outward — exactly the desired repulsion.

### Step 6 — Total Force and Desired Heading

$$F_{total} \;=\; F_{att} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{rep,i}$$

The **desired heading angle** in the world frame:

$$\theta_{d} \;=\; \operatorname{atan2}\!\big(F_{total,\,y},\ F_{total,\,x}\big)$$

### Step 7 — Convert to Wheel Commands (Differential-Drive Kinematics)

The heading error and a proportional controller give the angular velocity:

$$e_\theta \;=\; \theta_{d} - \theta, \quad \omega \;=\; K_p\, e_\theta + K_d\, \dot{e}_\theta$$

The linear velocity is typically **constant** (e.g., $v = 0.15$ m/s) or scaled with $\cos(e_\theta)$ to slow down on sharp turns:

$$v \;=\; v_{max}\cdot \cos(e_\theta)$$

The **inverse kinematics** of a differential-drive robot give the wheel speeds:

$$v_{L} \;=\; \frac{2v - \omega L}{2r}, \qquad v_{R} \;=\; \frac{2v + \omega L}{2r}$$

where $r$ is the wheel radius and $L$ is the wheelbase (axle length).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:**
- Robot at $q = (1.0,\, 1.0)$ m
- Goal at $q_{goal} = (4.0,\, 3.0)$ m
- Obstacle at $q_{obs} = (2.5,\, 2.0)$ m, with influence radius $\rho_0 = 1.0$ m
- $k_{att} = 1.0$ N/m, $k_{rep} = 0.5$ N·m²

**Step A — Distances:**

$$\rho_g = \sqrt{(1-4)^2 + (1-3)^2} = \sqrt{9+4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.606\ \text{m}$$

$$\rho = \sqrt{(1-2.5)^2 + (1-2)^2} = \sqrt{2.25+1} = \sqrt{3.25} \approx 1.803\ \text{m}$$

Since $\rho = 1.803 > \rho_0 = 1.0$, **the obstacle is outside the influence zone** and contributes $F_{rep} = 0$.

**Step B — Attractive Force:**

$$F_{att} = k_{att}\,(q_{goal} - q) = 1.0\cdot(3.0,\, 2.0) = (3.0,\, 2.0)\ \text{N}$$

$$F_{total} = (3.0,\, 2.0)\ \text{N}$$

**Step C — Desired Heading:**

$$\theta_d = \operatorname{atan2}(2.0,\, 3.0) \approx 0.588\ \text{rad} \approx 33.69^{\circ}$$

**Interpretation:** The robot should drive northeast at about 33.7° from the x-axis. The obstacle is too far to influence the path at this moment. As the robot approaches within 1.0 m, repulsion will engage.

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix — Common Obstacle Avoidance Algorithms

| Algorithm | Type | Pros | Cons | KTU Exam Tip |
|-----------|------|------|------|--------------|
| **Bug 1 / Bug 2** | Reactive | Simple, complete, no map | Long, jagged paths | Asked in viva for "guaranteed-completeness" |
| **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** | Reactive | Smooth paths, low compute | Local minima traps | Most common in lab; tune $k_{att}, k_{rep}$ |
| **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** | Reactive | Handles dense obstacles | Needs dense range data | Mentioned in differential-drive contexts |
| **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)** | Local planner | Considers robot dynamics | Slower than APF | Used in ROS navigation stack |
| **A\*/D\*** with replanning | Global | Optimal, complete | High compute, needs map | Out of scope for real-time lab |
| **RRT / RRT\*** | Sampling-based | Handles high DOF | Stochastic, slow | More in Manipulator (Module 4/5) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Goal Point xg yg] --> C[Attractive Force Computation]
    B[Sensors Ultrasonic IR LiDAR] --> D[Obstacle Position Estimation]
    D --> E[Distance Calculation rho]
    E --> F{Is rho less than rho0}
    F -- Yes --> G[Repulsive Force Computation]
    F -- No --> G0[Repulsive Force equals 0]
    G --> H[Force Summation F total]
    G0 --> H
    C --> H
    H --> I[Desired Heading theta d]
    I --> J[Heading Error e theta]
    K[Wheel Odometry IMU] --> L[Current Pose x y theta]
    L --> J
    J --> M[PID Controller omega]
    M --> N[Differential Drive Inverse Kinematics]
    N --> O[Motor Driver L298N]
    O --> P[Left and Right Wheels]
    P --> L
    L --> Q{Goal Reached rho g less than rho tol}
    Q -- No --> H
    Q -- Yes --> R[STOP Robot]
```

## 4.2 Reactive Control Loop (Sequence-Style Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S([Start]) --> Init[Initialize Pose and Goal]
    Init --> ReadSens[Read Ultrasonic Distances]
    ReadSens --> CalcAtt[Compute F attractive]
    CalcAtt --> CalcRep[Compute F repulsive for each obstacle]
    CalcRep --> Sum[Sum all forces]
    Sum --> Head[Compute desired heading]
    Head --> Ctrl[PID gives omega]
    Ctrl --> Drive[Send wheel PWM]
    Drive --> Odom[Update odometry]
    Odom --> Check{Goal reached}
    Check -- No --> ReadSens
    Check -- Yes --> Stop([Stop Motors])
```

## 4.3 Sensor Configuration on a Differential-Drive Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FrontSensors[Front Facing Array]
        S1[Ultrasonic Left 30 deg]
        S2[Ultrasonic Center 0 deg]
        S3[Ultrasonic Right minus 30 deg]
    end
    subgraph SideSensors[Side Sensors Optional]
        S4[IR Left 90 deg]
        S5[IR Right minus 90 deg]
    end
    S1 --> MCU[Arduino / STM32 / Raspberry Pi]
    S2 --> MCU
    S3 --> MCU
    S4 --> MCU
    S5 --> MCU
    MCU --> MotorDriver[L298N Motor Driver]
    MotorDriver --> ML[Left Motor]
    MotorDriver --> MR[Right Motor]
    ML --> Enc[Encoders for Odometry]
    MR --> Enc
    Enc --> MCU
```

## 4.4 State Machine for Obstacle-Avoidance Behavior

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SeekGoal
    SeekGoal --> AvoidObstacle : obstacle within rho0
    AvoidObstacle --> SeekGoal : obstacle cleared
    SeekGoal --> GoalReached : distance to goal less than tolerance
    AvoidObstacle --> WallFollow : obstacle hugs robot path
    WallFollow --> SeekGoal : clear path to goal regained
    AvoidObstacle --> GoalReached : bypassed obstacle and at goal
    GoalReached --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a function in the control firmware. In the KTU lab, students are expected to draw such flowcharts in their *Lab Record* and verbally explain the data flow during the **viva voce**.
